Original_Search_Title: Stumbling Towards Tragedy in Malawi
Headline: Malawi: Stumbling Towards Tragedy in Malawi
Body_Text:
Johannesburg — HUMANITARIAN agencies are struggling to focus world attention on the acute and worsening food crisis in southern Africa. As food stocks dwindle and the region's hungry season begins in earnest, 12-million people in six countries will need humanitarian aid by early next year, according to the United Nations (UN).
However, the UN's World Food Programme (WFP) and other relief agencies have struggled to raise funds for southern Africa in a year that saw international donors' attention riveted by the Asian tsunami and earthquake, and food crises elsewhere in Africa, including Niger.
Amid signs that southern Africa's food problems are becoming chronic, relief workers are pushing for long-term solutions that address these ills head-on.
In Malawi, the worst-hit country, nearly 5-million of the country's 12-million people will need food aid until next April, according to the WFP. The other countries affected are Mozambique, Zambia, Zimbabwe, Lesotho and Swaziland.
Prodded by donors, Malawi's government is drafting an economic growth strategy aimed at mapping a future for a landlocked country where most people subsist on tiny farms dependent on rainfall. It is also urging farmers and consumers to diversify away from the staple crop of maize and into drought-resistant crops such as sorghum and cassava.
While Malawi's population has nearly doubled since the 1970s, farm productivity has barely changed. Prices for tobacco, the country's main cash crop, have fallen, while fertiliser prices have risen, fuelled by higher oil prices. "Even if it rains well, people can't produce enough food to feed themselves," says Sylvester Kalonge, food security co-ordinator with Care, a nongovernmental organisation in Malawi. "And when there's drought, it just pushes them over the line."
Unlike Niger and other arid west African countries, Malawi could easily feed itself with the help of irrigation and improved farming techniques. But government and foreign donors have for decades worked at cross purposes. On foreign donors' advice, in the 1990s Malawi partially dismantled Admarc, an agricultural marketing board that had formerly bought and sold crops at guaranteed prices. The move exposed farmers to new risks, yet the government continues to subsidise some maize and fertiliser.
Observers say the resulting market distortions compounded the effects of the last growing season's failed rains, which led to this year's food crisis. As is the case throughout southern Africa, HIV/AIDS -- which affects 15% of adults -- has fractured families and depressed farm output.
Political instability has also contributed to Malawi's problems. The country has had four agriculture ministers in two years. Apart from budgetary and other money bills, parliament has passed no laws since President Bingu wa Mutharika took office last year. The president faces the threat of impeachment from angry MPs in the United Democratic Front, the party with which he broke after standing as its candidate. Declaring a policy of "zero tolerance for corruption", he has presided over the arrest of supposedly corrupt officials.
Donors, led by the UK, took the unprecedented step in October of writing to MPs to warn them that impeachment would imperil future aid to Malawi. The move angered some in the opposition, who claim that Mutharika and his associates are themselves corrupt.
Despite the political turmoil, donors say Malawi is making progress on lowering inflation and the fiscal deficit, and addressing structural issues that impede the private sector. Because of transport costs and other market barriers, donors say Malawi cannot compete against its coastal neighbours in producing manufactured goods. In a discussion paper drafted in support of the government's economic growth strategy, Britain's Department for International Development (DFID) recently suggested agriculture as a future "engine of growth".
DFID formerly funded a programme offering Malawian farmers a "starter pack" of improved seed and fertiliser. After negligible results on that programme, DFID is now advising the government to focus on increasing productivity in big farms that might provide future employment for inefficient smallholders. Financial Times

Original_Search_Title: Use DDT to Fight Malaria, Say Experts
Headline: Uganda: Use DDT to Fight Malaria, Say Experts
Body_Text:
Nairobi — BIO-CHEMISTRY and pathology experts at Makerere University are advising Uganda's Ministry of Health to use dichlorodiphenyltrichloethene (DDT) in its campaign to eradicate malaria.
The recommendation follows a study carried out in Kihihi subcounty of Kanungu district in western Uganda, where the World Health Organisation (WHO) malaria control team sprayed two grammes of DDT per square metre in households and kraals 45 years ago.
Dr Gabriel Bimenya, the head of the Pathology Department at the university, said that traces of DDT were found in urine, soil and foodstuffs such as beans, fish, bananas, cassava, and Irish potatoes.
Dr Bimenya said that after conducting the study, researchers at Makerere were recommending application of DDT through indoor residual spraying to avoid a spill-over to the environment as it may affect exporters of agricultural produce.
He said that DDT is used by many European and American airlines to prevent malaria infections among travellers, which underlined the fact that the chemical was not a health hazard to humans.
"In June last year, a number of European ministers were found to have DDT in their blood and the concentration was three times higher than that found in Ugandans in the just concluded research," Dr Bimenya said, adding that it has not been reported anywhere that Europeans or Americans are medically affected or disadvantaged by the presence of DDT in their bodies.
Edward Ssenyange, a trade promoter with the Uganda Export Promotions Board, said, "The use of DDT jeopardises the country's market for agricultural produce particularly in Europe where the use of DDT was banned and blacklisted as a persistent organic pollutant." Ssenyange called for further consultation. "The use of DDT is not just a health issue as it affects the economy. This is why we must all be involved in such sensitive decisions," he said.
Beside the 383 who were sampled, the researchers also interviewed 25 people who were involved and resided in the study area 45 years ago (1959/1960) when the WHO team applied the chemical. The researchers said that there was no empirical evidence that attributes a medical complication on DDT.
A concurrent study for comparative purposes was carried out in Nyarusiza, a village 100km from the major study area where DDT was applied and consistent results were realised from similar samples.
The Uganda Ministry of Health which has for long wanted to reintroduce the use of DDT in its malaria control programme, has to wait for the National Environmental Management Authority (Nema) to study the environmental impact assessment report on DDT use before it makes a decision. Malaria is said to kill 120,000 children in Uganda every year.
The programme manager for the national malaria control programme, Dr John Rwakimari, told The EastAfrican that the environmental impact assessment report will be released soon and submitted to Nema for approval before they start using the chemical.
Dr Gerald Sawula, the deputy executive director of Nema, said the authority will advise on the issue after consulting and analysing the DDT environment impact assessment report.
Dr Rwakimara said that DDT is the most effective alternative in malaria eradication. He added that pathogens could be an alternative but they are three times more expensive than DDT.

Original_Search_Title: Benue Orders Judges to Move Into Quarters
Headline: Nigeria: Benue Orders Judges to Move Into Quarters
Body_Text:
Makurdi — High Court Judges in Benue State have been given a 72 -hour ultimatum to pack into the newly constructed Judges Quarters.
Government has also entered into contractual agreement with a U.S. firm, for the construction of a $20 million Yam and Cassava processing plant, which is to be completed before May 2007.
Both decisions were announced by the Information Commissioner, Honourable Cletus Akwaya, in a press briefing yesterday after a state executive meeting.
Akwaya said the pop musician 2-face Idibia, would be honoured by the state government today, for his outstanding contributions to the music industry both in the country and abroad.
Akwaya said the ultimatum issued to the High Court Judges followed completion of work on the building and furnishing of the quarters located along Makurdi-Gboko road.
The quarters, which he described as one of the projects initiated at the inception of the George Akume administration in 1999, has been provided with all necessary conveniences, including access roads, fencing, water, electricity among others.
For this reason, he said the state government has resolved that "all High Court judges in Makurdi, allocated papers for the quarters should move into the quarters on or before the end of this month."
He said government has also entered into agreement with Messes J.P. Technologies Incorporated of the U.S.A, for the construction of a $20 million cassava processing plant.
He said by the agreement, "the state government is going to contribute about $500,000, and a piece of land that would be used for the factory."
With the establishment of the factory, the state would have three factories related with the processing and utilisation of cassava and its products.
Others are the cassava processing factory and the ethanol producing factory.

Original_Search_Title: Children's Plight Worsened By Lingering Conflict
Headline: Cote d'Ivoire: Children's Plight Worsened By Lingering Conflict
Body_Text:
Abidjan — Twelve-year-old Monique Yao lost her father to war, as well as her seat in the classroom.
Her mother hopes to help her reclaim the latter some day soon.
"I asked for help from people, but I could not come up with enough money and she was dismissed," Dame Sophie Yao says, seated next to the table where she sells fruit and attieke, a cassava-based favourite dish.
She says she might be able to come up with enough money to put Monique back in school soon, but the young girl would have to split her time between studying and working with her mother in the market to keep the family afloat.
Monique sells fruit at their street stall daily until 10 p.m., when she returns to what was to be a temporary home, where she, her mother and her three-year-old brother have lived since they fled fighting in western Cote d'Ivoire three years ago.
Monique's father was killed as rebels and government forces wrestled for control of the Yaos' hometown of Man.
The young girl represents the increasingly tough plight children face in Cote d'Ivoire - West Africa's one-time oasis of stability, split in two in 2002 and slogging along ever since in a state of 'no war, no peace.'
The conflict forced 700,000 children to abandon their studies, according to the UN Children's Fund (UNICEF).
"Thousands of children are up against poverty, abandonment, lack of education, malnutrition, negligence and vulnerability," Youssouf Oomar, UNICEF representative in Cote d'Ivoire said last week on the release of the agency's annual report on the state of the world's children.
Cote d'Ivoire ranks among the 15 countries with the highest under-five mortality rates in the world, the UNICEF report says, and is one of the few countries where child mortality is on the rise.
The rate climbed to 194 deaths per thousand children under five in 2004, from 157 deaths per thousand in 1999 - the year of Cote d'Ivoire's first coup d'etat.
UNICEF puts armed conflict among the greatest threats to childhood worldwide, along with poverty and HIV/AIDS.
Armed conflict exposes children to exploitation as combatants or sex workers, guts basic infrastructure, and devastates primary education, the report says. Most often, UNICEF says, children are among the most vulnerable when conflict precludes essential services like health and protection.
For Cote d'Ivoire's children, Oomar said, "Day in and day out life is a fight for survival."
Survival for 14-year-old Ousmane Kone and his family rests partly in the hands of motorists he urges to buy packets of Kleenex at a bustling roundabout in Abidjan's Abobo neighbourhood.
Beads of sweat on his forehead, in his tattered knee-length T-shirt Kone runs non-stop from car to car amid the roundabout's nearly permanent traffic jam.
"If I don't sell a package of 15 by the evening, there won't be enough money for the family's needs, and it will be my fault," he said.
If he is lucky enough to find buyers for all 15 packets, Ousmane brings 500 CFA francs, or a little less than one US dollar, home to his family.
Ever since Kone's father - a craftsman - lost all his tools in arson fires during unrest in Abidjan, Ousmane and his 12-year-old sister, Kady, must work to help make ends meet.
Kady washes dishes for her aunt who sells alloco - fried plantain - another Ivorian favourite.
Sometimes it is the children's income alone that allows the family to buy the day's meal, Ousmane says.
Ousmane did not attend primary school - only a few years of Koranic school. He hopes to train as a mechanic one day.
Some students in the rebel-held north who have gone to school are wondering whether they're any better off for it.
National exams have not been held in the north in over two years, leaving some 60,000 students in limbo. Without their end-of-year diplomas, their studies will have been in vain.
The UN has repeatedly called on the Ivorian government to get the education system back on track.
In a statement earlier this year UNICEF said the blockage "not only threatens [children's] educational development, but also their physical safety: thousands of children who should be studying and writing for exams are out on the streets with no certainty of their future."
The situation also breeds resentment and potential violence among youth - an explosive element in a country whose condition is already fragile, UNICEF warned.
[ This report does not necessarily reflect the views of the United Nations ]

Original_Search_Title: Cassava - an Alternative Crop
Headline: Zambia: Cassava - an Alternative Crop
Body_Text:
Lusaka — Famine is being experienced in many parts of Zambia. In my home village Msoro, all mangoes were harvested while still green and used for food. My father's mango trees attracted scores of people who cleared them early in the season.
People are still wondering how they will survive until the pumpkins save them. Thanks to food aid efforts that have provided them with peas and other food stuffs, the people in that area are still alive. The country has continued to rely on food aid year after year. Moreover, food aid NGOs grow faster than developmental NGOs in this country.
The newspapers continue to inform the public on the plight of people in various parts of Zambia. The cause of the famine is always quoted as drought that was experienced last season. The lack of irrigation facilities has been recognised as contributing to this famine.
But lack of irrigation is not the only cause of low productivity. Lack of good seed, lack of fertiliser, low soil fertility, poor crop management and poor post-harvest management are some of the causes of low productivity and famine. Presence of irrigation facilities will not automatically remove famine from smallholders. Many times the FAO representative has questioned Zambians why they do not take up cassava as a food crop as West Africans have done. After experiencing this year's famine, it is an opportune time for Zambians in many provinces other than the Northern Province which depends on cassava, to start looking at cassava as something that can be a staple food rather than a snack only. Slowly, many Zambians will come to appreciate cassava.
With cassava in the ground, the famine caused by drought or lack of inputs would be less severe for many small holders. The market, through the Food Reserve Agency, is available and that should be an incentive to production of cassava for commercial purposes. However, having food all year round should be enough incentive for smallholders.
Cassava is a perennial shrub with an edible root. It grows in many areas of Zambia. Cassava can grow on marginal lands where maize and other crops do not grow well. It can tolerate drought and can grow in low-nutrient soils. Because cassava roots can be stored in the ground for up to 24 months, and some varieties for up to 36 months, harvest can be delayed until market, processing, famine or other conditions are favourable.
Cassava provides human food as a source of dietary energy, is used for production of animal feed and other starch-based products. Roots can be processed into granules, pastes, flours, etc., or consumed freshly boiled or raw while leaves are consumed as a green vegetable, which provides protein and vitamins A and B. Cassava starch is used as a binding agent, in the production of paper and textiles, and as monosodium glutamate, an important flavouring agent in Asian cooking. Nigeria is the largest producer of cassava in the world. The average yield of cassava in Nigeria is about 10.6 tonnes per hectare. Impact studies have revealed that in Nigeria the introduction of improved cassava varieties has provided food for 50 million people. Zambia has only 10 million people.
Cassava can be grown singly or intercropped with crops. Although fertiliser can be applied to increase productivity, cassava is grown without fertiliser application. Roots can be harvested between six months and three years after planting.
The major pests of cassava are the cassava green mite, the cassava mealybug, and the variegated grasshopper. The main diseases affecting cassava are cassava mosaic disease, cassava bacterial blight, cassava anthracnose disease, and root rot. These together with poor cultural practices cause high yield losses that may be as high as 50 per cent.
Improved cassava varieties that are disease and pest resistant, low in cyanide content, drought resistant, early maturing, and high yielding, good cooking quality have been developed and research is continuing. Varieties with resistance to the major diseases give higher yields of about 50 per cent more than the local varieties. Through the introduction of natural enemies, there has been a reduction in cassava mealybug damage and damage caused by the cassava green mite.
Many cassava varieties contain cyanides which are poisonous. Inadequate processing can lead to chronic toxicity. Various processing methods are used such as grating, sun drying, and fermenting. These reduce the cyanide content. Cyanide is also removed from leaves used for vegetables through crushing and cooking for long periods.
The production of cassava is dependent on a supply of good quality stem cuttings. The multiplication rate of these vegetative planting materials is unfortunately very low compared to grain crops, which are propagated by seeds. About 10 planting stakes can be gotten from a parent plant. In addition, cassava stem cuttings are bulky, and highly perishable as they dry up within a few days. But the field can be expanded using cuttings from each harvest.
Research has developed techniques to make two-node cuttings or ministakes that can make 50 plants from each parent cassava. These ministakes are moved and protected in plastic sacks until they can be grown on nursery beds before being planted in the field. Farmers can try experimenting with different methods. After all farmers are best scientists.
As a root crop, cassava requires considerable labour to harvest. Because they are highly perishable, roots must be processed into a storable form soon after harvest. Scientists have been developing simple machines and tools to reduce processing time and labour, as well as production losses.
This is a good time to plant cassava cuttings for they will soon sprout with the rains. Depending on the variety, they will be harvested at a time they are required.

Original_Search_Title: Looking Forward to a New Day
Headline: Uganda: Looking Forward to a New Day
Body_Text:
Belinda Akuge, aged 20, is breastfeeding her 9-month-old baby. She is
wearing a bright-coloured dress and is spotting a weave on her head and can
be mistaken for a business lady.
Akuge is one of the many Congolese women who followed their partners
when they were returning from the Democratic Republic of Congo.
A statement was issued on September 21st, 2005 by the RCD-LM secretary
general, Jean Louis Ernest Kyaviro that the Congolese Rally for Democracy
Liberation Movement wanted the Uganda government to repatriate the Congolese
women who are in Uganda back to Congo.
According to the statement, they had learnt that these women were living
under difficult situations. When the news was broken to the Congolese women,
some showed anger and pain because of the suffering they are enduring in
Uganda as a result of following the men they loved while others showed
relief and joy because their countrymen are concerned about them and want
them back, meaning that they still have hope for a better future in their
own country regardless of what they have undergone in Uganda.
Tough times
Akuge who is eight months pregnant for instance comes from Kisangani and
has three other children but they were stolen by their father and taken to
Mbarara although she does not know the exact village where the children are
staying.
She was abandoned by the father of the three children and the man who
impregnated her has denied responsibility of the pregnancy.
"I am pregnant but everyday, I have to go to the disco or drinking place
to get a man who will give me money to feed myself and pay rent," she says
with a sad face.
Akuge says because she cannot earn a living, she has now resorted to
prostitution in order to survive. She says she wants to go back to her
homeland and parents where she can get food and someone to look after her
children.
Another lady, Solangi has three children all with different fathers who
abandoned them. She also earns her daily bread through prostituting herself
so that she can put a meal on the table for her children at the end of the
day. "I am sleeping with men whose health status I do not know as a result
of poverty," she says. Solangi hopes the Ugandan government can provide
transport for them since they neither have money nor any other means to go
back home.
After more than five years of suffering, sometimes going without food,
becoming prostitutes, watching their friends die and not being given decent
burials, the Congolese women are ready to return home, begin new lives and
forget what they have gone through while in Uganda.
"Here, we are being stigmatised everywhere we go. People say, "Congolese
have Aids'," says Anachi, one of the Congolese interviewed.
"We have become the laughing stock of people in Gulu, they call us
thieves, some say we have stolen their men and they even laugh at us because
we eat cassava leaves as food," says Tete, who has a 16-month-old baby.
Tete says they have suffered enough and that if they were near Congo,
they would have gone back home by now. She says they are still stuck in
Uganda because they do not have money.
"We have been humiliated by the very men we followed," she says. If the
Ugandan government or even the government back home can give them transport,
then they are ready to go back. But one thing they will not do is to leave
their children with their so called fathers.
"We shall go back with our children, these soldiers did not pay our
dowry so we shall take our children," Solangi continued. "We came with them
but we do not even know their parents or where they come from, so why should
we leave our children with people we do not know?" Anachi questions.
According to Opio Bosco, the secretary for youth, Kasubi central, the
government should find a way of repatriating these Congolese back to the DRC
because they are really suffering.
Most of them have been abandoned by the soldiers so they resort to
prostitution as a means of survival. Opio says the women now sell themselves
to the civilians and to whoever will give them money.
Evelyn Aya, the secretary for finance LC1, Kasubi central, says that the
women have gone to the extent of providing their services to young boys who
have money. "For any amount of money, they will sleep with any man and they
normally ask 'how much do you have?'" Aya says.
Government's stand
When contacted after the release of the statement, the Army spokesman,
Lt.Col. Shaban Bantariza is reported to have said that the women's return
was not Uganda's responsibility.
"Why did they follow us? Were they abducted? Those women were adult
Africans who came to Uganda. They should get in contact with them." He said
that for the Ugandan government to return the Congolese could amount to an
extradition.
Bantariza however denied allegations that the women were living in poor
conditions and is also reported to have said that they are business women on
kyeyo.

Original_Search_Title: Government Bans Jatropha Tree Exports
Headline: Zimbabwe: Government Bans Jatropha Tree Exports
Body_Text:
Harare — THE Ministry of Agriculture has specified the jatropha, banned its export and adopted the tree along with cassava as major development crops for the next five years, a Cabinet minister said yesterday.
In an interview, the Minister of Agriculture, Dr Joseph Made, said his ministry would also work to increase production of sugarcane, castor bean and soyabean, among others, as it seeks to develop crops which can be used to develop bio-fuel.
"This means that the crops, which we are calling new crops, will be developed with great vigour and a lot of energy will be put in the planting, marketing and funding of the crops.
"Special emphasis will, however, be placed on jatropha because of the quantity of bio-fuel it can produce," said Dr Made.
"We want to grow jatropha as a plantation crop and it has already become a controlled crop. We are working on the necessary paperwork and with immediate effect we will not allow the selling or export of the seeds to any country although local farmers can sell the crop or seed among themselves.
"In addition, the National Oil Comp-any of Zimbabwe (Noczim) will become the sole buyer of the crop while the Grain Marketing Board (GMB) will be involved in the distribution of the seeds because we want them to be distributed widely," said the minister.
He said although the country had conducted some useful research on jatropha, a team of agronomists and researchers would be sent to India, Mozambique and the Democratic Rep-ublic of Congo for further explore the optimum capacity of the plant.
Dr Made said India had developed several high-yielding varieties and an efficient crop husbandry system which Zimbabwe wanted to study for possible implementation.
He said his ministry would begin vigorously promoting the crop next year, adding that they were already making budgetary considerations for the campaign.
"We want every farming household to be involved in the production of the crop. We want all agricultural institutions to be involved in the promotion of the plant while schools will also be involved in its production.
"Extension workers have been asked to talk about jatropha at all agricultural functions they organise and where they are invited, in addition to the subject they would be tackling," he said.
Jatropha is a shrub-like tree which grows naturally in all regions of the country.
It, however, thrives more in areas with low rainfall and poor soils, which cannot sustain many ordinary crops.
The seed have between 30 and 40 percent oil content, which has great potential in bio-diesel production.
Jatropha can be grown from seed or cuttings and will start producing seeds after two to three years.
Cassava, which has also been targeted for development, is rich in starch and ethanol. It is a high-energy plant, which is suitable for both human and animal consumption.

Original_Search_Title: Mini Agric Show Revives Buea Potentials
Headline: Cameroon: Mini Agric Show Revives Buea Potentials
Body_Text:
- A mini Agric Show co-sponsored by the Ministries of Agriculture and Women Empowerment pulled crowds in the Great Soppo neighbourhoods of Buea, October 17.
Daouda, representing the Governor, chaired the occasion which marked this year's World Rural Women's Day and the World Food Day. Both Provincial delegates, Francis Leku (Agric) and Dr Mrs Niger for women empowerment, underscored the reason for the joint celebration as focusing on the rural woman as a principal producer of food.
Mrs Niger urged the custodians of culture to move with time lifting the hurdles which had so far disfavoured the woman. Such include the customary laws that compel the woman to use land but never to own it. The mini shows exposed the food production and animal rearing prowess of the women and men. Cassava, cocoyams, plantains, pigs and goats among many were in abundance. The organizers offered wheelbarrows, cutlasses, hoes and water-spray cans to encourage the farmers.
The show was animated by choir groups from various cultural settings. When the show turned into a market to end the day, the two organising Ministries certainly took stock of the potentials of the rural woman.

Original_Search_Title: Agriculture Ministry Increases Sharing in GDP
Headline: Angola: Agriculture Ministry Increases Sharing in GDP
Body_Text:
Luanda — The Ministry of Agriculture and Rural Development has increased in 2005 its sharing from eight to 12 percent in the Gross Domestic Product (GDP) comparing to the previous year, in the sectors of agriculture, cattle breeding and fisheries, the incumbent minister Gilberto Lutucuta said on Monday in Luanda.
Mr Lutucuta, who was speaking on the sidelines of the 29th Broad Consultative Council of the Ministry of Agriculture and Rural Development, taking place in Luanda, said that 80 percent of the activities carried out this year by his ministry yielded "a good result", which will enable to achieve the foreseen goal of 26 percent, of its contribution to GDP.
The minister guaranteed that, from next year the country will stop importing cereals such as maize, whose production has grown from 600 to 800,000 tons per year, while the production of cassava flour generated surplus and fruit-growing, which will guarantee a self-sufficiency in consumption throughout the country.

Original_Search_Title: One Small Meal a Day, Or None At All
Headline: Kenya: One Small Meal a Day, Or None At All
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Beatrice Kalunda can hardly complete three sentences without uttering the word "El Nino". It is easy to understand why. The last time she and her family had complete meals for a season was during the abundant El Nino rains. That was seven years ago, when she last harvested from her seven acre farm.
"You see that big granary over there? It overflowed with food," she reminisces as she points at a collapsing wooden store.
Her expression dims as she refers to a much smaller storage bin nearby. "After that, we built that granary to cater for subsequent seasons, which we expected to get back to normal. Unfortunately, no food has ever seen the inside of it."
The empty store shakes and squeaks in response to the wind blowing past. It, too, may crumble any time. The 54-year-old woman does not seem to worry much about this. She can tell that it will not be useful this season either. The maize seedlings on her dry shamba, barely 15cm high, are beginning to wilt.
Kalunda is a resident of Mutikati Village in Kathonzweni division, one of the places hardest hit by chronic absence of food in Makueni district. The district is listed among the 17 that are experiencing extended periods of food unavailability.
About 31 per cent of the people in the entire district lack enough food to eat. More than 78 per cent of the affected six divisions in the area need emergency food aid.
Most households here have experienced failed crops for successive years because of worsening weather. Rains come for two to three days, prompting people to plant, and then abruptly stop. This combines with poor agricultural practices to render the already impoverished households incapable of getting enough food to eat.
The starving divisions of Makueni are generally semi-arid. They are suitable for producing hardy crops like cassava, millet and sorghum. But the majority of farmers are not willing to change their agricultural practices to go with the present weather development. They plant maize and ignore the drought-resistant crops.
"It is difficult to convince these farmers not to grow maize," says District Agriculture Officer, John Kibe. A drive around parts of Kathonzweni division reveal great disparities between farmers producing crops other than maize.
"The last time we had a harvest here was during El Nino," she says, praising the weather phenomenon that otherwise caused disaster in other parts of the country in 1997 and 1998.
She is aware of the havoc El Nino brought along, but to her, the food harvest that came out of the season outweighs the problems it caused. Says she: "Even though El Nino came along with both human and animal (water-borne) disease, it brought us a lot of food. There was so much to eat that some of the food rotted in the granary. When I reflect now, it appears as if we were being blessed with a once-and-for-all good harvest to last us a lifetime."
And she has good reason to think along those lines. "Since then, we plant crops, which germinate, but only grow for about one-and-a-half to two months. Thereafter, they simply wither and allow the wind to blow them off the ground," she says. This event has repeated itself every season.
Once that happens, Kalunda and her neighbours pick up the drying diminutive maize stalks and feed them to the animals. With a slight beam, she remarks: "At least the cows get something to eat."
After that, the villagers spend another year staring at empty farms and glancing at the skies in anticipation of rain. They are an admiringly optimistic lot. They keep hoping that the following season would be good, and quickly till their lands at the slightest signs of rain.
However, signs of tire are beginning to emerge. Kalunda, for example, is already musing over relocating. "From the look of things, I may have to go to Mavindini, where my husband lives with my elder co-wife," she says, and reveals: "I go there once in a while to solicit for food when I am stuck. We might as well just follow the father of my children to live there."
Mavindini is a neighbouring division, to which Kalunda's husband went to when the situation in Kathonzweni became too much to bear. It is not as badly affected by hunger. That is the scenario in Makueni district. One village can completely lack food, while the next shows no sign of food scarcity.
In Mutikati village, where Kalunda lives, many young men have abandoned their homes in search of alternative sources of food and income.
Our guide, Martin Kamwanza, a data analyst with the Arid Lands Resource Management Project (ALRMP) in Makueni, reveals that many also are desperate teenage girls from this region, who now prostitute in some of the trading centres along Mombasa Road, such as the fast growing Mlolongo.
They have had enough of one small meal a day, the norm among famished population in Kathonzweni. "I would not be surprised if I were to establish that most of those girls at Mlolongo are from Kathonzweni," says Kamwanza.
Once in a while, when Kalunda feels she needs a change of diet, she walks to a village some three hours away to solicit cowpea leaves (kunde) from better-off farms "across the valley". Nothing else, other than the boiled maize, salted water and Kunde, features in their daily meal.
Her menu typifies that of villagers in the greater southern part of the district, starting from Kathonzweni division in the central part, to the lower divisions of the district, such as Makindu, Kibwezi and Mtito Andei. These are the areas most affected by the menacing food insecurity. The northern parts are generally unaffected.
Lately though, sections of the north-western regions of the district are beginning to exhibit signs of food stress, according to Daniel Mbuvi, the district drought management officer. "While doing my rounds, I recently noticed that there is a strip cutting across Kilome area and heading towards the north, that is increasingly getting affected," he says.

Original_Search_Title: Season of Many Hungers
Headline: Kenya: Season of Many Hungers
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Robert Rose rolls a pen between his fingers, then raises his head and asks: "Famine? There has not been famine in Kenya. There has been mitigation."
A programme officer with the World Food Programme (WFP), Rose, while trying to demystify a "general misconception", explains that what has often been reported as famine in parts of the country is, in fact, persistent food insecurity or chronic hunger.
"It becomes a famine when there are mass deaths arising from a complete absence of food," he says.
Away from terminology issues, discussions with Rose, officials of Famine Early Warning Systems Network (Fews-Net) and the Government's Arid Lands Resource Management Project (ALRMP), all of whom are members of the Kenya Food Security Steering Group (KFSSG), point towards improper land use by communities, changing climatic conditions, and lacklustre performance by authorities in the affected regions.
Latest information reveal that food insecurity in 17 districts across the country is alarming, and could get worse as the year turns.
The short rains, on which the affected lowlands depend for food production, are failing, says Nancy Mutunga of Fews-Net and James Oduor of ALRMP.
This is after repeated seasons of bad weather. In pastoralist areas, for example, even the hardy camels are reportedly dying of extreme weather conditions. Droughts and floods have chronologically replaced each other in some regions.
Both Oduor and Mutunga say the frequency of poor weather conditions is becoming more regular and unusually prolonged. This has particularly been observed within the past 10 years. Increasing frequency and duration of drought, in particular, have destabilised coping strategies of affected communities. Many have been stretched beyond survival limits.
"This is visible in increasing levels of destitution and a rising number of pastoralist drop-outs, for example, appearing in urban centres," says Mutunga. She says it is a result of reduced pasture and declining numbers of livestock, forcing patoralists out of their traditional ways.
Besides climatic reasons, it is now noted that a major contributor to perennial hunger in the 17 affected districts is linked to rigid practices and incapability among locals, and sometimes negligence by authorities.
In order of severity, the arid areas of Baringo, Turkana and Samburu in the North Rift region, Isiolo, Marsabit, Makueni and Moyale in Eastern, the whole of North Eastern Province, and Tana River in Coast Province, top the list.
Extreme weather patterns, marked by a prolonged absence of rain and, sometimes, floods combine with poor development indicators and stiff lifestyles to make people here highly vulnerable.
Most inhabitants of these areas, being pastoralists, derive their livelihood entirely from livestock. This has played against them lately. When drought strikes, animals die for lack of pasture and water. The immediate sufferers are children. They become malnourished as milk, their only source of proper nutrition, diminishes.
These areas also have poor indicators of development. Education levels are low and infrastructure is poor. "Pastoralists rely on markets, yet these areas lack proper structures and roads," says Oduor.
The long distances that people trek to the markets are serious setbacks and worsen when animals are weakened by harsh weather. Add these to general insecurity, poor health and widespread poverty, among other unfavourable factors.
Yet this is what the people have endured for the past 10 years - bad weather. Droughts in 1992-93, 1996-97 and 1998-2001, floods in 1997 to 2001, and the current agro-climatic changes have only heightened food insecurity in these regions.
There has been a consistent reduction of livestock productivity owing to "inadequate availability of key fundamentals - water, pasture and browse," according to information from Fews-Net. This has led to many deaths in the area.
Hardship coping mechanisms have also been eroded. Mutunga, the Kenya representative for FEWS-NET, says pastoralists had ways of withstanding the extremities of weather, but these have now been interrupted by prolonged climatic changes and increased encroachment on reserve land.
"There used to be designated areas for grazing during wet and dry seasons. During wet seasons, pastoralists grazed their animals around their homesteads. They moved to special areas further away only when the dry season set in. Now there are no dry season areas," she says.
This is not only because of sustained poor weather but also encroachment by people for settlement and charcoal burning practices. Worse, these communities are unable to "develop and adopt new productive initiatives."
Rose cites education as the key, saying it would enhance the people's ability to develop quick escape alternatives. Livestock are not easily recoverable assets, adds Mutunga.
Currently, livestock prices have fallen drastically in some areas, accentuating poverty levels. A few months ago, a resident of Kitui, for example, lamented the declining prices of goats in the area, saying he had sold one for about Sh300.
In marginal agricultural areas, mostly the lowlands of Eastern Province, such as Ukambani, a sustained population pressure has pushed people from the comparatively more productive highland to the unproductive lower sections.
As they shift, the communities practise the same agricultural methods of the higher grounds, which is mainly production of maize. The result has been mass failure of the crop. Parts of Makueni district, for example, are affected, particularly the southern divisions of Kathonzweni, Makindu, Kibwezi, and Mtito Andei.
A drive around Kathonzweni division reveals crop failure in households that planted maize at the start of the last season a month ago. The ones who went for crops like cassava and cowpeas may have a harvest, at least in the first quarter of next year.
"One of the key problems in marginal agricultural areas is enterprise. Some households have moved from the highlands and transferred their planting habits to the lower zones," says Mutunga. "They try to grow maize as they did on the higher ground, but this is not the right crop for some of these places. The result is persistent crop failure," adds Rose.
The lower areas, he points out, are more conducive to production of hardy crops like cassava, millet and sorghum, but which the present generation is uninterested in.
"A change of attitude is needed here," says Mutunga. This compounds the communities' state of unpreparedness when rains fail, and is exactly what has happened. "The failed 2005 long rains represent the second consecutive poor season," says last month's Kenya Food Security Update, jointly produced by the Government, Fews-Net and WFP.
The document says poor start of the critical short rains has raised fears of a worsening situation. The season contributes close to 70 per cent of farm output in the lowlands of Makueni, Kitui, Malindi, Kwale, Kilifi and Taita Taveta districts. Catastrophic food insecurity cannot be ruled out.
The current emergency intervention, planned to end in February 2006, may be extended, Oduor says. "If rains fail, as they are likely to, we shall start another emergency intervention from March to August 2006."
For instance, six divisions in Makueni - namely Kibwezi, Mtito Andei, Nguu, Kathonzweni, Kalawa and Makindu - are listed by the district's drought monitoring team, headed by Daniel Mbuvi, as experiencing a worsening scenario.
Experts are beginning to note a marked rise in stunted children in these areas, suggesting an unresolved problem.
"The nutritional status of children under five years is deteriorating," says Mbuvi. Frustrated farmers are now intensifying charcoal burning and sand harvesting as coping strategies.
Authorities are beginning to express fear that the level at which these activities are being conducted will be disastrous to the environment. Charcoal sacks marked for sale dot the dusty road linking Wote (Makueni's main town) and Makindu, selling at Sh200.
Undeniably, food insecurity in most of the marginal agricultural regions date back to the late nineties, when a dangerous swing of weather between floods and drought hit the country.
According to Oduor, the first of a series of interventions started after the drought of 1999 to 2000. Between May 2000 and September 2002, 26 districts across the country received emergency assistance valued at about 360 million US dollars. About 3.3 million people needed this relief.
There was then a temporary reprieve until September 2004, when it again emerged that about 2.6 million people were in need of aid. This was supplied at a price of 100 million US dollars, until February this year. Again, 26 districts were affected.
Emergency food requirement has been persistent since then. Between March and August this year, about 60 million US dollars has been spent to support over two million food-insecure people in 21 districts. The on-going phase of assistance is to cater for 1.2 starving people in 17 districts. By February next year, about 32.5 million US dollars will have been spent.
Going by a recent observation in Makueni and the reports generated from there and other agro-ecological zones, Oduor's fear that emergency food assistance may go beyond February next year may be well founded.
These emergency operations, known among humanitarian personnel as EMOP, are the mitigation Rose refers to. They have prevented a possible outbreak of famine.

Original_Search_Title: Why is HIV Still Being Transmitted?
Headline: Zambia: Why is HIV Still Being Transmitted?
Body_Text:
Lusaka — A story in The Post on Tuesday this week told of how the poor
people of Chama district "are living in fear due to marauding
elephants". As it is, there is not much food around in parts of the
district and the people are apparently depending on mangoes for food,
but when the elephants come into the villages, they eat all the
mangoes. This is after the elephants had up-rooted and eaten all the
cassava crop the people got from the government earlier in the year.
For me, while I sympathised with and felt for my dear brothers and
sisters in Chama, the story reminded me about another type of marauding
elephant, the human immuno deficiency virus, HIV.
Wherever I speak in public these days on whatever subject, I will
always start with telling the audience about our great elephant and how
it bears great similarities and contrasting features in its behaviour
and characteristics to the human immuno deficiency virus. The African
elephant (Loxodanta africana to give it its full scientific Latin
definition) is the largest land animal, and as such it consumes a large
amount of food and water resources.
One adult elephant can eat up to 260 kg of food, and drink 220
litres of water per day. Anyone who lives in elephant territory and
tries to grow or store food, will tell you that these gentle giants eat
a tremendous amount of food in a short time. They consume a large
amount of food resources - quite often, laying fields of crop and
stretches of vegetations completely bare over the years. Ask those
people of Chama.
On the other hand, HIV is a virus, a microbe so small that it can
only be seen through a specialised 'electron' microscope. Ordinary
laboratory microscopes, no matter how powerful, cannot see it. Yet it
too consumes a lot of resources daily - human, financial, physical,
mental, intellectual, time and medical resources, sometimes wiping out
entire families and whole communities in the process.
The history of AIDS in Africa will always remind us of the story and
images of the empty huts of the Ugandan village of Rikai - where scores
of families were completely wiped out by the "slim disease", leaving
only the very old, to care for the very young.
In Zambia alone today, we hear that over 200 people die everyday, as
a result of HIV-related causes. HIV is not only consuming people, it is
consuming finances.
Unprecedented huge amounts of money are being spent every day on HIV
and AIDS - in education, awareness, care, support and treatment. There
is a whole new industry around HIV and AIDS - providing jobs,
nessecitating transport, travel, communications, feeding and so on. Not
to mention the hidden costs of people spending time tending the sick,
travelling between townships and towns to be at bedsides of the sick,
and gravesides of the dead.
There is no other medical condition in the history of mankind which
has consumed and continues to consume so much money, time, medicines,
hospital space and medical personnel.
The African elephant, though gigantic in size, moves very quietly as
it ambles silently between feeds and to look for water to drink. A herd
of elephant can pass through a village at night and wipe out all their
crop, and no one will hear a thing. So too the human immuno deficiency
virus moves from person to person mostly at night, through entire
families, communities and populations. Quietly and silently over the
years, only for the effects of its having moved there to be seen many
years later.
We also hear that in Zambia, more than 200 new HIV infections are
taking place everyday. The proverbial elephant is moving around quietly
in the peace and tranquility of our homes, colleges, bars, clubs and
hotels, planting the seeds of consumption of huge resources later.
What remains truly amazing though, is the fact that these new
infections are taking place in the midst of the vast amounts of
knowledge we all have, from all the awareness education we have
received and continue to receive, over the last two decades. New
infections are taking place despite each one of us having had a very
close personal encounter with AIDS. We all have seen a very close
friend or family member suffer and die from the consequences of HIV
infection. Yet so few of us are willing to take the necessary personal
steps to find out where we stand with this virus, so that we can take
the necessary steps to protect ourselves and prolong our lives. It
seems so true, as we have said several times in this column, that as
far as HIV and AIDS are concerned, experience teaches us nothing.
The tragedy of it all is that even as I write these words, they ring
hauntingly familiar and repetitious, because they have been written
again and again in this column in the last two years, and in other
pages of all our newspapers countless numbers of times before.
They have been taught to us in workshops and seminars, and repeated
on the radio and on electronic media. Yet more than two hundred new
infections are taking place daily.
Someone wrote to The Post in the course of the year (during that
controversial debate on whether aspiring parliamentarians and
presidents should undergo mandatory HIV testing) to say that the HIV
and AIDS message has reached a point now where it is now "boring"!
Boring? Really? When over two hundred Zambians are dying every day,
and a further two hundred or more new infections are taking place
everyday? Are people so bored with hearing the same messages over and
over again that they now no longer care anymore what happens to their
lives?
Perhaps they are very bored. When you think about it, it cannot be
because of ignorance of the truth that people are ignorant of the being
infected daily. People who study these things have told us time and
time again that awareness levels of HIV and AIDS issues in Zambia are
very high - as high as 98 per cent.
This means that nearly ten out of every ten adult Zambians are not
ignorant of the facts regarding HIV and AIDS, and how the virus is
passed around. Just about every adult knows what needs to be known, so
that the spread of HIV cannot be placed entirely on ignorance of the
dangers and possible consequences of risky sexual practices, for
example. So if it is not ignorance, what can it be? Could it be that
people, though armed with knowledge, are deliberately ignoring all this
knowledge and knowingly taking risks? Or is the knowledge that they
have been numbed by fear - fear of the unknown, and the fear we have
described in these pages in the past as "fear of fear itself". Or is it
the fear of the stigma and discrimination, that has left many of our
people more prepared to die of AIDS-related illnesses than to find out
whether they have been infected with HIV or not. On the eve of World
AIDS Day, I was honoured to be the main speaker at the candlelight
service at my local church in Riverside Kitwe, with the theme Make a
promise. Keep your Promise. At some point during my speech, I asked the
congregation how many of them have been for an HIV test. In a
congregation of over one hundred, only ten or so people had had a test.
There was nothing new in this low figure, (and there is no reason for
anyone who was in the congregation that night to feel bad), because
that is how the situation is throughout our country. It seems that
Zambians just do not like to test for HIV. The National AIDS Council
tells us that national coverage of VCT is only at about nine per cent,
which means that only nine people out of every one hundred have taken
the trouble to know what their HIV status is. So, I pointed out to the
congregation that in reality, the larger majority of those among them
who have not been for a test (mainly because they are afraid of the
result) are actually afraid to find out that they do not have HIV. In
our country which has an adult HIV prevalence of around 16 per cent, 84
per cent of the adults (about eight out of every ten, or four out of
every five) are actually not infected with HIV. For every one person
who has been infected and is HIV positive, there are four people who
are not infected and who are HIV negative. Yet most people are still
afraid to test. Afraid as it were, to find out that they do not have
the virus. And so the virus continues to spread, moving quietly among
our people consuming them and resources in the process. Not entirely
because of ignorance, but some mostly merely ignoring everything they
have been taught and have heard. Of course it would be naïve to suggest
that people ignoring the knowledge and facts they have constitute the
only reason why HIV is still being transmitted at such unacceptably
high rates in our country. There are many other factors, most of them
so inextricably intertwined and interlinked with each other in a very
complex matrix where everything not only depends on everything else,
but is also closely related to everything else.
For example, we know that the high prevalence of sexually
transmitted diseases plays a major role in the transmitting of HIV. But
the transmission of sexually transmitted diseases itself is linked to
the poor state of the health facilities to treat and control the STDs
effectively, and to a culture where infected people hide their STDs
anyway (even from their regular sexual partners) and are reluctant to
have them treated till very late. STDs and HIV, as we have seen in the
past, are sexually transmitted "cousins" - an unholy alliance of
travelling companions that move together, opening doors for each other
and each creating conditions under which the other cousin can easily
enter and then thrive. Where one thrives, the other will enter more
easily and also thrive. All these factors are themselves also linked to
a culture that tolerates men having multiple sexual partners, and to
men generally not using condoms as well as to men not allowing women to
initiate the use of condoms. This in turn is linked to lack of
empowerment of women, which leaves them in the lower social status that
is perpetuated by the fact that women tend to be generally less
educated and more likely to be forced by economic circumstances to seek
commercial sex work, during the execution of which they may be 'forced'
into risky sexual behaviour to increase their earnings.
Low levels of education among women, sometimes related to girls
dropping out of school early, is linked to early engagement in sexual
activities, which we know is one of the reasons why HIV infection rates
are four times higher in teenage girls than in teenage boys. As if it
is not enough that these 'social' factors staked up against women,
making them vulnerable to HIV infection, women are also biologically
and physiologically more susceptible to HIV infection just by virtue of
their being different biologically. Low levels of education in general
and among women in particular, the biological factors, early sexual
activity, commercial sex work and the low status of women are all
linked to unemployment and the general prevailing abject poverty that
the majority of our people are living in. Everything inevitably relates
to everything else. But underlying them all is the fact that while
knowledge levels on HIV and AIDS matters are high, the ignoring of the
knowledge and facts is also high, and is a major factor in explaining
why HIV is still being transmited. Who still does not know that
unprotected sexual intercourse is by far the most important means by
which HIV is transmitted in Zambia? Yet how many people still go ahead
and have unprotected sex with sexual partners whose HIV status they do
not know? How many people have multiple sexual relationships?
Ignorance? Or ignoring?
Who still has not heard that condoms do provide some protection - it
may not be absolute or one hundred per cent protection but it is still
a million per cent better than not using any protection at all. Yet how
many people are reluctant to use condoms, with partners whose HIV
status they have no idea of. Ignorance? Or ignoring the facts? Why is
the commercial sex industry obviously still thriving in all our large
cities and towns? How safe is commercial sex work from HIV transmission
for the workers? For the customers? Are the workers ignorant or
knowledgeable of HIV and AIDS facts? Ignorance? Or ignoring common
knowledge? Who has not heard that there is as yet, no cure for HIV and
or AIDS? Yet how many men are being duped into believing that sex with
a virgin will wipe out the virus from their body and their blood?
Ignorance or just ignoring knowledge and fact? Why is HIV still being
transmitted in Zambia? reflectingaids@yahoo.com

Original_Search_Title: SADC Agricultural Production Records Growth
Headline: Southern Africa: SADC Agricultural Production Records Growth
Body_Text:
Luanda — The production of cereals of 653,000 tons produced this year in
the Southern Africa Development Community (SADC) is estimated to grow
by 35 percent.
According to SADC executive secretary, Tomaz Augusto Salomão, who
was speaking on Friday to the press, in Luanda, these estimates include
mainly the production of maize that recorded an increase from 531,000
to 734,000 tons.
The official stated that among the products that are not part of the
cereals, is cassava that is expected to rise from 6,6 million to 8,5
million tons.
Speaking about the Protocol on free flow of people and goods, signed
this year by SADC member States, he said that in spite of being in an
initial stage, the countries of the community can still set up
bilateral contacts.
Thus, he stressed the need for the SADC national committees to reach the same level of development.

Original_Search_Title: National Micro Finance Policy Launched
Headline: Nigeria: National Micro Finance Policy Launched
Body_Text:
Abuja — President Olusegun Obasanjo On 15 December, in Abuja launched the
new Micro finance Policy, Regulatory and Supervisory Framework for
Nigeria and also declared open the National Seminar to mark the United
Nations International Year of Micro Credit, 2005 (IYMC).
The President Said that the two events coming at the same time,
signal the seriousness with which both the international community and
the Nigerian Government take the issue of micro credit.
He stressed the importance of credit or finance as the lubricant of
every economy and that an economy without a stable macroeconomic
environment and a sound financial system cannot meet the needs of the
nation in the contemporary global system.
He reiterated his administration's commitment to Poverty reduction
and employment creation, which are the two major objectives of National
Economic Empowerment and Development Strategy (NEEDS) incorporated in
the Millennium Development Goals (MDGs). This he said, led to the
rationalization and strengthening of some existing institutions as well
as creation of new ones and listed some of the initiatives to include,
1. Merger of Peoples Bank of Nigeria and the Nigeria Agricultural
Programme to form the Nigerian Agricultural, cooperative and Rural
Development Bank Ltd in October 2000 to provide funding and advisory
service to its various customers.
2. Recapitalisation of Agricultural Credit Guarantee Scheme Fund in
1999 and 2000; and also recapitalisation of the Bank of Industry.
3. Establishment of the National Poverty Eradication Programme
(NAPEP) in 2001 to formulate policies and implement sustainable poverty
alleviation programmes in favour of disadvantaged groups.
4. Establishment of the National Investment Promotion Council (NIPC)
and the Nigerian Export Processing Zone (NEPZ) to encourage domestic
investment and exports, respectively.
5. Establishment of Small and Medium Enterprises Development Agency
of Nigeria (SMEDAN) in 2003 to promote the development of the micro,
small and medium enterprises sector of the economy.
6. Special Presidential Initiatives on many key agricultural
products including cassava and rice, with the aim of boosting domestic
production and by extension, providing additional opportunities for
farmers.
He acknowledged the special initiatives of the Bankers' Committee
under the leadership of the Central Bank in setting up the Small and
Medium Industries Equity Investment Scheme (SMIEIS) Fund, which enables
commercial banks to invest in the equity of small and medium
enterprises.
He noted that these initiatives of Federal and State Governments
including the private sector have yielded significant result in poverty
reduction and reiterated Governments commitment to providing an
enabling environment and support for effective and sustainable micro
finance development in Nigeria.
The President noted that a major element of the new policy which
pertains to Government is the requirement that States and Local
Government devote at least one percent of their annual budget to micro
credit while the Federal government would continue to provide enabling
legal, macroeconomic and regulatory environment.
He stressed that the new policy does not necessitate state and Local
Government to set up their own Micro finance Bank (MFB) they should
rather encourage their residents to set them up and run them as private
Banks.
He commended the Central Bank of Nigeria for its creative and
innovative efforts in putting together the days event and in particular
for articulating the Policy that will assist the Governments employment
generation and poverty reduction objectives and called on all
interested stakeholders to apply to the Central Bank for licenses to
operate their Micro Finance Banks and Institutions.

Original_Search_Title: Between Southern Leaders And 2007
Headline: Nigeria: Between Southern Leaders And 2007
Body_Text:
Lagos — The auditorium of Excellence Hotel, Ogba in Lagos, was filled to the brim on Tuesday when Southern leaders held its historical political summit. The aim was to set a new agenda for power sharing in 2007. Writes Philip Ogunmade
For the people of South-east and South-south zones of the country, it is now sunrise. The thick blanket which has hitherto severed these zones from the seat of power began to disappear last Tuesday, when in an unanimous decision, all the ethnic nationalities which conglomerate the entire Southern part of the country gladly conceded the position of the President of the Federal Republic of Nigeria in 2007 to these zones. With this development, either of the zones now has the legitimacy to produce a credible personality who by his antecedents, can command the mandate of the people of Nigeria, to hold the ace at Aso Rock come 2007, depending on the consensus they both reach.
This agreement, which was the first of its kind in the history of the southern region, witnessed the South-west of the country represented by Afenifere, the pan-Yoruba socio-political group and the Yoruba Council of Elders (YCE), allowing common sense to prevail. The zone reasoned with its counterpart zones that it was high time either of them produced the President of the country, a position which has since the 45 years of Nigerian independence, eluded them.
This decision was the by-product of the political summit jointly convened by South-west, South-east and South-south under the auspices of Afenifere cum YCE, Ohaneze N'digbo and South-south People's Assembly. The summit was also the aftermath of the committee meeting held last week by the groups at the Lagos Airport Hotel under the aegis of Southern Leaders' Forum. Led by representatives of each zone which included Chief Olu Falae of South west, Chief John Oyegun and Chief Chukwuemeka Ezeife of South-south and South-east respectively, the committee agreed to strike a common deal that would lead to the emergence of the President from the zones.
The summit held at Excellence Hotel, was no doubt, a mission accomplished. The South-west, without raising any question, conceded the Presidency in 2007 to the two zones, a move well appreciated in an apparent spirit of understanding.
It was no doubt a comngregation of who is who as eminent personalities across the South made it a date to grace the summit . Among the eminent personalities at the occasion were Vice Admiral Mike Akhigbe, Chief Mathew Mbu, Chief Olu Falae, Chief Reuben Fasoranti, Anyim Pius Anyim, Chief John Odigie-Oyegun, Rear Admiral Ndubuisi Kanu, Prof. A. B. C. Nwosu, Dr. Tunji Otegbeye, Commodore Ebitu Ukiwe, Chief Chukwuemeka Ezeife, Alhaji Lateef Jakande, Chief Raymond Dokpesi among many others.
General Adeyinka Adebayo, leader of the YCE and chairman of the partnership summit was unavoidably absent and hence, Mbu, a co-chairman chaired the occasion. All and sundry lamented the level of depravity the nation has been driven into by the activities of unsrupulous and disgruntled elements who have allegedly been piloting the affairs of the Nigeria since independence 1960.
In his speech, Dr. Tunji Otegbeye who spoke on behalf of YCE, expressed utmost happiness that such a summit marked the beginning of progress. According to him, it was high time the idea of North-South dichotomy which has virtually formed the basis for power rotation be jettisoned and consigned into the dust bin of history. For Otegbeye, a renowned political activist since the pre-colonial days, the idea of North-South dichotomy is nothing else, but a creation of the colonial masters, which he argued should have faded over the years. His candid view is that power rotation should be based on zonal classification as against the North-South structure.
Appraising the decision, Mbu said the concession on the Presidency was absolutely essential, because according to him, the hour has come for the people's government with a due sense of service to be constituted. This he believes, will put paid to the numerous woes blighting the progress of this country. Mbu regretted that Nigerians have been accustomed with the idea of watching the status quo with the assumption that problems should rather solve themselves as against confronting problems with a view to getting them solved. Hence, he argued that the idea of power concession was taking the bull by the horns.
"It is time we had the government of our own that would make impact through the programmes of that government. Nigeria is facing miriads of problems. Nigerians believe that there are problems, but that those problems would get themselves solved. This is the view of Nigerians. But they don't get solved. These problems must be addressed," he stated.
Mbu was of the view that the partnership of the three zones as well as its concession to pursue a common goal, was the foundation for addressing the problems confronting the entity called Nigeria. Moreover, he argued that the South east and South-south zones would henceforth, reject the feelings of inferiority complex foisted on them and take their positions as parts and parcels of the Nigerian state. He expressed optimism that either of the zones would produce the next President in 2007.
Former Minister of Information, Professor A.B.C. Nwosu, in his own comment, did not hesitate to laud the the South west for agreeing to concede the Presidency to the zones. He was of the firm belief that the consensus was a sheer manisfestation of truth, fairness and equity, assuring the Yoruba that both zones would exploit the opportunity to set an agenda for the progress and development of Nigeria.
"I salute you, the leaders for rejecting in totality the North -South dichotomy. I salute our friends, brothers and sisters from the South-west for accepting that the President must come from South-east and South-south. This is a meeting of veterans to fight against ineptitude in the system on the basis of truth, fairness and equity. It is on this basis that we have agreed that Presidency must come from the South-east and South-south. We must set agenda for good governance. If people here can't set agenda, who else can?" Nwosu asked.
Nwosu also assured that following the resolve on the Presidency, both zones of South-east and South-south would henceforth strike a common deal as to which of the two should present the presidential candidate, who will be well supported by the entire peopleof the South.
Former governor of Lagos State, Alhaji Lateef Kayode Jakande, in his address, x-rayed the state of the country and concluded that a summit of that kind was only necessary, if only the country would be rescued from the woods.
Jakande absolutely regretted that Nigeria, blessed with both human and mineral resources has been battered by poverty, homelessness and all maners of physical degradation rendering it completely backward in the comity of nations.
Hence, he pleaded with the gathering not to be carried away by mere zonal agreement, but should rather device a way of fashioning out development programmes which would promote the living standard of the people when the South deal eventually yields the expected dividends.
His words : "I thank the organisers of this meeting. We need to move Nigeria forward and not backward. We have passed through different times in the history of this country. We 've experienced things we 've never heard; things we've never contemplated. The degree of poverty is unprecedented. 70 per cent of the people are poor and homeless. Yet day after day, dismissal is going on to join a number of Nigerians who have no work. This should be our focus. This country is blessed. The resources we are blessed with cannot be compared with others. How many countries have oil, cassava? Some people get wealthier and wealthier, while our people get poorer and poorer. We cannot continue to allow that. We talk of N1.8 trillion budget, yet education is in a mess. There is nothing to write home about the hospitals. Most of us will remember the time that if you graduated, you got a car in addition. It is not so nowadays. We have a number of graduates on the streets who have no job. We talk of robbery; there will be robbery. Mr. chairman, I plead with this audience, let's formulate a programme that can carry this country forward. We want progress in this country. We want people who will live on their own, not the people who will live on the country.
"We must have agenda, concrete agenda. I'm not bothered by third term, fourth term, fifth term or sixth term. We want a clear cut programme that will serve as a stepping stone for greater Nigeria, "Jakande advised.
However, the consensus of the South to produce the President in 2007, may signify another battle front as the decision will no doubt cause a stir in the camp of Northern elites, who recently resolved to seize power, come what, come may in 2007. These recent developments foretell that events in the next couple of months as the general elections draw nearer and nearer, will be exciting. Exciting, because the battle line has now been drawn between the North and South. And undoutedly, it is going to be a fierce battle as the zones seem to have teamed up for a common goal. Whereas, South-west, South-east and South-south have now agreed to raise one candidate to break the invincibility of the North, the region is also leaving no stone unturned to raise a consensus candidate to battle the South, and thus implying that the presidential battle come 2007, will be a race between the North and the South. This again, brings to the fore, the battle against the North- South dichotomy which is the major bedrock upon which the Southern leaders' summit is predicated.
The summit's strong argument is that Nigeria is a conglomerate of six-geo political zones. Therefore, a situation where power is only being shared among other zones of the North and the South-west negates the principle of justice and fair play which the summit said it is committed to. The South-south and the South-east with strong backing of the South-west seem set to take their destiny into their hands. Presidency then, come 2007, will no doubt be a do or die affair as the zones have vowed not to be spectators any longer in the affairs of the country, but rather as active participants, dispensing power like other zones have been doing. The summit reasoned that for the North to lay claim to 2007 presidency on the belief that the incumbent President Olusegun Obasanjo from the South-west zone has exhausted the chances of other zones, is ungodly, unholy and alters the principle of fair play.
Little wonder then, when Chief Raymond Dokpesi, chairman of the planning committee, while summing up the resolution of the summit said : "We deplore the current simplistic attempt to polarise Nigeria into a North/South divide, even on issues of national importance, particularly with respect to the rotation of power at the centre. For the principle and process of rotation to be equitable and meaningful, it must be based on the existing six-geo political zones.
"That given the fact that the other zones have dominated the highest office of the land since independence, the South -east or the South-south shall produce the President of the Federal Republic of Nigeria come 2007. This demand is based on justice, equity, fairness, good conscience and historical antecedents."
How much impact this resolution will make remains an issue to watch out for as the election year gathers momentum. Although, the summit is not ruling out any possibility of selling the idea to the North, based on the above clarifications, the question agitating the minds of many is, what measure of efforts can get the North convinced, that now is the time for these zones of the South, in the face of its recent launch to seize power again come 2007?

Original_Search_Title: Igbinedion Suspends Two Commissioners
Headline: Nigeria: Igbinedion Suspends Two Commissioners
Body_Text:
Benin-City — Governor Lucky Igbinedion of Edo State has suspended two of his commissioners and members of the State Executive Council (EXCO).
A statement by Chief Press Secretary (CPS) to the governor, Mr Lucky Wasa, said Commissioner of Works, Mr Adolphus Ojobo, and Commerce Commissioner, Mr Francis Naboya "have been suspended from office indefinitely as commissioners in Edo State, for dereliction of duties."
The statement said their suspension had been approved by Igbinedion and takes immediate effect.
"They are to "handover the affairs of their ministries to their Permanent Secretaries."
The announcement of the suspension came as preparations were in top gear yesterday, to welcome the Inspector-General of Police (I-G), Mr Sunday Ehindero, who is scheduled to launch a special armed response squad cum security surveillance telecommunications centre at the Government House, Benin-City today.
Much criticism trailed the activities of the two commissioners, even before Igbinedion's dissolution of the EXCO last year, which is why some commentators said they were amazed that the two men found their ways back when the body was reconstituted about three months later.
However, THISDAY gathered that their suspension may not be unconnected with their alleged dismal performance, especially in the execution of contracts.
The governor is said to be unimpressed by the progress of work at the much-talked-about industrial firms conceived by his administration, which has largely led to the inability of either the Ehor Fruit Juice Factory or the Cassava Processing Plant at Uromi, to see the light of day, after over five years.
Naboya came under fire recently when he expressed immense delight at the "outright sale" of the Ikpeshi Mineral Processing Factory, for what some called a paltry N50 million, though the project is believed to have gulped over N.5 billion of state funds.
Ojobo the Works Commissioner, on the other hand, came under heavy criticism over his role in the scandalous contracts for the supply of vital equipment for the still ongoing Cassava plant, Uromi.
However, Ojobo was reported to have said on the floor of the Edo State House of Assembly that contracts for road projects awarded between 1999 and 2003 had no contractual agreement at all, adding that he had managed since assuming office to regularize five out 19 contracts so far awarded.

Original_Search_Title: 'Politics of Economics' And Zambia's Coming Food Crisis
Headline: Zambia: 'Politics of Economics' And Zambia's Coming Food Crisis
Body_Text:
Lusaka/Hong Kong — Hong Kong trade negotiators are considering a proposal that would allow
developing countries to protect their agricultural commodities from global
commitments to lower import duties. This is seen as a vital instrument to
safeguard the food security of some of the world's poorest nations.
Zambian farmer Margree Chilwesa is faced with a hard choice - he either
has to sell his last season's crop on the cheap or eat it.
"Last year the National Milling Company was buying my grade-A soya at
K1,900 (US$2.5) per kilogram and K1,600 for the standard grade. This year
grade-A is only K1,450 per kilogram," says Chilwesa, who is based in
Kanakantapa on the outskirts of the capital Lusaka.
This year he has produced 21 bags - or 1.5 tons - of soya and 50 bags of
maize on his 20-hectare farm. But he says the market is flooded with produce
from hunger-struck Zimbabwe, where contract farmers grow crops on large
commercial farms of over 300 hectares each. In contrast, Zambian small-scale
farmers have an average farm size of two hectares.
"Who would listen to you even if your produce on the small piece of land
was of high grade? The politics of economics defeats you," Chilwesa
complains.
Chilwesa's problem is that the market price for his crops has plummeted
- fuelled by cheap imports - but he lacks adequate warehousing facilities to
store the produce until the prices rise.
