more students," Bitamazire said.
She also said Government was grant-aiding more community schools as
well as putting up seed schools with the ultimate goal of having a day
school per sub-county.
However, 327 of the 970 sub-counties in Uganda still lack
government-aided secondary schools. Museveni says the possibility of
giving incentives to those who want to invest in building secondary
schools is being looked into. The donor community that finance more
than half of the sh620bn education sector budget say they will give
their view after a formal communication.
"We don't act through newspaper reports. We shall give our opinion
as EFAG (Education Funding Agencies Group) after receiving official
communication from the Government," said Harriet Nanyonjo, the World
Bank's education specialist and EFAG coordinator.
Fagil Mandy, of FAMECON, an education consultancy firm said the
phased implementation of the programme makes it manageable. According
to Museveni's plan, the scheme will kick off with the current S.1
students.
It is estimated to benefit 300,000 pioneers at a cost of sh100b for
the first year. The amount shall be progressively raised until all
levels are covered. Under the scheme, the government will pay sh15,621
per student per term. In addition, a lump sum of sh7m shall be paid per
secondary school. This is expected to reduce the cost for the current
continuing students.
Government has been concerned that expanding access to post-primary education was constrained by high fees.
Presently, only a fifth of Uganda's population of 25 million have
had access to secondary education, the majority from the higher income
groups and urban families. The new scheme is targeted at the low-income
groups and children from rural families.
While school administrators describe as peanuts, the sh15,621 being
paid per student per term, the amount actually translates into a higher
capitation grant than the sh75 government currently pays per student
per school day. This translates into a capitation of only sh6150 per
student per term.
Bitamazire said the amount was computed taking into account the fact
that Government will continue paying teachers salaries, supply
textbooks, laboratory equipment and chemicals as well as the
construction of classrooms. "The fees should be able to come down
because these have been direct costs on the parents," she said.
Headteachers of government schools are worried that any reduction in
the current fees is likely to compromise standards. A headteacher of a
prominent school in Kampala say most schools in the city require not
less than sh350m a term to operate, an amount far above the government
capitation of sh8m.
They said the fixing of fees was one of the reasons why standards in
the once famous government primary schools dropped and the same is
likely to happen to government secondary schools.
"A generalized fees structure is impossible because of the unique
nature of schools, particularly those in urban areas. We have to pay
for piped water. We use flush toilets and electricity for cooking
unlike up country schools. These factors have to be considered," argued
the headteacher.
But a parent said there was no reason why a school whose teachers
are paid for by the government, the building set up by public funds and
the textbooks and laboratory equipment given free of charge should
charge the same fees as private schools that finance all their running
costs.
Mandy also says headteachers take advantage of parents who in most
cases do not know the unit cost of education. He said the issue of
Parents and Teachers' Association (PTA) fees should be addressed.
He said schools that are run like luxury centres were the ones
likely to be affected by the policy and headteachers should be checked
against introducing hidden costs under the guide of boarding charges.
Mandy said special arrangement should certainly be made for urban
schools, although USE targets day schools where three quarters of the
secondary student population is enrolled.
Another parent, Prince Jimmy Galabuzi however warned that the rushed
implementation of USE before addressing quality under UPE will produce
poor quality students.
"UPE on which USE is being built is still in shambles," Galabuzi
said. "When cassava is freshly extracted, it can be eaten raw. It is a
delicacy when well cooked. But the moment it is half-cooked, you will
never like the taste. So unless proper planning is done before
implementing USE, we might end up with half baked students who are not
very useful to society," he argued adding that instead of USE, there
should be free tertiary and university education.
While accepting the quality challenges, Mandy said high quality was
not something achieved overnight. "Quality improvement is s continuous
process," Mandy said. "Quality assurance bodies like the Education
Standards Agency (ESA) and the inspectorate departments in the
districts if strengthened and well facilitated should be able to ensure
that quality is maintained," Mandy said.
Bitamazire said USE will actually lead to quality improvement in UPE
schools. "Because the pupils will now be seeing opportunity for upward
mobility, they will study hard. There will be no automatic promotion to
the secondary level.
The pupils will have to pass the PLE in order to benefit from the
scheme," Bitamazire said. USE is certainly a brilliant idea. It will be
a big relief to the rural parents.
Like was with UPE, it is bound to face a lot of challenges. But
postponing its implementation would mean postponing the challenges. It
therefore needed a bold political decision and with the UPE experience,
the Government resolve and donor support, there is no doubt that the
programme will succeed.

Original_Search_Title: Third Meeting of Comesa Ministers of Agriculture: Cairo, Arab Republic of Egypt, 16-17 November
Headline: Africa: Third Meeting of Comesa Ministers of Agriculture: Cairo, Arab Republic of Egypt, 16-17 November
Publisher: Common Market for Eastern and Southern Africa (Lusaka)
Body_Text:
Cairo — The Common Market for Eastern and Southern Africa (COMESA), Africa's
largest economic grouping comprising nearly 400 million people covering
twenty countries in east, central, north and southern Africa, with a Gross
Domestic Product (GDP) of nearly 300 billion dollars, has continued its
resolve to deepen economic integration through increased trade and
investment.  COMESA accounts for nearly half of Africa's population and the
bulk of the Continent's GDP.
COMESA's integration efforts rest on two major pillars, trade and
investment.  Through the COMESA Free Trade Area (FTA), COMESA member States
have committed themselves to removing tariff, quotas and other restrictions
to trade so as to have free flow of trade.  Already, eleven of the twenty
countries belong to the FTA and this is expected to increase to at least
fifteen countries by 2006.  The FTA provides the basis for transforming
COMESA into a Customs Union by the target date of 2008.  The other pillar is
the COMESA Common Investment Area (CCIA) which seeks to harmonize
investment, legislation, policies and
practices across the region so as to provide an environment conducive to
increased investment both from within the region and also outside the
region.  Egypt hosts the Regional Investment Agency (RIA), a newly
established COMESA body whose mandate is to spearhead the creation of the
CCIA.
The Key to COMESA's integration efforts is the agriculture sector which
accounts for 32 percent of COMESA's GDP, provides livelihoods to about 80
per cent of its labour force, accounts for about 65 per cent of foreign
exchange earnings and contributes more than 50 percent of raw materials to
the regional industrial base. Agricultural trade constitutes an important
aspect of intra-regional trade contributing 36 percent of the US$5.4 billion
COMESA trade in 2004.
It is against this backdrop that Ministers of Agriculture from COMESA
countries met in Cairo, Arab Republic of Egypt, from 16th to 17th November,
2005 to review progress made in enhancing agricultural cooperation and
development within the region as a follow-up to their last meeting held in
Nairobi, Kenya in October 2004.
Apart from Ministers from the COMESA member States, the Cairo meeting
was attended by senior representatives from African and international
organizations and cooperating partners who included among others, the
African Union, Inter-Africa Bureau for Animal Resources (AU-IBAR), the
African Development Bank (AfDB) the Food and Agricultural Organization of
the United Nations (FAO), Eastern and Central Africa Programme for
Agricultural Policy Analysis (ECAPAPA) of the Association for Strengthening
Agricultural Research in Eastern and Southern Africa (ASARECA), African
Agricultural Technology Foundation (AATF), US Embassy in Kenya, USAID's
Regional Agricultural Trade Expansion Support Program (RATES), International
Development Enterprises (IDE), Their Excellencies, the Ambassadors of COMESA
countries based in the Arab Republic of Egypt.  The private sector and
business community in the region was represented by Mr. Mostafa M. El-Ahwal,
President of the COMESA Business Council.
The Third Meeting of COMESA Ministers of Agriculture was officially
opened by H. E., Engineer Ahmed El-Liethy, Minister of Agriculture and Land
Reclamation who was accompanied by a high-powered Egyptian delegation which
included, H.E. Ahmed Abou-ElGheit, Minister of Foreign Affairs and
H. E. Dr. Faiza Aboul Naga, Minister for International Cooperation, who also
made statements at the opening session.  The Ministers from Egypt informed
the meeting that a key positive development was a major policy shift by the
Arab Republic of Egypt to source more of its food requirements from other
COMESA countries rather than from outside the region.  For example, Egypt
seeks to increasingly source the bulk of its 250,000 MT per year beef
requirements from within COMESA, notably from Ethiopia, Sudan and Uganda
instead of external markets.  Egypt also extended an offer of assistance to
other COMESA countries in the areas of irrigation development and
agricultural training, which was warmly received. Ministers from other
COMESA countries also made statements.
Dr. Babagana Ahmadu, Director, Agriculture and Rural Economy made a
statement on behalf of the African Union, while Honourable Fakudze Mtiti,
Minister of Agriculture, Kingdom of Swaziland, proposed a vote of thanks to
the Government and People of the Arab Republic of Egypt on behalf of the
delegates.
Addressing Ministers and delegates at the meeting, the COMESA Assistant
Secretary General for Programmes, Mr. Sindiso Ngwenya, observed that COMESA
was a very large market with huge economic potential.  He observed that the
growth of intra-COMESA trade among the eleven countries making up the FTA,
averaged 22% annually.  Mr. Ngwenya informed the meeting that the good news
was that an increasing amount of trade was being transacted directly between
and amongst member States instead of through third countries and that there
was evidence of increased cross-border investment among COMESA member States
with Kenya becoming the largest investor in the East African region and
Egyptian investors being active in Sudan, Mauritius, Kenya, The Union of
Comoros and Uganda among others.  He noted that the elimination of poverty
and creation of wealth can only be done through the intensification of
regional cooperation and integration through COMESA.  Representatives of
regional and international organizations also made statements and/or
presentations.
The Ministers of Agriculture re-affirmed themselves to increased
cooperation in agriculture but observed that this had to be done in tandem
with measures to improve infrastructure connectivity and improving the
efficiency of transport systems within the region.  The Ministers noted that
COMESA has on-going programmes in the infrastructure sector and just two
months earlier, COMESA Ministers of Transport, Communications and Public
Works met in Egypt, to address issues of infrastructure development within
the region.
The Cairo meeting of Agriculture Ministers addressed key issues among
them, the critical need to develop irrigation capacity within the region to
move way from the current dependence by most countries on rain-fed
agriculture; coordinated agricultural research and technology transfer; the
need for a regional policy on biosafety and biotechnology; efforts to stem
the ravages of tsetse and trypanosomiasis; measures to mitigate the threat
of the highly pathogenic avian influenza (HPAI) or "bird flu"; devising a
regional response to the Catastrophic Spread of Cassava Mosaic Virus Disease
(CMVD) and Banana Xanthomonas Wilt (BXW) in the African Great Lakes
Countries; fisheries and livestock development; measures to be taken to
accelerate the implementation of the Comprehensive Africa Agriculture
Development Programme (CAADP) under NEPAD; and the impact of HIV/AIDS on
small-holder agriculture production and productivity.
The Ministers cited food security as a major challenge which needed
concerted and sustainable action through a coherent Regional Food Security
Policy and Vulnerability Reduction Programme for the most affected
populations in the region.  A major aspect of an effective Regional Food
Security Policy is the need to put in place effective Market Information
Systems (MIS), and in this regard, the Ministers noted progress in the
establishment of a COMESA-wide Food and Agricultural Market Information
System (FAMIS) being developed with the assistance of the AfDB.
The Ministers emphasized the need to add value to agricultural products
by promoting agro-processing through the creation of regional value chains
not only within countries, but also across the region with the active
participation and involvement of the private sector.  The Ministers noted
the major achievements made in promoting trade in commodities such as
coffee, cotton and textiles, dairy, maize and other cereals as a result of
targeted interventions through COMESA programmes such as RATES, a USAID
supported programme, which during 2004, convened major regional forums which
brought together key public and private sector players to devise strategies
in these key commodity areas.  The most recent was the Regional Grain Trade
Summit held in Nairobi, Kenya in October 2005, which among other
recommendations, called for the establishment of a Regional Grain
Council.
The Ministers also noted and welcomed the establishment of regional
industry associations such as the Eastern and Southern African Dairy
Association (ESADA) and the African Cotton and Textile Industry Federation
(ACTIF) and urged countries that were not yet participating in these
regional associations to do so as these bodies provided an effective avenue
for forging private sector alliances and networks as well as public/private
sector partnerships.
Other key issues considered by the Ministers included measures that need
to be taken by COMESA countries to take better advantage of the trade
opportunities offered by the U.S. African Growth and Opportunity Act (AGOA)
and the need for a common regional position in the preparations for the
forthcoming negotiations under the World Trade Organization (WTO) and
on-going negotiations under the Economic Partnership Agreements (EPAs) with
the European Union (EU).  The Ministers noted that while issues of
agricultural subsidies in developing countries were critical, it was equally
important for African countries to address supply-side and competitive
issues so as to be prepared to take full advantage of developed country
markets as subsidies are removed.
As a re-affirmation and commitment on the need to enhance, strengthen
and accelerate cooperation on the agricultural sector, the Ministers adopted
the Cairo Declaration on Agriculture titled, "Enhancing Agricultural
Productivity and Competitiveness through the Adoption of Modern Irrigation
Systems, Attaining Regional Food Security through Expanded Regional Markets,
and Creating a Conducive Environment for Investment in the Agricultural
Sector".

Original_Search_Title: Kaduna Refinery Flaunts Capacity to Boost Jobs
Headline: Nigeria: Kaduna Refinery Flaunts Capacity to Boost Jobs
Body_Text:
Before now, Kaduna refinery was a no-go area for journalists. Why? The answer is simple, the gigantic refinery had nothing to show off. It was hardly in operation but now, things have changed. The management of the Kaduna Refining & Petrochemical Company popularly called Kaduna refinery, admitted that they are no longer press shy. Why? The answer is in the article below as stated by the management of the refinery.
A couple of weeks ago, some journalists were taken on a tour of the Kaduna refinery to see things for themselves. As we approached the refinery, scores of men and women and tankers were seen about 200 meters from the gate of the Kaduna Refining and Petrochemical Company. "Who are these people and what do they want around here," asked a journalist not familiar with the petroleum beat.
"They are here for job opportunities. They are around to tap business opportunities," replied an energy editor of a national newspaper.
The journalists were received on behalf of the management of the Nigeria National Petroleum Corporation (NNPC) by the organisation's spokesman, Dr. Levi Ajuonumah who proudly informed the guests that
"All the Fluid Catalytic Cracking Units are working. Kaduna refinery is in business." He said this was unlike what it was before the team of Engr. Funsho Kupolokun introduced Project Pace. This was confirmed by the managing director of the refinery, Engr. Voka Mukoro who spoke on production at the refinery.
Kadunarefinery boosts job creation --Mukoro
Engr. Mukoro spoke on the state of the refinery before Project Pace this way: "There was a time we ran away from the press because of the state of our operations and the state of our refineries. Today, we open our doors so that we can beat our chests that we are doing what we have to do. There has been tremendous changes within the NNPC and at the Kaduna refinery. Within the last 18 months, we've maintained the plant steadily with parts that weren't working now working."
He said the company has produced so much asphalt from plants that were not in operation for sometime, explaining, however, that its production requires some level of foreign crude. With asphalt, construction workers and their counterparts in the various ministries would be engaged and our roads would be motorable.
Asphalt production is not the only product of the refinery that creates jobs.
Commenting on plans by NNPC to introduce ethanol into the Nigerian market, Engr. Mukoro disclosed that although refineries do not produce ethanol anywhere in the world, it has great potentials for creating jobs. He explained that ethanol is produced from cassava, yam, and sugarcane which are essentially agricultural products.
Kaduna refinery produces a lot of tins and drums which are being used by other companies especially vegetable oil-producing companies, thereby helping to boost jobs in that sector of the economy.
In his words, " we are the largest producer of tin in West Africa." Presently, the chief executive of the plant said it produces "200-litre capacity drums from two lines of production each of which has a capacity of 420 drums per hour." He said:
"The drums are used to lift asphalt of different grades and lubricating oils."
In addition, he said the plant produces 18-litre tins whose design manufacturing capacity is 2,400 tins per hour.
KRPC officials placed it on record that "the company has the only Lubes and Lab Plants in West and Central Africa". That's not all, "it is the largest manufacturer of high quality tins and steel drums in Nigeria." It also has "potential demand for both conventional and specialised products."
That's not all, he said the company also produces 2,010 metric tonnes of LPFO popularly called Black oil, per day. Textile factories are major users of black oil although they have complained of the high cost of the product.
Manufacturers of detergents according to Engr. Mukoro, have found the refinery a reliable source of raw materials. For as he said, Kaduna refinery produces 91 metric tonnes of Linear- Alkyl-Benzene per day. Linear-Alkyl-Benzene is a major product used to manufacture detergents.
Already, Engr. Mukoro said many local companies are patronizing the refinery thereby boosting jobs in that sector also.
Petrol - The Kaduna refinery according to its management team, has the capacity to produce 3,857 metric tonnes of petrol a day but Engr. Mukoro said, "as more crude comes in and the capacity for production is on, we could do better." He made it clear that "Kaduna refinery has as at today, 19 million litres of PMS ( Petrol) in storage. For now, the KPRC receives 60,000 barrels per stream day . of Nigerian crude from which it could produce 1,686 metric tonnes of Kerosine daily. Diesel is not left out as the KPRC also has the capacity to produce 3,000 metric tonnes daily.
All of these have been known to create thousands of job opportunities for people who trade in these products or use them for manufacturing purposes.
The company's chief executive said it is a company that can be self-sufficient.
"This refinery can run profitably."
Workers won't lose jobs to privatisation -- Management
Since the Kaduna refinery is one of those listed for privatisation, Engr. Mukoro was asked about the fate of the company's employees.
Engr. Voka Mukoro who spoke of a harmonious working relationship with employees said of the privatisation plans: "We've discussed extensively with the unions. The core investor is to take 51% , NNPC 49%". He assured that "anybody still working here is still working as NNPC staff. Those who no longer have jobs to do would be redeployed to any other department in our unit of NNPC."
In his opinion, "fears of retrenchment are not real as there would be no mass lay- off of staff."
Labour Vanguard was however not in a position to verify his comments on meetings with in-house unions on privatisation as at the time of filing this report.
Engr. Mukoro said, " We are working with the Bureau of Public Enterprises to achieve privatization by the end of this year; 2005".
Summing up the activities of KPRC, Engr. Mukoro said: "Project Pace is transforming NNPC. This is phase two of Project Pace and we are still making sure that everybody in NNPC is on board. Before the end of 2007, we should have arrived at our goal."

Original_Search_Title: Nigeria Envoy Calls for Direct Trade
Headline: Namibia: Nigeria Envoy Calls for Direct Trade
Body_Text:
Windhoek — NIGERIA'S High Commissioner to Namibia Okun Ayodeji has appealed
to Namibian business people to consider forging trade links with their
counterparts in his country if the two countries are to strengthen
economic ties.
In an interview with New Era, the diplomat acknowledged the strong
political relationship that flourishes between the two nations, though
he expressed disappointment at the low volume of trade between the two.
Even though there is a global perception that Nigerians are not
trustworthy particularly when it comes to business, Ayodeji stated that
given his country's population of 130 million, crooks would be found
just as in every society.
However, he appealed to all people interested in venturing into
business with Nigerians to go through his country's diplomatic missions
to get the profile of companies they would want to go into partnerships
with.
According to him, Namibia and Nigeria have what could be termed a
"go between". He said the trend that Namibian products going to Nigeria
and vice-versa always have to pass through South Africa has to some
extent hindered fast trade between the two countries.
"We prefer having 'direct' bi-lateral relations. We want Namibia to
export fish directly to Nigeria and this is an issue we raised with the
Ministry of Fisheries," he stated.
Two years ago, a proposal was made that a Joint Economic Commission
be hosted in Namibia in an effort to address and review pressing
bi-lateral trade issues but this has yet to be done.
Ayodeji has only been in Namibia for a year. Last year, he invited
35 Nigerians to train Namibians on how to grow cassava. The initiative
was regarded as viable given the fact that the crop can grow anywhere
and its by-products can be used in a variety of ways.
But disappointingly, the High Commissioner stated, little interest
has been shown, resulting in Nigeria taking the idea to China where it
has so far been well received.
Another trade area is salt, which is currently being imported from Cape Cross to Lagos.
Last year, the Nigerian government signed an agreement with the
local Ministry of Environment and Tourism to import between 40 and 45
wild animals such as springbok, giraffe and oryx. These were to be put
in paddocks for tourism purposes.
"The implementation was to take place during the catching period but the period has expired," he stated.
An effort to establish when the catching period is proved futile.
But despite these hurdles, the High Commissioner described relations
between the two countries as excellent and referred to the assistance
that his country rendered to Namibia during the liberation struggle.
During his tour of duty as High Commissioner, Ayodeji promises to
foster the spirit of NEPAD. He says because Nigeria and Ghana are the
only two West African countries with missions in Namibia, he would
strive to harmonise SADC and ECOWAS, the regional economic grouping in
West Africa.

Original_Search_Title: Resumption of River Traffic a Great Relief to the Public
Headline: Congo-Brazzaville: Congo-DRC: Resumption of River Traffic a Great Relief to the Public
Body_Text:
Brazzaville — Only five kilometres separate the ports of Brazzaville and Kinshasa
but a two-week suspension of river traffic between the two cities made
the distance seem infinite. To the relief of Brazzaville's 856,410
residents, authorities reopened the trade route across the River Congo
on 4 November.
Kinshasa, capital of the Democratic Republic of Congo (DRC), is the
main supplier of both food and non-food commodities to Brazzaville,
capital of the Republic of Congo (ROC), on the south bank of the mighty
River Congo. The traffic ban, which was imposed on 18 October,
triggered price hikes and shortages in the ROC's capital city.
River traffic between the two capitals was suspended after former
Zairean army troops occupied Brazzaville's riverside port, known as the
Beach, and demanded that they be allowed to return to the DRC.
The troops had fled Zaire to the neighbouring Republic of Congo in
May 1997 after Ugandan and Rwandan-backed guerrillas, lead by
Laurent-Desire Kabila, toppled Zairean leader Mobutu Sese Seko and
renamed the country Democratic Republic of Congo.
The ex-army troops, who were members of Mobutu's presidential
guards, had been trying to return home from ROC after what their
representative, Capt Ambroise Kusa, said were "several tough years in
exile". Authorities in both Congos, however, had blocked the river
crossings, saying there had been no accord between the two governments
for this movement.
After successful negotiations between the two countries, the first
group of soldier returned to Kinshasa on 1 November. Now that the issue
of repatriation has been completed, the trade route has also been
reopened.
Effect of port closure
The ban on river crossings had an immediate effect on Brazzaville's
residents. Some food prices in the market rose sharply: a 30-kg sack of
fufu flour, the cassava-based staple, rose from 18,000 francs CFA to
32,000 francs (US $32 to $57).
Port officials at the Beach reported that during the closure
smugglers tried to keep the lifeline open by supplying small quantities
of some of these commodities via small, motorised canoes but they could
not meet demand.
ROC imports vegetable oil, palm oil, soap, margarine, coffee,
alcoholic and nonalcoholic beverages, brown sugar, biscuits, salt and
salted fish from DRC. In addition, building materials like cement,
concrete, and steel reinforcements also come from the DRC.
Commerce at Brazzaville's Beach contributed "enormously to the
economic development", according to river transport agent Joachim
Lemba. Therefore a repture in traffic between the two cities could
squeeze of lot of people out of work.
"I live from day-to-day thanks to my odd job at the Beach. During
the two weeks [of traffic suspension], it was difficult to provide for
my family's daily needs," declared Arsène Elombila, a self-employed
port labourer.
"On average I make 12,000 francs CFA [$21.41] per day. This enables me to feed my wife and two children," he said.
Importance of the Beach
The Beach is always a hive of activity. Porters rush around loading
and off-loading goods. Under the shady mango trees that dot the port,
moneychangers do brisk business and turn a healthy profit, thanks to
the strength of the ROC's currency.
Merchants on opposite banks of the river transport their goods on
barges belonging to the national river transport companies of both
countries: la Coordination nationale des transports fluviaux (CNTF) in
the ROC and the Office national des transports (ONATRA) in the DRC. The
Beach also serves as a border post and is staffed by immigration,
customs and police agents.
Some 30 percent of ROC's imports come from DRC, and the latter
imports 5 percent to 10 percent of its wood, plywood and wooden
electricity poles from ROC. The ROC Customs Department said it lost 100
million francs ($178,450) in duty during the traffic closure.
Despite being a producer of crude petroleum, ROC depends on its
neighbour for much of its refined paraffin, oil, gasoline and jet fuel.
ROC's foreign trade office, the Direction générale du commerce
extérieur, has recorded 30 billion franc CFA ($53.54 million) of
imports from DRC since 2001. This amount represented about one-third of
the country's total global annual imports, making the resumption of
traffic a welcome relief to the public and the nation as a whole.
[ This report does not necessarily reflect the views of the United Nations ]

Original_Search_Title: The Quest for Food Security in Nigeria
Headline: Nigeria: The Quest for Food Security in Nigeria
Body_Text:
Lagos — At the time of independence, agriculture was the backbone of the
Nigerian economy. It continues to be the main source of employment in
the economy (accounting for about 70 per cent of the active labour
force). However, due the increased importance of oil for the economy,
the contribution of agriculture to national revenue declined from 80
per cent in 1960 to less than 10 per cent in 1990s.
Today, Nigeria faces challenges of meeting the food security
requirements needs of the people. For many years, the annual budget
allocation to agriculture was only about 1 per cent. The current
government has, however, began a massive reversal of the budgetary
allocation to the sector and expectations are that the effects will
begin to be felt in no distance time. In the mean time, agriculture is
increasingly becoming an unsustainable livelihood, especially for the
younger generation.
However, majority of Nigerians still depend on the sector for their
living. For instance, the majority of the people in the Middle Belt,
hitherto described as the 'Food basket' of the country depend on the
sector. In the past, a substantial portion of the country's
agricultural production, including cassava, cocoa, cotton, maize, yams,
groundnuts and rice, used to came from the zone. In the northern part
of Nigeria, cotton farming used to play a significant role in the
livelihoods of poor communities. This was when cotton farming and
processing was so popular that the area was branded Nigeria's Cotton
Belt. The groundnut pyramids have also disappeared.
The southern part of the country also made the list of one of the
world's largest producers of cocoa. So did various parts on Nigeria
have crops they were famous for producing. All these are now history.
There are different definitions of food security, all basically
bringing out the same concept. For instance, the World Bank defines
Food Security as "Access by all people at all times to enough food for
an active, healthy life." The FAO Committee on World Food Security sees
it as a situation where "All people at all times have both physical and
economic access to the basic food they need."
In the Agricultural Trade Development and Assistance Act of 1990
{P.L.480}, it is seen as "Access by all people at all times to
sufficient food and nutrition for a healthy and productive life."
For USAID Bureau for Africa, it sees Food security as a situation
"When all people at all times have access to sufficient food to meet
their dietary needs for a productive and healthy life."
Generally, there is near unanimity on the view that Food Security is
the ability of a country to feed its population in a sustainable manner.
The divergence of views arises as to what method a country may
employ in achieving FoodSecurity. These views can be summarized into
two main general groups, viz:
Those that support the issue of food security being addressed from
the comparative advantage criterion and those that advocate for the
strategic crops criterion. The comparative advantage criterion is an
outgrowth from the classical economic school of thought. Promoters
argue that a country should not necessarily be able to produce all the
food it needs to feed its people.
Rather, they propose that a country should focus on the products or
services that it has comparative advantages in the production and use
the resources it can generate to import food.
In the case of Nigeria, this would imply that the country should
focus on producing more oil and maximize all it can earn from this
product to import all the food it requires to feed itself. Even as we
debate and propose considerations of the centrality of the various
agricultural crops to achieving export adequacy and economic viability,
rural development and sustainable livelihoods and above all food
sovereignty, as a basis for determining how to pursue the goal of food
security, it bears to note that there are a number of other factors
that play significant roles in determining the achievement of this goal.
These include Agricultural policy, Trade policy, Land Tenureship, Access to inputs, etc and their implementation.
It is, therefore, imperative that the policy framework that
regulates these practices should have the input of all stakeholder
groups including farmers and all those involved in the food production
cycle and who would operate under the regime, they must be sound and
clearly understood by all, and should not contradict one another.
To the central question what can NGOs do? We dare say it is pretty
apparent to all what the role of the non-state actors should be within
this environment. As people and groups who work with farmers at the
grassroots and governments at various levels, it seems to us that our
role is clear and pretty cut out. Our first and foremost role is to
support and compliment the role of government in ensuring that it
delivers on its policy framework.
On the other hand, we are to interrogate farmers to determine their
challenges and felt needs, and to partner with them towards seeking out
solutions to these challenges. In addition, we are to provide
opportunities for farmers to access resources, enhance production and
access markets by organising themselves effectively.
Specifically, the role of the NGOs should include helping to make
agricultural policies more 'pro-poor' in the way they are formulated
and implemented; facilitating market access for the small-scale
agricultural producers; facilitating access to agricultural support
services and resources by small-scale farmers; strengthening the
capacities and confidence of producer groups to define their rights and
defend their interests; and promoting gender equity in agricultural
production and marketing.
At the other spectrum, as facilitators and catalysts, NGOs should
partner with the government in the areas of policy design and
formation, create a platform for the producers to participate in policy
decision-making process so as to ensure that their views are reflected
on policy documents and hence they can understand it and claim
ownership of the processes and outcomes which will ensure their full
cooperation towards their implementations.
Additionally, for NGOs such as Oxfam, which at the risk of being
immodest has abundant international clout and credibility, an added
value to influence the thinking, policy and practices of the
international community towards ensuring that their agricultural and
trade policies are not inimical to the food security of developing
nations such as Nigeria. It may be a tall order considering the
beggar-my-neighbour practices of the international community, but with
the partnership of all stakeholders in the South we can achieve success.
Oxfam for instance, has taken on these challenges by leading the
process and campaign to ensure fairness in the current negotiations on
the Economic Partnership Agreement (EPA), Common External Tariffs (CET)
and the World Trade Organizations (WTO).
However, experience has shown that the ability of non-state actors
to undertake these responsibilities is heavily depended or
circumscribed by the amount of cooperation they receive from the
enabling environment.
Governments, whether national, state or at local level, we believe
should embrace pro-poor policies. These not only help to legitimize
these policies, but encourages NGOs to partner with the government
towards ensuring their successful implementation. Again, government
should create avenues and platforms to harness the views of the poor in
the formulation of these policies. Experience has shown that despite
not having the advantage of formal education for many local people,
they however know what they want and given the opportunity they can
articulate their views.
- Demand that the WTO recognize the rights of the Nigerian
government and other developing countries to use measures it deem fit
to further strengthen their ability to protect their domestic producers.
These policies are inimical to the ability of countries such as Nigeria to realize food security need in their current forms.
Nartey, Country Programme Manager, Oxfam GB Nigeria, presented this
paper at a symposium to mark the World Food Day October 17, 2005.

Original_Search_Title: Dar to Sponsor Firms for Rwanda Trade Fair
Headline: Tanzania: Dar to Sponsor Firms for Rwanda Trade Fair
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Tanzania will sponsor compa- nies wishing to take part in the 8th Rwanda
International Trade Fair and the 7th Afro-Arab Trade Fair in Sudan.
This will be the first sponsorship under the country's new National
Strategy for Growth and Poverty Reduction (NSGPR).
The Board of External Trade (BET) said last week that through trade fair
participation, Tanzanian companies will have the opportunity to explore the
market potential for their products in Rwanda, Sudan and other countries in
the region.
BET will support stand rents, transportation and clearance of sample
products to and from Rwanda and Sudan - with a maximum capacity of 20
kilogrammes per company. It will also meet the administrative costs of the
stands.
Those who qualify for the sponsorship under the category are the small
and medium enterprises. Other companies will contribute Tsh500,000 ($476) to
cover space rent, air freight of 20 kg of samples for each exhibitor to and
from Rwanda and Khartoum, and administrative costs.
The Rwanda Fair will take place in Kigali from November 24 to December
5. The Afro-Arab Trade Fair will take place in Khartoum from November 23 to
December 1 this year.
By last week, 20 Tanzanian small and medium enterprises (SMEs) had
expressed interest in participating in the Rwanda Fair. Under the aegis of
the Small Scale Industrial Development Organisation. Three large-scale
companies and seven direct applicants have also applied to participate.
"The response is overwhelming. We may need to screen the companies so as
to reduce the number to allow few companies to cope with the tiny space at
the fair, acting director general of the Board of External Trade Cleophas
Ruhumbika told The EastAfrican last week.
This is the first time Tanzania has sponsored local companies to
participate at the Kigali Fair in 10 years. Mr Ruhumbika said that
previously large corporates such as the Tanzania Port Authority, Tanzania
Railways Corporation and the Tanzania International Container Terminal
Services have been making independent arrangements to participate at the
fair.
Recently, BET sponsored two women led firms to the 9th China
International Fair for Investments and Trade. Among the business deals that
were clinched at the fair was a lucrative contract to supply 5,000 tonnes of
cotton a month to China.
The Chinese were also interested in undertaking the construction of the
Kigamboni Bridge.
They also expressed interest in establishing cassava plantations and
cassava processing plants and animal feed factories.

Original_Search_Title: Don't Sell Those Inputs, Urges Manjata
Headline: Zambia: Don't Sell Those Inputs, Urges Manjata
Body_Text:
COMMUNITY Development and Social Services Minister Stephen
Manjata has urged the beneficiaries of the farming inputs they were
receiving from the Programme Against Malnutrition (PAM) not to sell
them.
The minister advised the beneficiaries to plant all the seed in order to avoid hunger next year.
Mr Manjata who flagged off the 2005-06 planting season at the
cooperative offices in Kaoma at the weekend, said using the seed
correctly would save many households from starvation next year.
The input packs to be distributed to the area comprised 298 tonnes
of maize, 60 tonnes of beans, 94.1 tonnes of groundnuts and 161 tonnes
of soya beans.
Others were 23 tonnes of cowpeas, 1,528 tonnes of urea and 1,603
tonnes of D compound fertiliser, 639 tonnes of agriculture lime and
10.3 million pieces of cassava cuttings.
The minister, however, noted that the inputs to be distributed to
the vulnerable people were not enough to cater for all the needy
households in the district.
The vulnerable could not afford to contribute the 50 per cent
counterpart contribution to the Fertiliser Support Programme (FSP).
Mr Manjata directed the District Food Security to ensure that the
targeted vulnerable but viable people benefited from the distribution
of the inputs.
"Councillors, community leaders are the eyes of Government and must
ensure that the guidelines for the distribution are followed and the
agreed quantities of inputs are given to the beneficiaries," he said.
Only the vulnerable but viable beneficiaries who include the
elderly, female and child -headed households and the terminally ill
would be covered under the programme.
Mr Manjata said Kaoma was the last district to flag off the
programme because of the wrangles that had surrounded the distribution
exercise.
At the same function, PAM executive director Drinah Nyirenda said
this year her organisation was distributing inputs to 33,000 vulnerable
households.
PAM was also targeting 20,000 households in the wetland areas throughout the country.
Dr Nyirenda said the wetlands area had the potential to produce enough food that could alleviate suffering in the country.

Original_Search_Title: The Quest for Food Security
Headline: Nigeria: The Quest for Food Security
Body_Text:
Lagos — A couple of days back, Nigeria joined other nations to mark the 2005
World Food Day. With the events marking the occasion over, it is important
for us to ask ourselves what really we mean when we talk about food security
and what needs to be done to ensure that this great country can meet the
food need of its population and secure food sovereignty.

Nigerian economy. It continues to be the main source of employment in the
economy (accounting for about 70 per cent of the active labour

requirements needs of the people. For many years, the annual budget

unsustainable livelihood, especially for the younger generation.

living. For instance, the majority of the people in the Middle Belt,
hitherto described as the 'Food basket' of the country depend on the
sector. In the past, a substantial portion of the country's agricultural

and healthy life."

groups, viz:

economic school of thought. Promoters argue that a country should not
necessarily be able to produce all the food it needs to feed its
people.
Rather, they propose that a country should focus on the products or
services that it has comparative advantages in the production and use the
resources it can generate to import food. In the case of Nigeria, this would
imply that the country should focus on producing more oil and maximize all
it can earn from this product to import all the food it requires to feed
itself.
However, this argument does not seem to address the issue of the source
and hence livelihood, food sovereignty and other issues. Because a
population may have all the food that it desires in the right quantity and
quality, but do not produce it themselves, may face the consequences of
shocks in the international macro economic and socio-political arenas.
In addition to the problem of lack of food sovereignty, there are a
plethora of challenges in the model. These include: Problems in the supply
chain can lead to hunger and crisis; ·Problems with a country's specialized
product in the international community adversely the country's ability to
meet up with its obligations;For a country such as Nigeria where there are
weak linkages between the various sectors, challenges still exists with
spreading the gains of exports earnings, hence the advantages of
specializing in a comparative product will not benefit all citizens
equitably. Again for a country such as Nigeria, the question of
sustainability of oil revenue becomes an issue; Jobs creation opportunities
are lost to other countries; As for the strategic crops criterion, promoters
summarily share the view that food security should be considered against the
backdrop of the extent of strategic importance of the crop(s) in question to
the country. The strategic importance here is measured in terms of its of
economic viability.
While this is a good start, especially as it avoids all the challenges
associated with the comparative advantage criterion, it appears it does not
go far enough in addressing livelihood and rural development issues.
At Oxfam we think that food security issues should be addressed from a
broader perspective which not only looks strictly from the perspective of a
population having as much food as it may need quantity and quality wise, but
also from the point of view of the source and hence food sovereignty.
That is inherently important for a country such as Nigeria where
agriculture is responsible for the livelihood sustainability of over 70 per
cent of the population living in rural areas. Again, given that the
government's current economic policies including NEEDS, SEEDS and LEEDS are
anchored, among others, on the creation of wealth and employment generation,
Oxfam considers that it should be of huge concerns to this government that
achieving Food Security should be also looked at not just from the Strategic
Crops Criterion or Export Adequacy and Economic Viability as some may term
it, but from the Rural Development strategy.
Even as we debate and propose considerations of the centrality of the
various agricultural crops to achieving export adequacy and economic

Nartey, is the Country Programme Manager, Oxfam GB Nigeria, Abuja

Original_Search_Title: Hope for an End to Seasons of Hunger
Headline: Malawi: Hope for an End to Seasons of Hunger
Body_Text:
Johannesburg — WITH stalks almost ready for harvesting towering above him, Edward Dobvu
walks through the lush green maize field that holds the promise of a better
future for Malawi.
Almost half of his 12 million fellow citizens face severe food
shortages; the "hunger season" that usually comes with the rainy months of
December to March has started early. But thanks to a simple irrigation
scheme, Dobvu has enough maize to feed his family.
The village elder is one of 176 farmers who are part of the Chitsukwa
irrigation project in the Nsanje district of southern Malawi, hardest hit by
the worst harvest in a decade and four consecutive years of drought.
All around him, hidden by the tall plants, men and women, husbands and
wives, mothers and children are working at treadle pumps in the 45°C
heat. Up and down they go -- and water flows through their fields from a
nearby lagoon, fed by the perennial Shire River.
The project, run by the World Food Programme and Irish aid organisation
Goal, with support from the European Union and the Malawian government, was
started in July as a coping mechanism for the impending crisis.
It was meant to be limited to 150 farmers, but the numbers have grown
and now there are 18ha under irrigation, with more canals being dug so the
project can be extended next year.
"This is something I never imagined. Before people did not understand
the idea of irrigation, but this has opened our eyes. Now we can survive,"
says Dobvu.
He remembers a time when food was abundant in the area. The rains came
without fail in October and farmers could feed their families for a year
from a single harvest, "but now things are very different and it is very
difficult".
Nowadays, most of the maize in the villages comes from traders who go on
forays into neighbouring Mozambiqe. But, as supplies dry up across the
region, the staple food is becoming scarcer -- and more expensive. In the
nearby dusty market, villagers are paying 15 kwacha for a large plastic cup
of maize, up from five kwacha in June.
The WFP has warned that about 12 million people across Southern African
will need food aid before the next harvest.
But Adam Amidu, another member of the irrigation project, has five 50kg
bags of maize in his home -- and he hasn't finished harvesting his first
crop yet, let alone reaped the benefits of at least two other harvests he
can produce thanks to the water in the canals.
"When I hear on the radio that other people are suffering from food
shortages, I feel really sorry for them. I am glad to be part of this
project, otherwise I would be scared of being hungry," he says.
Projects like this show how needless is the terrible situation the
country is in -- and point to a way it could be prevented.
"Malawi is a water-rich country. It has the lake and a number of
rivers. It should not be in the middle of a drought," says Amos Zaindi,
operations manager of the project.
Irrigation seems such an obvious solution that it is hard to believe
there are few such schemes in Malawi, and no state-owned dams. While 80% of
Malawians are subsistence farmers, only 1% of arable land is under
irrigation -- and most of this is for cash crops such as sugar and tea.
But, as Zaindi points out, up until the last two decades or so, there
was no need for irrigation as the rains were plentiful.
But it is not only drought and the reliance on rain-fed agriculture that
is the cause of the deepening food insecurity in the country. Yields have
dropped significantly as the soil has been depleted of nutrients, making
farmers dependent on fertilisers. Population growth, which has almost
doubled in the past decade, has put further pressure on land, and an
HIV/Aids infection rate of 15% to 20% has also had a negative effect on
people's ability to feed themselves.
In addition, there is a deep-seated tradition, dating back to the years
of Life President Hastings Kamuzu Banda, that views maize as a kind of
"super food" -- when other crops such as cassava or sweet potatoes may be
more suitable.
All this has made households increasingly reliant on having to buy maize
to fulfil their food needs, and put them at the mercy of volatile market
prices. They are forced to find what little casual work they can to earn an
income, and this means spending less time in their own fields.
Malawi is without doubt among Southern Africa's worst-off countries. A
recent USAID-commissioned report says Malawi is among the most food-insecure
countries in the world.
The effect of this is frightening, with almost 50% of children under the
age of five stunted as a result of chronic malnutrition.
Food security is the biggest challenge facing Malawi, says Randson
Mwadiwa, a top official in the Ministry of Agriculture. "There is the
realisation that we need to intensify irrigation. We are moving full
throttle in this regard," he says.
Mwadiwa says a $43-million agreement has been signed with the World Bank
and International Fund for Agricultural Development to rehabilitate the few
existing, but defunct, irrigation schemes. The government is also
encouraging small-scale irrigation by providing farmers with treadle or
mechanised pumps and are considering other water-conservation methods, such
as building village dams.
"We believe that if we are able to have systems on up to 50% of the
irrigable land, then we should be able to address much of the food-security
problems," he says.
But Undule Mwakasungula of Lilongwe's Centre for Human Rights and
Rehabilitation says the government must take responsibility for not driving
donor involvement.
"The crisis is worse than four years ago. That was supposed to have been
a lesson for us, but why are we in this crisis again if the government is
doing something? We are not moving ahead. Malawi is going backwards," he
said.
Food security is a deeply politicised issue in Malawi and has been used
to win votes. The previous government of Bakili Muluzi, who ushered in
multi-party democracy in 1994, ran programmes handing out free seed and
fertiliser, which have been replaced with a system of subsidies.
It was also on Muluzi's watch that the strategic grain reserve was sold
off in 2000, on the advice of the IMF. The move is regarded as highly
controversial and has implicated politicians in profiteering.
Analysts say that Bingu wa Mutharika, who was elected president last
year, has the will and the vision to address the food situation and improve
the running of the country -- but he has had no time to implement his agenda
because of political infighting.
"It is sad that parliament is not addressing national issues," says
Mwakasungula
Raniero Leto of the EU's rural development and natural resources section
says it would take only two years to make Malawi food secure.
For farmers such as Dobvu and Amidu, it has taken only three months to
reach a stage that makes it unlikely they or their families will go hungry
again.

Original_Search_Title: Chinese to Boost Rice Production
Headline: Cameroon: Chinese to Boost Rice Production
Body_Text:
Prime Minister Ephraim Inoni yesterday granted an audience to the chairman of the Henan International Chamber of Commerce, Zhang Shiling.
The Chairman of the Henan International Chamber of Commerce, who doubles as the Board Chairman of the Kaifeng Sida Agricultural Productions Equipment Co, Ldt , Zhang Shiling is on a study mission in Cameroon to explore investment opportunities in rice production and cassava transformation. This was the subject of discussion during a one-hour audience the Prime Minister and Head of Government, Ephraim Inoni granted to the Chinese delegation yesterday, November 9 at the Star Building.
At the end of the audience, Zhang Shiling told the press that he was in Cameroon for the first time. He said the audience enabled him to know the country and gave him a green light in his exploration mission. According to Zhang Shiling rice production and cassava transformation for exportation are two important projects that can help enrich Cameroon. With the green light already given, the Chinese investor declared that they will start feasibility studies today at Nanga Eboko to learn about the temperature and other natural conditions. It is only after the studies that they will know the rice species that can do well in the area, he said.
The Minister of Agriculture and Rural Development, Clobert Tchatat accompanied Zhang Shiling to the Prime Minister's Office for the audience.

Original_Search_Title: Kaduna to Get $5m Grant From ADF
Headline: Nigeria: Kaduna to Get $5m Grant From ADF
Body_Text:
Kaduna — THE Kadu na State government is to receive a five million dollars grant
from the African Development Foundation (ADF), an agency of the American
government to assist farmers and small scale businesses in the state over a
five year period.
Signing the Memorandum of Understanding (MoU) at the Washington DC
headquarters of the organisation, Kaduna State governor, Alhaji Ahmed
Mohammed Makarfi said that the state government chose agriculture because of
its potentials of reducing poverty and idleness, and create employment for
the youths. He said that with the signing of the MoU, the state government
was now ready to tackle incidents of poverty and unemployment in the state
head-on.
Makarfi said that the government has concluded plans to enourage
agriculture which will in turn discourage idleness and create employment for
the youth. He said that this effoprt will impact positively on the economic
and financial capacity of the people with a multiplier effect on other
sectors such as health, education, etc, he said, could hardly be
quantified.
According to the governor, the Kaduna State Government truly appreciated
the kind gesture of the African Development Foundation and promised that the
opportunities presented by this would be maximally exploited by the state to
ensure a marked improvement in the living conditions of the people. The
state government he said would not fail in the discharge of its obligations
as per the understanding, even as efforts would also be intensified in the
provisions of infrastructure like access roads and power to complement areas
to be taken care of by the agreement.
Makarfi explained that his government's decision to make agriculture and
agro-allied activities the focal point of this deal with the ADF was borne
out of the desire to ensure that farming which was the major occupation of
the people regained its lost glory, since their future depended heavily on
it.
"Kaduna State government is poised to confront poverty by encouraging a
massive return to agriculture, which in turn would eliminate idleness by
generating employment opportunities for the youths.
"With the right kind of assistance and exposure, farmers in the state
would in a very good position to not only feed the country but also earn
foreign exchange for the country through the export of such crops as
cassava, rice, maize, as well as ginger of which it is the largest producer
in the country", he said. He expressed the readiness of the government to
ensure that the spirit of the understanding was implemented to the letter,
even as he expressed the hope that Kaduna State Government and ADF would in
no distant future partner again in some other sectors. In his remark, the
President of the African Development Foundation, MR. Nathaniel Fields, said
that said that the decision of the organisation to extend such assistance to
the Kaduna state government was based on reports reaching them on the
efforts of the Kaduna state government at developoing the state and provide
qualitative leadership. This, he said was in addition to the report of the
on-the-spot-assessment carried out by officials of the Foundation who were
in Kaduna recently. "What the ADF team saw in Kaduna, as well as discussions
held with the state government left indelible impression on our minds on the
serious efforts going on to change the face of agriculture and small and
medium enterprises in the state. "This was what strengthened their resolve
to partner with the state in order to improve the living conditions of the
people as well as encourage other states to follow suit. He commended
Governor Ahmed Makarfi"s dogged pursuit of revolutionising agriculture in
Kaduna State; a situation that he said had been brought out starkly by the
governor being the first to insist on signing an agreement with the
Foundation solely on agriculture and agro-allied matters. He gave an
assurance that the ADF will faithfully discharge its part of the bargain,
while also expressing the hope that its presence would generate tremendous
positive interest in the state. The signing of this understanding was the
culmination of efforts initiated by Governor Makarfi when he visited the
Headquarters of the African Development Foundation in August this year
soliciting for a partnership that would assist in enhancing both the
capacity as well as the economic well being of farmers in the state.

Original_Search_Title: 'My Mission is to Empower Our People'
Headline: Nigeria: 'My Mission is to Empower Our People'
Body_Text:
Lagos — Mrs. Bolanle Richard-Bruce is an Ekiti State House of Assembly
aspirant on the platform of the Alliance for Democracy (AD). Richard-Bruce
who seeks to represent Irepodun Constituency in the state spoke with Sheriff
Balogun on governance in Ekiti, her ambition and AD's move to regain power
in 2007
What is your comment about the recent impeachment of the former
deputy governor of Ekiti State, Chief Abiodun Aluko?
I do not actually know the real story because you and I have been
reading different stories on the pages of newspaper. So, whatever must have
been behind the suspension and final impeachment of Aluko like they say in
(Peoples Democratic Party) PDP, its a family affair. Facts will be sorted
out at the right time.
What is your general assessment of Governor Ayo Fayose's
administration when compared with the government of his predecessor, Chief
Niyi Adebayo?
I go home (Ekiti) very often. So I don't belong to the class of people
who who boast on the television or on the pages of newspapers that they
don't visit their home state, but rather stay in Lagos and are
complaining. The real truth is that there is nothing on ground to show that
there is any government in Ekiti State. For how many years now has he
(Fayose) been in office? At least, he had come into power since 2003. Two
years is gone now, yet there's nothing to show for it. All those things I
watched in Ekiti transformation series on the television, whenever I go
home, I expect to see those things, but I cannot see them. The roads are
bad. And the so-called poultry projects are empty. There are no fowls in the
poultry. They only brought chicks from Ibadan to Ekiti for a particular
purpose and at the end of that event, they took them back to Ibadan.
You are aspiring to be a lawmaker in the state house of assembly. But
how do you think you can cope, bearing in mind that majority of lawmakers
are usually men?
That is so fantastic! I've always been like that, if you know my
antecedents. I've always been very comfortable among men. My husband even
praises me for that ability. I cannot begin to shiver or begin to fear
because I will be working among men. I'm a human being. I'm like you and I'm
capable by the grace of God. So, I don't have any problem about that.
Well, considering the power of incumbency, how do you think your
party will confront the PDP in 2007?
The truth of the matter is that we are conscious of this factor right
now. We are happy and we thank God that the PDP government in Ekiti State
has failed our people. Go to Ekiti and see that everybody in the state is
saying 'no more of Fayose, no more of PDP'. And so all we need to do in AD
is to put our house order and ensure that we settle every rancour that is
within the party. Let's settle our differences amicably especially at the
national level because I know that as at now, we have two factions that are
laying claims to leadership. Let's sort this thing out and then come
together and work as a family. I know if we can do that, we'll wrestle power
from PDP in Ekiti State and I don't even have any doubt that we'll not claim
the South-west.
As a woman in politics, how do you think you can offer adequate
representation to the people?
By the grace of God, I actually plan to do so. I will and God will help
me to make an impact because again, I 've found out that a lot of our people
want to be involved in government activities. They make a lot of promises,
but when they get there, they forget our people. But God will help me. When
I get there, I will really want to make an impact and like I said, I want to
focus on the youths and women.
The reason is because if you train a woman, you're training a whole
nation. Again, if you neglect the youth, then the future has perished. So
this two sets of people are actually very sensitive. The youth and women are
the people I'm focusing on. We need to empower our youths. We also need to
empower our women so that the women can support the family and when the
family is fully supported, the man himself would have a respite and before
you know it there will be peace everywhere. Also, when the youths are
gainfully employed, the one that is into any vocation has the tool to work
with. The one that is also into formal education also has the necessary tool
and after leaving the school, he will be gainfully employed. Of course, at
the end of the day, everybody will have peace of mind and the society will
be a better place to live in. So, these are the categories of people that I
'll be focusing on
Besides the concentration on women and youths, can you state in clear
terms some other goals you want to pursue?
Plenty. A lot of things are in stock. And like I told my people the last
time I went to Ekiti, I'm not going to make any empty promises or boast of
anything that I know I can never fulfil. I told them that fact and I
promised them some other things that will be surely achieved and
specifically, I intend to assist students in my constituency in the areas of
basic and social amenities.
If you go to my town, as big as it is today, there is no community
centre there, yet we have a representative in the House of Assembly. That's
about the least you can think of. There is absolutely nothing like that. My
town today is not yet connected to any telecommunications network. These are
simple and achievable things that at least, one can pursue and I also intend
to spearhead breast cancer enlightenment programme among our women.
I intend to do that because I find out that breast cancer kills more
than HIV AIDS. So we need to do this. We also need to encourage the active
participation of women in politics in our constituency. When I I announced
my intention during my last visit to my nativity, the women were jubilating
that finally, a lady is coming out because they have been waiting for this
for long. We have been so much intimidated that no woman had ever come out
to say I want to go for an elective post.
You must have heard from Fayose that Ekiti State is number 35 on the
(West African Examinations Council) WAEC list, that is, last but one among
the sates of the federation in terms of performance. And so, we sat down and
said what would have been the cause? And we found out that finance has been
a major problem. Then it was decided that WAEC and (National Examinations
Council) NECO fees would be promptly paid for every student preparing to
write WAEC and NECO. How much is the fees? But of course, we will recognise
excellence and hardwork through scholarships and payment of WAEC and NECO
fees.
Then we need to give bursary awards to all our students in tertiary
institutions. Of course, we are in (Information and Computer Technology) ICT
world now and there is no single cyber cafe in my village. So, there is so
much work to do, my brother and so we are going to be technology driven as
well.
We'll go home and put ICT world on ground and at least let our people
have the knowledge of information technology. We need to assist our
people. There's so much work to do; even subsistence farming is no longer in
practice in my village and so we need to empower our women and assist them
with micro-economic skills and maybe offer farming assistance, in terms of
loans that can encourage farming. It may not be on a large scale but at
least subsistence farming that can keep the body and the soul together. I
mean something they can do to enable them support the family so that the
burden will not be too much for the man. The woman can also bring in some
money.
I believe that if the women at home are also empowered economically, a
lot will be achieved. I give you an instance. With ordinary N2,500, I spoke
to a woman who told me that if she had N2,500, she would begin to sell
garri. She said the N2,500 will be enough for her to buy 60 tubers of
cassava and she will start from there, do all the necessary things that need
be done and prepare the garri. She added that at the end of the day, she
would make N8,000 from the money. When she said this, I was grieved in my
spirit and I said l'ook at that.' Give that woman N10,000, will she not be
economically empowered? So like I said, I haven't made any promise that I
won't be able to fulfil.

Original_Search_Title: 400 Families Team Up Against Hunger
Headline: Kenya: 400 Families Team Up Against Hunger
Body_Text:
Nairobi — More than 400 families have teamed up to fight hunger.
After going hungry for many years, hundreds of villagers from the arid
Matungulu Division in Machakos District have started an irrigation
project.
The families in a remote part of the division will use the Athi River
waters to improve food security.
The desperate farmers have spent seven months digging the 7.2 kilometre
Kayaata canal, which will irrigate 320 acres.
A government engineer offers advise to the villagers who rely on hoes
and other simple tools.
Both the Government and the International Fund for Agricultural
Development (Ifad) are funding work on the Kayaata Amua Water Systems
Irrigation Project to the tune of Sh18 million.
The small scale farmers hope to improve food production and boost their
farm incomes.
According to Ifad project coordinator David Nyamai, farmers will be
required to meet Eurep-Gap - a farming standard that would allow them access
into the international market.
Mr Nyamai said the Ministry of Agriculture was already helping the
farmers enter into contracts with exporters, giving them information on
world prices, training them on better farming and book-keeping, and building
produce collection sheds, latrines and water points.
Agricultural extension officers, he said, are also helping the farmers
with crop processing to add value, and training them in horticulture.
Mr Nyamai said the canal would also supply water for domestic use.
A loan proposal for 170 farmers was sealed in January with Cooperative
Bank of Kenya. Qualified savings and credit cooperative (sacco) members have
been trained by the Ministry of Cooperatives and the bank on credit
management.
On sharing water, currently managed by Kayaata Farmers Cooperative
Society (KFCS), Mr Nyamai said an agreement on the sharing and use of a
common water permit had already been signed between the KFCS and the sacco,
giving farmers access to the water.
Project chairman Paul Mutungi said, the Kaayata Amua Systems Water
Irrigation Project is a sacco with 213 members and 166 share holders and was
registered in 2003. It has four sub-committees: water users; loans;
production and marketing; and environment.
Mr Mutungi said sacco members had already been trained on how to carry
out simple repairs on the 12.5 kilometre Mukaa Mukuu-Kayaata access road,
which farmers will use to ferry farm produce to various markets.
Mr Nyamai noted that the farmers were planting tomatoes, onions, French
beans and okra. And out of the potential 75 acres, 60 acres were already
under horticulture, he said. The sacco had already been given a contract by
Frigoken Company that will buy their French beans, he said.
Another exporter, Equator Product Limited, had shown interest in buying
their chillies, Mr Nyamai said.
He said farmers had also been given 100 TC bananas and suckers, 4,464
kilograms of seeds and 28,000 seed pieces of root crops from the Kenya
Agricultural Research Institute and Thika agricultural research stations,
adding that the farmers' annual yields had increased. He said that
marketable farm yields by the sacco were now two tons per week, from 100
kilos.
Mr Nyamai said the project emphasised post-harvest management and
compliance with the Eurep-gap farming standards, hence the training on seed
production, seed banking and post-harvest management. A seed bank, he said,
had already been put up in the area.
He said besides encouraging farmers to grow traditional foods like
cassava, sweet potatoes, millet, sorghum, pigeon peas, cow peas and
pumpkins, the farmers and farmer groups were also being advised to set up
other income generating activities.
Mr Mutungi said the major problems facing them in horticultural
production is marketing and farm loans. (KNA)

Original_Search_Title: Uganda Research Centres Should Serve the People
Headline: Uganda Research Centres Should Serve the People
Body_Text:
Kampala — A look at Ugandan institutions commonly known as "powerhouses" shows a
missing link between them and the surrounding communities.
It is worth noting that over time, Uganda has invested in institutions
of higher learning and research.
For more than two decades now, institutions such as the National
Agricultural Research Institute Kawanda, Namulonge Agricultural Research
Institute, National Agricultural Institute, Busitema Agricultural College,
Makerere University Agricultural Research Centre at Kabanyolo, Nyabea
Forestry Research Centre and Bukalasa Agricultural College have been
involved in research to help improve the agricultural productivity in the
country.
Mpologoma banana species, composite maize and mosaic resistant cassava
are some of the species that have been developed.
However, a key question is; has this wealth of knowledge from the
"powerhouses" contributed to transforming the livelihoods of the people in
these areas?
The answer could be affirmative, but it is apparent that there seems to
be a deep gulf between these institutions and the surrounding communities
with regard to information transfer.
In a situation where poverty levels are high and illiteracy levels
soaring, one wonders why such communities cannot tap the relatively "free
knowledge" from the "powerhouses" to start similar activities, especially
regarding improving agricultural productivity.
The way the information generated is disseminated seems to have been a
problem. Information generation and dissemination should be aimed at
improving the usability of the information.
Increase in access and use of the generated information will help raise
innovation and productivity of the beneficiaries.

Original_Search_Title: Hungry Villagers Forced to Compete With Wild Animals for Food
Headline: Zambia: Hungry Villagers Forced to Compete With Wild Animals for Food
Body_Text:
Livingstone — Late into Zambia's long, dry season, the Victoria Falls are not at their
most spectacular. Undeterred, bus loads of tourists still stop here daily to
view the torrent. Most are unaware that a scarcity of water in the
surrounding area has driven many rural communities to the brink of
starvation.
Poor rains early in the year saw the total failure of most crops in
southern Zambia. As well as decimating the livelihoods of the area's
predominantly subsistence farmers, the drought has resulted in a severe
shortage of maize, the country's staple food. Maize prices have since
escalated to way beyond the means of most Zambians, the majority of whom
live on less than a dollar a day.
In the village of Koma, in the nearby district of Kazungula, families
have long since consumed any surpluses remaining from last year's
harvest. Even the seeds that would normally be set aside to ensure next
year's harvest have been eaten. Wild fruits that would usually supplement
the villagers' diet are now their main food source.
"There's serious hunger here," said Koma resident, Lackson
Siamukapi. "We're competing with monkeys and baboons for the fruit and by
the end of November these fruits will be finished."
Desperate to fill their stomachs, children sometimes eat the
indigestible seeds of the fruits. Diarrhoea is common and malnutrition is
increasingly a fact of life. But the children of Koma are luckier than
many. Through a World Food Programme (WFP) sponsored school feeding scheme,
they at least receive a daily portion of nutrient-enriched porridge.
A few kilometres away in Chazanga village, there has so far been no
outside assistance and villagers are increasingly fearful for their
future. The surrounding trees have been stripped of fruit and villagers must
now walk long distances to forage for more. They too have consumed the maize
seeds that should have been planted ahead of this month's expected
rains.
"Selling our livestock was our main hope of raising money for seeds but
now most have died from disease," said village headman, Rodwell Sidiwa.
Government allocations of seed and subsidised fertiliser for Kazungula
have been cut by more than half since last year, leaving district
agriculture and cooperatives official, Katupa Chongo, with the task of
dividing just 150 packs of fertiliser between 3,000 farmers in the
area. Pleas for additional rations, he says, have fallen on deaf ears.
"The government gave out a certain amount of money that's been
exhausted," Chongo told IRIN. "The government prefers to give fertiliser to
districts that aren't drought prone like Kazungula. The poor will remain
poor."
To compensate for the shortfall, international NGO, CARE, along with the
UN's Food and Agriculture Organization plan to deliver seed packets to some
of the more vulnerable households this month.
But even the combination of seeds and rain may not be enough to prevent
the cycle of hunger from repeating itself next year. A recent outbreak of
disease has wiped out most of the cattle needed to plough the fields and
hand cultivation will mean a smaller than normal harvest. Disease has also
killed most of the pigs and chickens that had not already been sold to raise
money for food.
There are no commercial farms in the area that could provide piecework
and most of the tourism jobs in the nearby town of Livingstone go to
residents. The villagers report that the sale of wild fruits and brooms made
of grass are their only source of income but that both the fruit and the
grass are running out.
"There's nothing unless someone assists us," said Sidiwa, the Chazanga
village headman.
In this drought-prone area of Zambia, adequate assistance has been
forthcoming during previous food shortages. But WFP coordinator for the
Livingstone area, Peter Otto, fears this year may be different.
Otto estimates that the government's recent appeal to the international
community has come too late to fully mobilise the increased relief efforts
needed in the months between now and the April harvest.
That delay was despite early warning of the growing problem.
After the failure of this year's maize crops due to lack of rain, many
rural households in southern Zambia are staving off hunger with wild fruits
such as these.
The Disaster Management and Mitigation Unit, a government agency,
alerted the cabinet in July that an estimated 1.2 million people would need
food relief, but it took a further three months for the appeal to be
made.
The government also waited several months to heed the recommendations of
organisations like the US-funded Famine Early Warning Systems Network (FEWS
NET) to lift the 15 percent duty on imported maize. By the time the duty was
finally abolished last month, traders were faced with much higher maize
prices.
Both imports and distribution of food aid have been further hampered by
