Delta region. Wake up call! I hereby congratulate 'EFCC' for taking the bull
by the horn.
Corruption has impoverished our people and most politicians in 'Creek
Haven' don't hear the masses cry from 'Creek Hell'. I urge members of Ijaw
Youth Council to embrace the legal option to their legitimate demands and
never be afraid to ask questions about accountability and transparency in
government. Other minorities in Niger Delta region look up to Ijaw
elders/leaders for leadership and the Ijaw have failed woefully. Bayelsa
State PDP executives and elders gave us a mini 'late Mobutu Sese Seko' of
Congo style of government. Blind loyalty and political cultism!
Ijaw should allow the courts of Britain and Nigeria to expose all the
bad apples in Government House.
This is a good opportunity for the Ijaw to begin from ground zero with
new set of dedicated leaders to rebuild our social, economic and political
infrastructure. Ijaw don't have free pipe borne water, even though the State
has numerous rivers/water and regular flooding. Yenagoa has become a
sleeping rural town with sputtering economy because the economic fuel was
diverted to US/UK where the thieves have friends and home. The Presidency
(Obasanjo) should deliver a radio and televised message to the people
Bayelsa State about his anti-corruption campaign. The Presidency has an
obligation to explain why his anti-corruption crusade was not specifically
directed at Governor Alamieyeseigha or other Ijaw people and the exercise
has no political undertone as perceived in segments of public opinion in
Bayelsa State.
Ironically, the same crooks conspired and shipped 25 years old Rolls
Royce turbine engine (from Gov Okilo's era) to UK several times for repairs
as Ijaw folks lived in total darkness till today. This is 21st century bold
looting of public funds and poor management of public funds. Corruption! How
much did Bayelsa State Electricity Board spend on the gas turbine repairs in
London?  Politicians/Public Officials used the people's desire for
electricity to siphon money to foreign banks while our people, live in
abject poverty are unbelievable. Ijaw elders/leaders shouldn't condone
illegality or corruption in the public sector if they aspire to reduce
poverty and develop Niger Delta region.
Corruption has affected Bayelsa State education policy that allow greedy
politicians have undermined the youth desire to gain management employment
opportunities in the Oil industry. Bayelsa State is host to CHEVRON and
SHELL, two of the biggest oil companies in the world. In the quest for
providing qualified candidates for the oil and gas industry, Bayelsa State
Scholarship Board/DSP sent IJAW students to Belarus/Ukraine/Cameroon and
other remote areas of the world for better education to compete with other
candidates from other regions who studied in UK, Germany, USA, Canada for
management positions. Ijaw elders/leaders don't understand our desires and
the global implications of crude oil/gas world markets and regional
politics.
If the Governor DSP Alamieyeseigha, could send his children/wife/himself
to study in USA/UK, why should Ijaw leaders deny our youths (young minds)
similar education opportunities in western countries that have massive
investments in Niger Delta Ijaw land? Imagine an Ijaw man or woman learning
a second language before graduate program and do you think the hiring
manager at Chevron or SHELL would give them the opportunity in this
competitive labor market. I think some Ijaw Elders/Leaders are confused
about fighting corruption and real investment returns.
I pray someday Ijaw women would wake up to challenge those arrogant
crooks in Ijaw land. Ijaw leaders/elders always blame the FG or someone else
for their problems. Therefore, I challenge so-called Ijaw elders/leaders to
look themselves in the mirror! Please, define your objectives strategy;
develop political strategy and practise inclusive democracy. Bayelsa State
Governor Alamieseigha's problem has nothing to do with resource control. If
Nigeria CJ Uwias (Judiciary) couldn't do it, the British would help. We are
fighting corruption together.
This is wickedness! Selfishness! Gov Alamieyeseigha is no prophet! Ijaw
political leaders/Ijaw national congress leaders failed to realize that
labourers don't make hiring decisions in the oil industry. Corporate
Managers do! Bayelsa State scholarship board should be scrapped! Why should
Governor-General's children be granted exclusive right to study in USA and
UK? Why should an Ijaw/Ikwerre/Ogoni person or other minorities in Niger
Delta region be forced to attend non-English University at this time of
rapid development period?
Most Ijaw politicians/State Assemblymen/Federal Legislators are
intimidated or afraid of Governor-General. They see the giant of Ijaw land
in their dreams and it has become a nightmare. It's sad to see Ijaw
elders/politicians patrolling Aso Rock (Abuja) with hat in hand to beg
President Obasanjo to intervene. How do you fight corruption that has
impoverished Ijaw land? It's a shame for Ijaw leaders to go that low to
rescue a thief or a jailbird. It's true! It happened in London. Busted!
Busted!!
Also, Ijaw elders or leaders are not democratically elected therefore
they don't articulate geo-political nature of our struggle. Ijaw
elders/politicians don't understand the global dimensions of oil
politics. Do they condone corruption? Yes, I believe so. How do they justify
their blind loyalties? We have an oppressive culture and male class
dominance in Ijaw land regardless of the persons' ability to lead. It's
common practice in African cultures. It's about time Ijaw women stood up to
fight for social and economic change peacefully. For Asari Dokubo's
detention, Nigeria National Security Advisor, Gen Aliyu Gusau (rtd), is
flexing his muscles to show the multi-national oil companies that he can
crack down on youth restiveness in Niger Delta region. My fellow Ijaw
people, IYC in particular, NSA Gen. Aliyu Gusau (rtd), has more power in
Nigeria than President Obasanjo!
This is a carrot and stick approach strategy adopted by Gen Gusau. NDDC
provides the carrot for cassava cultivation and soccer programmes for the
youths and Gen Aliyu Gusau adopts the military/security/legal option of
arrest and detention. NDDC is unfocused. NDDC is now NFA of Niger
Delta. Ijaw youths would rather enroll in fully equipped vocational
institutions in their LG areas and learn employable skills (electrical,
mechanical engineering, weldering, driving, building, marine tech, catering)
for the oil/gas industry. Our youth should have choices in their careers or
what they want to do? If NDDC goes in agriculture or farming, then what's
the role of Ministry of Agriculture? Why do we spend so much on Ministry of
Agriculture with zero return? Our youths should realize that the Nigerian
military has been trained and equipped by the oil/gas companies through
foreign military aid provided by USA/EU countries to protect their
investment.
Bayelsa State Governor Alamieyeseigha busted in London. Something is
missing from the front pages of various media publications. Is he corrupt?
Did he steal the money? Governor earns $74,000 per year and within 6 years
period our beloved Governor purchased Â£10 million properties in UK
excluding his properties in USA and parts of
Nigeria. While Ijaw people in Yenagoa/Port Harcourt and other rural
areas don't have clean pipe-borne drinking water, no sewage treatment plants
for public health sake (#1 public health issue), no garbage disposal policy
and poor marine transportation system.
Forget immunity and focus on the core issue of corruption and looting of
public funds thereby denying ordinary people opportunities for better
tomorrow. Monthly Federal Government oil/gas revenue allocation to the
States belongs to very indigene of individual States. There is no exclusive
clause in the constitution that gives the executive branch or other tiers
Government officials and family members the sole right to better life or
community wealth.
Why didn't the Governor buy the UK properties in the name of Bayelsa
State Government. If Gov Alamieyeseigha loves Bayelsa State, why didn't the
Governor-General of Ijaw land use his (Solomon & Peters Company)
resources to build massive (housing) apartment complexes, shopping centres,
commercial buildings for Federal Government to rent, fully equipped
(computers) vocational institutions in Bayelsa State. There are more rotten
apples in all PDP installed Governors/executives in the region and other
parts of Nigeria. Rivers State is not better either except the looters have
not been caught. Port Harcourt, the Garden City, is now filled with garbage,
potholes, pollution and traffic congestion.
It has been praises and blind loyalty without asking the politicians why
they don't have pipe borne water, sewage treatment plants, marine
transportation systems within Port Harcourt city. For example, how long does
it take an average person to travel from Borokiri or Mid Town to Rumumasi or
Mile 3 between 10am - 8pm? What if river transport/water taxi from
Town/Creek road to Diobu/AGIP/Wimpy waterside ease the congestion? Why do we
have bad roads year after year? From Diobu to GRA, the roads are terrible
during rainy/dry seasons.
For housing, the growing ghettos of PH where families have raised
children for the past 20 years or more are deplorable.

Original_Search_Title: Corporate Report: Transforming Ogun State From Civil Service to Industrial, How Possible?
Headline: Nigeria: Corporate Report: Transforming Ogun State From Civil Service to Industrial, How Possible?
Body_Text:
Ogun State is probably Nigeria's most peculiar state that many including the indegenes of the state take for granted. The state is the home of chief Obafemi Awolowo, Kashimawo Abiola, Dr. Tai Solarin, Professor Wole Soyinka, Prince Ajibola, Chief Earnest Shonekan, Chief Simeon Adebo, Chief Olusegun Obasanjo and Fela Anikulapo Kuti and many more. These men have made their marks and many are very much around. To rule over such a state the man trusted with such responsibility had better be careful and watch his actions and what he says in public. It is this state that Otunba Gbenga Daniel has been called upon to administer in the first instance for four years.
To the avid watchers of trends in Ogun state professionals abound like animals and birds. They have distinguishable attributes that constantly reminds Otunba Gbenga Daniel that his footsteps are being watched always. In the state, administrators are around like squirrels,calculating, spontaneous and tricky. Also in the state politicians are like elephants, wanting to dominate and possess. For the human rights activist, like parrots, always cautious and watchful. Bring all these together, Ogun state could be likened to a clustering of termites-busy, evaluating and sometimes tearing down Governor Daniels programme .
Ogun state has a collection of all these characters and for a people who were once blessed with a disciplined and visionary leader like Awolowo, Ogun state citizens seem restive and yearning for results.
This seem to be the driving force behind Gbena Daniels desire to make a difference. The Governor at the one day Presidential retreat on SME held in Abeaokuta told the president and participants at the forum that his vision is to transform the state from a civil service state to an industrial one. A tall dream critics of his administration are quick to say. He stated "Our vision is to broaden the economic base of Ogun state, under an innovative and effective government that is business friendly, people oriented and responsive to stakeholders' needs. To build a state of happy people and thriving business living in peace, prosperity and dignity within the context of one Nigeria".
To have a vision is one thing to translate it into concrete terms beneficial to the citizens is yet another and most times the bane of the Nigeria nation. But governor Gbenga Daniel seems to have an idea of what it takes to achieve his lofty dreams for Ogun state. He says his strategy is private sector led development in which the government will put the needed basic infrastructure on ground and ask the private sector to invest in such areas. To jump start its development plan, the Ogun state government single handedly dualised the Shagamu Abeakuta road without waiting for the federal government to carry out the function being a federal road. Today, the road is not only passable but with street lights right from the Shagamu end of the road to Abeokuta The state government has come to the realisation that basic infrastructural development are needed as incentive to attract private sector participation in the goal that the state government has set for itself.
One of such lofty goals is the proposed Cargo Terminal Airport that will serve as a central hub for the development of a new industrial town in the Sagamu/Lagos-Ibadan expressway axis of the State. The concept is akin to how chief Awolowo developed the Ikeja industrial estate by putting basic infrastructure which attracted private sector operators to the estate. The Airport, though Government initiative, is to be established as a commercially viable private sector driven project.
The intention of the state government is to use the available resources to get the best results and put the most appropriate structure in place within the quickest possible time.
According to Wale Sanni, Technical Assistant to the economic Adviser to the Governor, 10,200 hactres of land has been cleared for the take off of the first phase of the project, off the Lagos- Shagamu-Benin-Abeokuta road encompassing five communities across three local government areas of Ogun State.
The cargo airport is being fashioned after the Dubai airport. In the planned airport, there will be industrial, agricultural, and conference zones to accommodates the different sectors of the economy. Also being planned is residential areas. The proposed airport will have a hanger for aircraft maintenance which will be the first in the subregion. The state government Mr. Sanni said will build the first runway, terminal building in the first phase and encourage the private sector to invest and develop the airport to full capacity.
Justifying the investment the governor said "The proximity of Ogun State to Lagos, Ibadan and the Republic of Benin and its vantage position n-route other States in Nigeria, places the State at the appropriate position to take advantage of the present congestion of Lagos. The traffic gridlock in Lagos, a city of close to 15 million people, is increasingly making the movement of commuters and goods a daunting task. This cargo Airport will thus provide welcome relief in the movement of goods.
There is at present no Airport in the whole of West Africa dedicated to Cargo handling. The proposed agricultural Cargo Airport will be the first in the sub-region and it is expected to handle the inward and outward Cargo of the countries in the sub-region.
Constraint of space at the Murtala Mohammed international Airport (MMIA) and strong agitation for cold storage facilities by importers and exporters are additional strong points of justification for this Cargo Airport project. Also, there is evidence that a demand exists for an aircraft maintenance centre that will not only cater for the Nigerian commercial aircraft fleet need, but also support the aircraft maintenance requirement of all countries in the West and Central African Sub-regions which are certainly encountering similar problems".
"The proposed Cargo Airport Project is to be located away from the congested areas of Lagos, with proximity to 2 Major Trunk A roads leading to the Eastern and Northern parts of Nigeria. It is close to the major commercial centres of the Western part of Nigeria e.g., Lagos and Ibadan. It is also in the vicinity of the already licensed Ogun/Ondo Free Trade Zone and Deep Sea Port. Additionally, the Airport will help to address the constraints on available space at the Murtala Mohammed International Airport which the Federal Government of Nigeria is developing into a sub-regional West Africa hub terminal. The two Airports in Lagos are just two minutes air time from the location of the Cargo Airport.
The ease of development of Industrial Parks based on the nearness of Gas pipelines, Haulage road, Independent Power Plants already in progress is also a strong selling point for the Airport, which also has market opportunity for cargo transportation and ample opportunities for further expansion.
The planned expansion includes airstrip for light to medium cargo and commercial air traffic and helicopter shuttle; Aircraft maintenance facilities; Aircraft maintenance training schools; Flying/pilot training school; Airline and Crew training schools; Flying club; Theme park; Discount shopping warehouse/mall; Light flower, vegetable and fruit farming activities with supportive cottage industries; Housing Estate; Travelers' Inn/Bus Terminus, Hospitality and Resort Complex with training facilities; Parcel and Light mail delivery services; Warehousing services and cargo depot; Petroleum Products depot; Regional light rail hub/terminal; Truck transport depot; Well serviced industrial estate; A Specialist hospital.
The state in collaboration with Veetee Rice, the federal government is also setting up Ofada Veetee Rice project along Shagamu Abeokuta road. The preparation for the project has started with the clearing of part of the 20 hactres of land acquired for the project. According to the Ogun state government the setting up of Ofada Veetee Rice company is the need for import substitution for the high level of importation of rice in the country. Ogun State produces the Ofada variety of Rice in abundance in the Obafemi-Owode Local Governmnet Area of the State. The commercial availability of this nutritious rice variety is an attraction for Investors in the area of Processing. It is this that has attracted Veetee the second largest rice producer in the world to invest in Ogun state. The project is to cost $12million. The rice project is owned 70 per cent by Veetee, 10 per cent federal government and 5 per cent by Ogun state. The 5 per cent stake of Ogun state is the 20 hactres of land which was converted to equity stake in the company. The company will produce top quality rice under the brand name Ofada rice.
Another private sector project being developed in Ogun state is the cassava plant at Ifako. The company, Eka Agro farms is presently cultivating cassava in the state. The company is setting up a N3.4billion plant to process cassava into glucose syrup for pharmaceutical and food processing. Its installed processing capacity on completion is 40 metric tonnes of cassava per day. According to the contractor on sight at the factory premises the project is due for completion early next year.
Yet another cocoa processing plant is in the offing in the state as well as the Ogun/Ondo Free Trade Zone which is a collaborative venture of Ogun and Ondo States in the Southwest of Nigeria. The Project is conceptualised in line with the vision of expanding the economic base of the State by promoting rapid industrialisation and facilitating commerce, thereby harnessing the largely untapped resources of the State.The Project vision is to create conducive business environment that offers trade opportunities and supports efficient and profitable business operations. The Free Trade Zone is a 20,000-hectare industrial enclave, designed to facilitate the establishment of Export-oriented industries through the administration of Special Regulatory and Fiscal Incentives, which permit freedom of operation at globally competitive levels.
The Project focuses on International Trade, Transit Trade, Bonded storage and Export processing, as well as other ancillary Businesses, including Banking, Offshore Finance, Insurance, Futures market, Commodity Exchange, Wharf Management, Haulage, Forex operations, IT and related business.
The entire project will be Private Sector-led, both at the developmental phase and in its day-to-day Management.
The project lined out by Ogun state government are massive and capable of generating employment. The fear is how will the state government finance the projects as it plans to jump start them, with the kind of politics in place in Nigeria ,how is Gbenga Daniel sure that the federal government will grant him licence to build and operate a cargo airport, what if the governor does not secure a second mandate how is he sure of the continuity of the projects? Take the case of the Olumo Rock project, critics are quick to point out that the governor ought to have secured the neighborhood by convincing the people to move out of that area in which he intends to build hotels in before commencing the project. What ever the issues are one thing is certain, Otunba Gbenga Daniels has an idea of what he wants Ogun state to become in the next ten years by developing a master plan for the state. Whether the government will be able to execute the plan faithfully is a question in the lips of many critics of Gbenga Daniels administration. One thing is sure Governor Gbenga Daniel has set for himself a bench mark for which the success or failure of his government in Ogun state will be measured. Sadly, many states in the federal do not have this kind of road map. Time they say is the best judge so in 2007, people will ask the governor about these and many more of the projects Daniel has promised Ogun people and Nigeria at large.

Original_Search_Title: Researchers From Tropics to Give Bananas Big Chill
Headline: Africa: Researchers From Tropics to Give Bananas Big Chill
Body_Text:
Efforts to conserve the genetic diversity of bananas -- economically the
world's fourth most important crop -- have been boosted by the creation of
an international centre for research and training.
The centre at the Catholic University of Leuven in Belgium was set up
under an agreement signed today (18 October) by the university and the
International Plant Genetic Resources Institute (IPGRI).
The agreement recognises the university's expertise in freezing plant
material without damaging it -- a technique it will pass on to researchers
from developing countries, says IPGRI spokesperson Jeremy Cherfas.
For 20 years, the university has hosted the world's largest collection
of bananas -- with samples today of some 1175 varieties. Scientists can use
this resource to develop bananas with traits desired by both farmers and
consumers.
Bananas are a major crop throughout the developing world. But conserving
the genetic diversity of different varieties is difficult because most are
seedless and reproduce by sprouting shoots directly off a parent plant.
This means that samples of these varieties cannot be stored in
conventional seed banks.
Scientists from the University of Leuven have overcome this problem by
freezing parts of the banana plant in a way that does not damage them.
Under the agreement signed today, scientists from developing nations
will be trained in this and other techniques for conserving the genetic
diversity of important crops.
The need to preserve different types of banana was highlighted in the
1950s when the main commercial banana variety, Gros Michel, was wiped out by
a fungal disease. As a result, it was replaced by the Cavendish variety,
which could resist the disease.
Cavendish is now under threat in Asia from a new strain of the fungus
that wiped out Gros Michel, while another deadly fungal disease, known as
Black Sigatoka, has spread from Asia to the Americas and Africa.
A comprehensive collection of banana varieties should help researchers
adapt strains such as Cavendish to the threat of pests and disease or
improve their nutritional value.
"We need the diversity to breed better bananas," says the network's
director, Richard Markham. "And we need better bananas to meet the needs of
the smallholders who depend on banana and plantain for their staple diet and
most of their income."
The newly created centre will also conserve and research other important
tropical crops such as taro and cassava that, like bananas, can reproduce
without producing seeds.

Original_Search_Title: More Aid Needed, Says Unicef
Headline: Malawi: More Aid Needed, Says Unicef
Body_Text:
Johannesburg — The UN Children's Fund (UNICEF) has revised its appeal for Malawi to
meet the needs of those caught in the worst humanitarian crisis the country
has experienced in a decade.
The rate of severe malnutrition among children under five had risen
"alarmingly", UNICEF said. It has revised upwards its earlier appeal for US
$2.5 million to $13 million, of which $9 million will go to fund nutritional
programmes.
Humanitarian agencies initially expected the crisis to peak in the lean
season between December and March, but there have been indications that the
crisis may already be surpassing worst-case scenarios in some areas,
particularly in the south. This has forced many agencies to review their
plans in order to address the rapidly deteriorating situation.
Aid officials told IRIN they would be meeting with the government to
discuss revisions to the UN's $88 million Flash Appeal for Malawi, after
President Bingu wa Mutharika declared a state of disaster in the country at
the weekend. A new Malawi Vulnerability Assessment Committee report with
updated analysis of the impact of widespread food shortages is also expected
by the end of the month.
"The numbers of malnourished children are many times higher than the
normal emergency threshold - immediate and urgent action is needed to save
lives," UNICEF Regional Director for Eastern and Southern Africa Per Engebak
said in a statement.
Over 1,000 children with severe acute malnutrition were receiving
therapeutic feeding at nutritional rehabilitation units, and the number of
monthly admissions was expected to increase to 3,500 as more of the
country's estimated 46,000 severely malnourished children began to seek
treatment.
A further 92,000 moderately malnourished children could become severely
malnourished if they do not receive urgent assistance.
"At the moment, mothers in many areas are feeding their children only
cassava, which is low in the nutrients children need," said Engebak. "These
children are in a precarious situation. We will work with the World Food
Programme to substantially increase the number of children benefiting from
supplementary feeding from the current 16,000 to an additional 60,000
children and pregnant and lactating mothers."
Admissions of severely malnourished children to 48 nutrition
rehabilitation units run by the Ministry of Health, UNICEF and Action
Against Hunger were one-quarter higher in August this year than during the
humanitarian crisis of 2002.
[ This report does not necessarily reflect the views of the United
Nations ]

Original_Search_Title: Do Something About Armed Robbery
Headline: Liberia: Do Something About Armed Robbery
Body_Text:
RECENT REPORTS FROM the Monrovia suburbs of Paynesville, Gardnersville,
New Georgia, Barnesville, most of Bushrod Island, Brewerville and elsewhere
in municipal Liberia are persistent that violent crimes are becoming rife
and serious socio-security factor.
In the last few weeks in the Duport Road, New Kru Town, Freeport, and
Sinkor Old Road communities all in the Monrovia area, and Gbarnga, Ganta,
Buchanan, and elsewhere in the country, armed robbery is becoming routine
and increasingly deadly. A local daily reported yesterday that armed robbers
systematically swept 13 homes, shot and killed a young male and made away
with thousands of dollars in goods and kinds in the Duport Road Community
alone. In Ganta in Nimba County, masked individuals roaming isolated
villages and parts of the city, according to separate reports, are stalking
their victims extorting, raping, and dispossessing them of their meager
resources. In the Ganta incident, a hapless lady was caught in a cassava
patch, brutally assaulted, supposedly raped, and left for dead.
THE REPORTS SUGGEST that these heinous crimes which were perpetrated
against individuals who are not necessarily well-off or against business
conglomerations were perpetrated by opportunistic criminals, who from all
indications are fending for survival than hunting for socio-economic
fortunes. In other words, the crimes are uncoordinated, unrelated, and
characteristics of lone hunters. As the result of the upsurge of criminal
activities, nightlife and the support it gives to trade and tourism are
completely paralyzed as frightened residents and travelers observe
self-imposed curfew and as some areas, roamed freely by criminals, become
peacetime "no go zones".
Besides the effect these criminal activities are currently having on
society, the reports suggest also that left alone to cross the borders of
the transitional period, these sporadic criminal incidents are likely to
grow into organized crimes.
IF THEY DO, needless to say, the society will suffer gravely in all
aspect of the nation's reconstruction and development agenda. To begin,
there will be widespread distrust and disregard for the security forces of
the state as a legacy carried over from UNMIL which is currently providing
security for Liberia. There is no telling what implication this will have on
Liberia's postwar priority areas of reconstruction that involve the
institution of the rule of law, the wiping of the culture of impunity, the
institutionalization of respect for human rights, and the progressive
process of peace building that will pave the way for national reconciliation
and healing. There is no telling also that in a situation whereby a nascent
government will be fighting to consolidate its grips on public institutions,
it will do one of two things with the bet on the latter: battle common
crimes ruthlessly to stamp its authority, thereby gain public confidence and
trust, and risk allegation of human rights violation and vendetta peddling,
or trade pervasive community crimes for its own security and the opportunity
to implant itself into the security-starved Liberian society and
occasionally summarily crack down on suspected criminals just to "play the
government".
IN THE EVENT where the new government is so embroiled and trapped
between its own survival and the welfare of the larger society, experience
tells us that crimes will remain the de facto means of earning a living
amongst most unskilled Liberians who fought in the nation's 14-year conflict
and that there will be a corresponding upsurge of mob justice and official
violence. These in turn will undermine attempts at wiping out the culture of
impunity and the restoration of respect for human rights. Besides,
international opinion of Liberia will fall to the point of disdain,
contempt, and abandoned denial as security falls to zero.
WHEN THIS SCENARIO obtained under the Taylor regime, it lent reasons for
individuals that believe in a military solution to the nation's century-long
leadership question to take to the bushes to confront a government whose
defense was still in the formative stage as it will no doubt be in the first
three quarters of the reign of the next administration.
Where unbridled crimes, government's less than transparent and legal
attempts to curb it and the way how such attempts were interpreted both by
the international community and paranoia Liberians, and international
suspicion left the Liberian nation in its first search for peace and
democracy is a story well known today. No one wants to repeat that,
certainly not the international community that is supposedly in the country
to ensure that things are done more properly this time around to ensure
peace and stability in the ECOWAS subregion and the average Liberian who
wants to pick up the pieces and reorder his life.
IMMEDIATE ACTION THEREFORE needs to be taken to curb the growing wave of
violent crimes in our society.
Liberia may be in the throes of a violent conflict, but it will not take
kindly to crime as part of the private sector, or as trade-off for war or
trade-off for a failed RR programme. And it will seriously subtract from the
gains so far made by UNMIL and other international institutions and
governments if the current lordship of the communities by criminals is not
curbed by the vast UN military machinery prior to the inauguration of the
next government. We suggest therefore that IPS strengthen its working plan
with the LNP to increase community patrol, coordinate and effectuate
response to SOS, launch sustained post-crime investigations to curb criminal
impunity to instill fear in would-be armed robbers, and put information
boxes in strategic places as a way of increasing and making meaningful the
system of community policing so well spoken of within security circles in
the country.

Original_Search_Title: Corruption, Bad Leadership, Our Bane -- Cleric
Headline: Nigeria: Corruption, Bad Leadership, Our Bane -- Cleric
Body_Text:
Kano — THE Bishop of Calvary Life Assembly International Church, Kano, Bishop
Ransom S. Bello has said the problem we have in this country is leadership
and corruption, adding that the only way forward to achieve our political
dream in this country is to conquer the corruption in the nation's polity,
stressing that this cannot be done over night.
Bishop Bello who made this observation weekend, while enumerating the
activities line up for this year's national convention of the Calvary Life
Assembly International church, said that corruption is an epidemic in the
system, which cannot be eradicated within a short period of time.
Said he "Nigerians must realized that the nation's epidemic called
corruption has over stayed in the system and cannot be eradicated within a
short time as many Nigerians wrongly believe. It has to be done gradually
like the present government is doing if really we want it out of our system"
The cleric noted that there has been remarkable improvement in the
economy over the years, and added that hence Nigerians know whom they want
to be their leader, they should take advantage of the 2007 and elect the
candidate of their choice.
On the going power tussle among politicians, Bishop Bello stressed that
those who are clamouring for power now are only gladiators, adding that at
the right time with the prayers of Nigerians the real person for the
nation's number one seat will emerge.
Also commenting on the economic reforms introduced by the present
administration, he said eventhough they are good for the growth of the
economy, government should realize by now that Nigerians are suffering and
should come up with soothing measures to lessen the hardship in the
country.
Bishop Bello said "I am not against the reforms of the present
government, the fact is that things are hard in the land. It is like a
farmer who plants cassava as well as plant palm trees, he does not rely on
palm produce alone because it takes years to mature rather he relies on the
cassava which maturity period is shorter."
His words "I strongly belief that if there is sincerity and honesty in
the 2007 election, and the will of the people are respected, we will be able
to install choice of the people as our next leader, in anticipation of
change, am not saying that all the past leaders are completely"
The cleric who commended the present administration for the measures so
far adopted to curb corruption in the country, said for the first time
Nigerians are seeing the untouchable being caught up with the long hands of
the law, saying it a demonstration of commitment towards fringing corruption
by those in the power.
on the national convention of his church, he said is the most important
programmes of his church said it a periods where the whole church across the
country gather to gather to prayer to God for themselves and for the nation
as a whole.
Bishop Bello also disclosed that the chief guest speaker will be the
national present of PFN, Bishop Ayo Orisajefor will be supported by a
visiting German evangelist Daniel Schott and Bishop Taiwo Adelekun .

Original_Search_Title: Chipata Motorists Turn to Malawi for Fuel
Headline: Zambia: Chipata Motorists Turn to Malawi for Fuel
Body_Text:
SEVERAL motorists in Chipata district are crossing into neighbouring
Malawi in search of fuel as the shortage continues to hit the country.
Chipata district commissioner, Moses Nyirenda confirmed the development
in an interview with the Times during the week.
The fuel crisis had forced motorists to buy fuel in Malawi, a distance
covered in about 15 minutes drive, to avoid long queues in Chipata.
BP Zambia, one of the fuel stations in Chipata had managed to maintain a
constant supply of fuel despite the preference by the motorists to buy the
commodity in Malawi.
Mr Nyirenda also said kerosene which most of the rural people depended
on for their domestic use was the most sourced for fuel product but it was
currently difficult to find.
The situation was made difficult following the routine load shading by
Zesco as three quarters of the residents relied on the kerosene during
disruptions of electricity supply.
It was also feared that fuel shortage might have a negative impact on
farmers' preparations for the forthcoming season.
Mr Nyirenda said the shortage had already affected most of the machinery
that was fuel-operated and that adequate preparations for the farming period
were at a standstill at the moment.
"Although we have this crisis, we hope the farming season will not be as
affected as was the case last season when the farmers had to make do with
erratic rainfall," he said.
Local farmers were advised to consider diversification and grow crops
like cassava, tobacco and cotton which were also cash crops as an
alternative to maize.
The dependency on maize could be done at a different level and consider
planting early maturing crops in case of a short rainy season.
He said the farming capacity of Chipata was enough to feed the whole
district and even supply to neighbouring districts but farmers had not
reached their peak.
Meanwhile, the district commissioner has called on Chipata residents to
step-up measures to avoid recurring diseases like cholera and dysentery as
the rainy season nears.
Mr Nyirenda said with basic principles of desisting from throwing litter
in the drainages and having pit latrines in all remote areas could help curb
what had become annual diarrhoea diseases.

Original_Search_Title: Urgent Action Needed to Save Thousands of Hungry Children - Unicef
Headline: Malawi: Urgent Action Needed to Save Thousands of Hungry Children in Malawi - Unicef
Body_Text:
Immediate action is needed to save the lives of thousands of children
under five in Malawi, and the United Nations Children's Fund (UNICEF) has
increased its earlier appeal more than fivefold to $13 million as severe
malnutrition increases alarmingly in the southern African country.
"The numbers of malnourished children are many times higher than the
normal emergency threshold," UNICEF Regional Director for Eastern and
Southern Africa Per Engebak said.
He noted that more than 1,000 children with severe acute malnutrition
were already receiving therapeutic feeding, with the number of monthly
admissions expected to increase to 3,500 as many of the estimated 46,000
severely malnourished children seek treatment. A further 92,000 moderately
malnourished children could become severely malnourished if they do not
receive immediate assistance.
"Immediate and urgent action is needed to save lives," Mr. Engebak
said. "At the moment, mothers in many areas are feeding their children only
cassava, which is low in the nutrients children need.
"These children are in a precarious situation. We will work with the
(UN) World Food Programme (WFP) to substantially increase the number of
children benefiting from supplementary feeding from the current 16,000 to an
additional 60,000 children and pregnant and lactating mothers," he
added.
By all accounts, this year's food crisis may turn out to be worse than
that of three years ago. Admissions of severely malnourished children to 48
Nutrition Rehabilitation Units run by the Ministry of Health, UNICEF and
Action Against Hunger was one-quarter higher in August than during the
humanitarian crisis of 2002.
"We have revised our earlier appeal from $2.5 million to $13 million,"
Mr. Engebak said. "Of this sum, we intend to use $9 million on nutritional
activities, including expanding therapeutic centres, providing
micronutrients to 500,000 pregnant and lactating women, and de-worming of
children," he said.
The WFP is also rapidly expanding food assistance in seven southern
districts and plans to reach up to 2 million people by early November with
maize, pulses and oil. These measures, targeted at the poor and most
vulnerable, will complement the efforts of government and other donors to
ensure adequate supplies of humanitarian and commercial maize are available
in country.

Original_Search_Title: World Food Day : How Sufficient?
Headline: Cameroon: World Food Day : How Sufficient?
Body_Text:
Prime Minister Ephraim Inoni will today deliver a key speech at the 60th anniversary of the FAO and events marking the World Food Day.
Heads of State and Government are today meeting in Rome to examine and evaluate progress so far made since the existence of the Food and Agriculture Organization (FAO) 60 years ago. It is also a unique occasion for the world leaders to pore into diverse challenges ahead, such as hunger, famine and drought that continue to pose a threat to millions of lives world-wide, notably in Africa. Prime Minister, Ephraim Inoni is representing the Head of State at the ceremony.
It is within this context that the main theme of the commemoration revolves on five key words "Free the World From Hunger". This theme is itself not an accident of fate, but is studded with symbolism and significance. Cognizant of some unenviable realities like famine in Niger, Malawi and even hunger in some parts of northern Cameroon, the FAO could not have had another theme. As if cursed by nature, the African continent in particular has had its deadly toll almost on a yearly basis. Within this premise, Cameroon is determined to make the country's voice not only heard but to try and identify lasting solutions to the cankerworm called hunger.
As such, Prime Minister Ehpraim Inoni (the personal representative of President Paul Biya), is thus heading a delegation that was received yesterday at Rome's international airport by Mr Medi Moungui (the Second Adviser in Cameroon's Embassy in Italy and Cameroon's Assistant Permanent Representative to the FAO). He represented Cameroon's Ambassador to Italy and Permanent Representative to the FAO Michael Tabong Kima. Amongst those in the Cameroonian delegation is the Minister of Agriculture and Rural Development Clobert Tchatat.
It should be recalled that Cameroon became a member of the FAO in 1961 and the Organization has a representative bureau in Yaounde headed by Mrs Doaulé Diallo Ba. While the FAO commemorates its 60th years of existence, there is much the Organization has done in Cameroon.
The balance-sheet is glaring. The FAO has been involved in intensive cassava production, processing and distribution in Epen Bajoh in Bangem (South West Province), involved in rabbit rearing in Foto village (near Dschang) in the West Province, given much assistance in the production and processing of bananas and paw-paw in Nkongsamba and Pouma in the Littoral Province, given assistance in the production of fruits and vegetables in Maroua, helped in fish farming improvement in Limbe, assisted in intensive poultry production in Jakiri (North West Province), helped in the durable development of semi-modern small poultry in association with the cultivation of maize and Soya beans in many parts of the country, etc.
These projects are not exhaustive. However, the task ahead for the FAO remains enormous and challenging. The final resolution of this year's commemoration will not be known today. The draft resolution will be tabled for adoption during the FAO's General Assembly Conference slated for November 2005 in Rome.

Original_Search_Title: Donors Shun FSP-FAO
Headline: Zambia: Donors Shun FSP-FAO
Body_Text:
FOOD and Agriculture Organisation (FAO) country representative Dong
Qingsong has said that some donors have not been willing to fund the Food
Security Pack (FSP) believing that Zambia has enough food.
Mr Dong said yesterday at a Press briefing to mark the World Food Day
that falls on October 17, that his office had been writing letters to donors
seeking for assistance on the FSP but some of them had been hesitant.
Government was working with the FAO and donors in the provision of FSP
to the vulnerable people but beneficiaries could not be increased from
125,000 because of lack of funds.
On food security in the country the situation was okay though some
households in Southern province had no food due to drought.
"It depends on how you look at food security according levels because
how you move surplus produce from one province to the one with a deficit
matters a lot," he said.
He advised that as the country spoke of crop diversification there
should also be diversification in eating habits because Zambians were highly
dependant on white maize and were refusing to eat yellow grain.
The composition of the food basket also mattered because at needy times
people could mix maize meal with cassava, wheat or sorghum, which were also
nutritious.
Agriculture in Zambia was doing well and the country was endowed with
abundant natural resources, water and good climatic conditions couple with
Government's commitment.
Zambia should not only be a recipient of products from other countries
but have capacity to send its produce to other countries for it had exported
maize, wheat, millet and groundnut seeds to neighbouring countries.
This year's theme is Agriculture and Intercultural Dialogue because
sincere intercultural dialogue was a precondition for progress against
hunger and environmental degradation.
"Open-minded dialogue is important between different cultures in the
same country and at the international level, many societies feel threatened
by a form of intercultural dialogue," he said.

Original_Search_Title: Village Bears the Wrath of Soil Erosion
Headline: Kenya: Village Bears the Wrath of Soil Erosion
Body_Text:
Nairobi — When a colonial agricultural officer warned in the 1950s that if left unchecked, torrential waters would wash away the soil cover in the Kerio Valley, he was ridiculed.
The man, comically referred to "arap Leso" because of the cloth he often wrapped round himself, tried to educate the locals on soil conservation, but no one took him seriously.
Mr Julius Chepchieng, 50, points at the spot where he use to play football in his youth at Salawa Village, Kerio Valley, in Baringo District.
"Our fathers cursed him ... they wondered how water could wash away all the soil cover," said Kimoso assistant chief Joseph Chebii.
"Now if you woke them from the dead, they will not recognise the land they left," he says.
"Perhaps they will recognise their beehives and the hills, but everything else has changed for the worse," he said.
Among the Tugen community in Baringo District, it is a taboo to move a beehive unless it falls off a tree.
Chebii attributes the area's denudation to lack of education, ignorance and the widespread belief that "water is life".
"We pray for rain, but when it comes it is a blessing and a curse," says Kimoso.
The huge deep ravines and valleys caused by runoff are the frightening examples of how erosion can reduce good agricultural land into a massive wasteland.
The brown silt-laden waters of River Eron are evidence of the mass wasting.
"Our people do not know, or if they are aware, they simply ignore the fact that ploughing along river banks and on hilly ground is detrimental to the environment," he said.
W J Elliot, G R Foster, A V Elliot in Soil erosion: processes, impacts and prediction, say because soil degradation and erosion happen so slowly, they seldom give rise to immediate action.
"In fact, it is hardly noticed during one generation of say, 30 years."
When arap Leso gave the warning, it was impossible to imagine that the landscape at Eron would be so radically changed.
Where there was rich agricultural soil now lies deep grotesque gulleys.
Mr Julius Chepchieng, a resident in his late 50s, points with nostalgia to a spot where he played football as a young boy.
It has been stripped of the soil cover, leaving a wretched rocky landscape.
The villages are ringed by a rugged topography that none can build or farm on.
The area which once knew no hunger can only dream of bumper harvest.
"We have built gabions and dams, even planted trees, but with little success," Chepchieng says.
"The place will never be the same again. When it rains, the rivers fill up and the savage water gushing downstream sweeps away anything in its way. Once the torrent waters swept away a lorry loaded with sugar and flour," he says.
Between the riverbeds hang huge walls of soil like tall sand castles that threaten to tumble down any minute.
A web of roots jut out of riverbeds, a sign that trees once grew there.
The water currents have scoured out the soil, leaving the scene looking as if a madman hurriedly dug it with a giant fork jembe (hoe).
Gulleys are as deep as 10 metres and trees are felled by the sheer force of the water because the soil that held it has been washed away.
But it is not all hopeless.
The Kerio Valley Association (Keva), a Non-Governmental Organisation, is one of the NGOs involved in soil conservation in the area.
Dr Daniel Chebutuk, the co-ordinator, says a team of professionals is involved in soil conservation awareness campaigns. It is a food-for-work programme.
The gains have been little but promising.
"We fill gullies with logs and tree stumps and plant vegetation like lilies known locally as koromotwo," he says.
With contribution from members, Keva has built two check dams to control erosion.
A third one was delayed for lack of funds.
Mr Philip Matatei, the Divisional Agricultural Extension Officer, says to avoid the erosion and soil degradation, soils should be left to heal.
But his advice is not heeded because residents have to cultivate despite the obvious consequences.
Soil erosion is a natural process. But it becomes a problem when human activity causes it to occur much faster than the natural rate as is the case in Salawa.
Matetai estimates that the annual soil loss in Salawa is 100 tonnes, nearly three lorry loads, per acre each year.
"Ten more years and we will have no place to farm or even stand on," he says.
Soil erosion and degradation are global problems. The ploughing of lands unsuitable for cultivation has led to erosion and accelerated desertification.
In dry weather, wind blows the loose soil, sweeping away huge mounds.
The Food and Agriculture Organisation estimates that the global loss of productive land through erosion is 5-7 million hectares a year.
In America, the Food Securities Act of 1985 requires that farmers apply conservation measures to remain eligible for government support.
That has helped scale down the menace.
Jamii Mazingira Baringo (Jamaba), a Danish organization, tried this approach in Salawa with a bit of success.
Working with agricultural field extension officers, they helped educate the locals on soil conservation, including planting grass that hold the soil, digging terraces and planting crops like cassava, millet, groundnuts and sorghum that need little or no tilling.
Jamaba is the name that locals call trenches dug across the farms.
There are two types, fanya juu, that is- the soil dug out is heaped on the upper side, and fanya chini, its opposite. Jamaba pulled out in 1997.
Matetai says because of inadequate funds, little will be achieved especially now that Salawa Division was merged with Kabarnet Division.
That means resources meant for one division will have to be shared between two.
He says without urgent intervention, soil erosion could soon reduce the area to a desert.
The writer is a sub-editor at 'The Standard'.

Original_Search_Title: Cassava Export: Great Catalyst to Poverty Alleviation -- Sasore
Headline: Nigeria: Cassava Export: Great Catalyst to Poverty Alleviation -- Sasore
Body_Text:
THE exportation of cassava and its derivatives will serve as a great
catalyst to the present administration's efforts at poverty alleviation and
employment generation.
Speaking at a one-day seminar on export of cassava and its derivatives
held in Lagos recently, Special Adviser to the President on Export Programme
and Chief Executive Officer (CEO) of Nigerian Export Promotion Council
(NEPC), who was also special guest of honour, Mrs. Modupe Sasore said that
the setting up of the Presidential Committee on Cassava Export Promotion was
to create awareness and enlightened Nigerians on the need to develop and
promote cassava export.
Sasore said that several derivatives can be made from cassava which is
regarded as "Green Gold" in sub-Saharan Africa, adding that kerosene can be
processed from cassava -- a perfect substitute for crude oil based kerosene,
fuel wood used for fighting desertification and it offers import
substitution for monosodium glutamate used for seasoning foods and many
others.
She stated that through the efforts of NEPC, Chinese business men have
signed a Memorandum of Understanding (MOU)with Nigeria to import 100,000
tonnes of cassava chips annually, saying however that "the present
administration has put in place a conducive environment for exporting
cassava chips from Nigeria with arrays of incentives."
Earlier in his welcome address, the Executive Director of the Centre for
Sustainable Agricultural Development, Mr. Agbor Ndoma commended the Federal
Government initiatives which seek to transform cassava from a predominantly
food crop to a cash crop both for the domestic and export market, explaining
that "this initiative will facilitate immensely the current drive to
developing the nation's economy, hence reducing her dependence on oil."
Ndoma said that the major objectives of the Centre for Sustainable
Agricultural Development is to sustain increased awareness and positive
driving programmes that will attract dormant stakeholders to have a
re-appraisal in this sub-sector.

Original_Search_Title: Mission to Flood-Affected Areas Planned, Official Says
Headline: Central African Republic: Mission to Flood-Affected Areas Planned, Official Says
Body_Text:
Bangui — The government of the Central African Republic (CAR) may send a mission to flood affected areas outside the capital, Bangui, to assess the agricultural damage caused by floods that swept parts of the country in August, a government official has said.
"We are going to get in touch with the Red Cross to get their report in order to plan a mission into the [affected] zones," M'Peco Etienne, the director of planning in the Ministry of Agriculture, told IRIN on Thursday.
He said the government did not have a clear picture of the impact of the floods, especially in the central province of Ouaka, where farms were reported still flooded.
"We don't have the map of high risk zones in the country," he said. "We have no information about farms flooded by rain water in the towns of Bambari, Kouango, Grimari and Bakala as reported by the National Red Cross Committee".
However, Etienne said: "There is a great risk that the town of Kouango could get affected by the flooding as it is close to the Ubangui River. And if it is the case there will be a lack of cassava in the area".
Cassava is a staple in the country. Etienne said the consequences of the flooding on agriculture would not be established immediately."
On Monday, CAR Red Cross Programme Coordinator Alphonse Zarambaud said the country was still in need of international aid following the August floods. He said as the dry season approached, the Red Cross was concerned about the consequences of the flooding, especially in Ouaka.
"A large number of houses were destroyed by rain water in cities such as Bakala, Grimari, Kouango and Bambari," Zarambaud said. "Many farms were flooded and the risk of famine is looming ahead in the zones hit by the latest disaster."
He added that farmers in the region had sold their reserve food stocks in order to survive or to pay school fees.
"Farmers in the affected areas have no seed for the coming agricultural season and we would like the international community to help in providing the people with seeds," he said.
[ This report does not necessarily reflect the views of the United Nations ]

Original_Search_Title: Liberian Expert Conducts Assessment
Headline: Liberian Expert Conducts Assessment
Body_Text:
A Liberian agronomist and agriculturist, Solomon C. Hedd-Williams, is in
the country to conduct an assessment on agricultural
industry. Mr. Hedd-Williams who arrived in the country recently is resident
in the United States of America.
He is expected to assess the status of Liberia's agricultural sector in
a bid to formulate a comprehensive plan for agricultural development. During
his assessment mission, he will assess the status of the Central Agriculture
Research Institute (CARI) and other vital agricultural facilities.
He holds a Bachelor of Science (BSc.) Degree in Agriculture and Master
of Science (MSc.) in Agronomy. In an interview, Mr. Hedd-Williams recounted
the colossal damage done to the agriculture sector of Liberia as a result of
the civil war.
According to him, the war has caused a complete breakdown and neglect of
the sector. Hedd-Williams said current agricultural practices in Liberia are
mainly based on humanitarian assistance from international organizations
such as German Agro Project and United Nations Food and Agriculture
Organization (FAO), adding that this is evident by subsistence methods
amongst of local farmers.
He urged Liberians to return to the soil. He emphasized that "there is a
huge potential for the introduction of plantation farming as part of a
modern agriculture which will bring about mass production "in our local
agricultural produce". He stated: "There is evidence that our vast land has
adequately not been utilized and cultivated for agricultural purpose".
Hedd-Williams believes that with an "increased land cultivation",
increased incentives for local farmers, improved farming practices and the
introduction of a modern agricultural system, Liberia would obtain some
level of food security, thus making Liberia self-sufficient in food
production.
He reminded Liberians that the soil is a profitable bank which can
change the economic well-being of people who take it serious.
Cassava Cultivation Project Launched
A local non-governmental organization, promoting intermediate technology
as a tool for sustainable rural development, on Wednesday launched a
fifty-acre cassava cultivation and tuber processing project in Gbatoja,
Gawular District, Grand Cape Mount County.
The project, according to a release, is intended to encourage and train
poor rural youths acquire skills that will enable them cultivate and process
cassava into finished products by using available intermediate
technology.
Seed funding for the project is being provided by the Kulaa Foundation,
a poverty alleviation charity. Harris T. Kanneh, Chief Executive Officer of
APPROTECH Liberia Inc., at the launching ceremony noted that the fight
against poverty was a challenging but demanding task.
Describing the project as a model, he pointed out that the factors of
production available are recipe for success. He then called on the
government of Liberia and the international NGOs to assist the
project. Making remarks at the launching, Mrs. Ciatta Caine-Sheriff,
President of the Kulaa Foundation, reiterated her foundation's commitment to
sustainable development through poverty alleviation.
She admonished the rural poor to stand and fight poverty, asserting that
victory over poverty means improvement to livelihood. Thirty-five (35) war
affected people of Gbatoja and its surrounding villages are to benefit from
the project.

Original_Search_Title: Commodities Fund Invests N92bn in Nigeria
Headline: Nigeria: Commodities Fund Invests N92bn in Nigeria
Body_Text:
The Managing Director of the Common Fund for Commodities, Ambassador Ali
Mchumo has said that the fund has spend about $70million (about N92.4
billion) in financing some projects in Nigeria and some other countries
within the west African sub region.
Speaking to Business Trust Tuesday in Abuja at a dinner organized by the
ministry of commerce, Amb. Mchumo said his visit to Nigeria was to supervise
some of the projects executed in the country by the fund, adding that
already the fund has intervene in projects on some commodities such as
cocoa, sorghum, and rice growing.
He explained that CFC projects are based on the commodity focus, saying
that emphasis of the fund is to ensure commodity development and marketing
in the global market.
He said Nigeria as one of the major player in the fund has greater role
in providing information on its priority commodity for the CFC to execute,
stressing further that his organization's policy is that "we normally fund
project with the commodity focus and we intervene based on country's demand
on a particular commodity".
He said that with recent diversification of the economy by Nigerian
government aimed to reduce reliance on petroleum products "am sure
government has well intention in the area alleviating poverty and improves
the lives of its citizens".
The Project Manager of the CFC, Andrey Kuleshov told Business Trust that
the fund has in for the cassava development programme which at the moment
waiting for the proposal from the committee.
Kuleshov explained that the fund has discovered market for cassava in
some part of African countries.
The managing director who was in the country for a two-day visit met
with the officials of the ministry of commerce, Abuja Commodity and
Exchange, minister of agriculture as well as the executive secretary of the
Economic Community of West African States (ECOWAS).
CFC has 106 member countries, international organizations including
African Union, European Union among others.

Original_Search_Title: Charming Graca's Grand Resolve to Change Africa
Headline: Kenya: Charming Graca's Grand Resolve to Change Africa
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Tacking in her long braided hair neatly behind her ears, Dr Graca
Machel, wife of former South African president, smiles charmingly at the
delegates of the African Peer Review Mechanism (APRM).
The VIP lounge in Kisumu Hotel is packed with an enthused audience from
Nyanza Province to listen to the judicious counsel. She is not here to talk
Orange or Banana, but about good governance; how to eradicate the biting
poverty in the region, combating corruption and furthering democracy.
Little wonders that the anxious faces comprise a medley of political
persuasions and socio-economic status.
The light-skinned former first lady, adorned with golden earrings and a
matching necklace and bracelet, looks set for the task.
On her left lapel is a red and gold beaded Aids ribbon that reminds you
his husband, Nelson Mandela, and his determination to fight the
pandemic.
With that flawless skin and milk-white teeth, Graca's appears so a
beauty for a lady her age.
Forget about all the officialdom that surrounds her status. In her
simple brown kitenge and a matching brown handbag, there is nothing
artificial in her.
But there is something about the lady that easily endears her to almost
anyone.
The Planning minister, Anyang' Nyong'o, the PC and DCs keep referring to
"her excellency", yet her calm seems to show some discomfort with the
title. She smiles calmly and nods in concentration.
I want to ask her about life with Mandela, an icon revered globally, and
when she agrees to give me an interview, I list down my questions:
How did you meet Mandela? What attracted you to him? What was your first
impression? A phone call? Love letter? How have you adapted from one country
to another? How does it feel to be a first lady twice, in different nations?
What do you and Madiba discuss after work? What are the challenges in your
marriage? Any fond memories of your first husband, the late Mozambican
President Samora Machel?
But Graca is firm and principled: "I am sorry to disappoint you but I
never answer to personal questions. Ask me anything about APRM or Nepad and
I will talk." That's her.
You are always smiling your excellency. Is there anything you hate?
"You see my dear," she says, patting me on the shoulder during a tea
break, "these people call me 'Your Excellency" but I hate the title. I would
rather they called me mama."
Mama? The last I heard someone address a woman that way, she almost
pulped him, at least with her harsh tongue.
It's as if she reads the thoughts: "A woman is the best thing that I
could ever be. Offices and status come and go. I will not always be a first
lady, I will not always be a Nepad official, but I will always be a woman;
It is one thing I really cherish. If there is a quality that I am proud of,
it is being a mum."
At this juncture, former Gem MP, Grace Ogot, who sits next to (now, I
can safely say it) mama, throughout the interview nods in agreement and
interjects: "Yes, there is nothing greater for a woman than to be called
mama." Then the smile, mama. Let us talk about your smile. Do you ever get
angry? Do you sulk? For instance, were you annoyed last Friday when the
legislators failed to meet you?
"Well, nobody has ever asked me about my smile," she says, this time
with a wide, hearty grin, (it makes her look so youthful). "I smile because
I am very passionate about things. I feel very deeply about issues. I don't
pretend though, neither do I get very angry."
Two things upset mama this week, she confesses, wearing a serious face:
MPs failing to turn up to meet her and the many security personnel that
accompanied her to Kibera slums.
"You see, they (legislators) made me wait all morning without a word. I
could have rescheduled my day and done something useful to the
community. But that is all water under the bridge. We shall meet on
Thursday, God willing," she says.
Securing an interview with Graca is not easy. She has a heavy security
detail, some from the Office of the President - and they are not about to
risk anything for her life.
"Before I visited the Kibera slums yesterday, I specifically asked the
security detail not to accompany me in such a huge convoy.
"I grew up as a young rural woman in a very poor home; I have tasted
real poverty and I do not need anyone to tell me about it. I have
experienced the indignity of poverty. So, when I visit the poor I must be
sensitive; I must get down to their level and mix with them freely. Driving
in a convoy of flashy cars to Kibera, is, to say the least, unfair. It made
me so mad. It defeated the justice of my mission," says her,
as-a-matter-of-factly.
She has beauty, grace and charm, but what attracts one to Graca most is
that she is so down to earth and candid.
Mama has a high sense of humour too, and this gets infectious. Soon,
Prof Nyong'o picks the cue and creates fun out of her name: "Graca is
Italian for 'Thank You'," he says. "It is a word that spells
gratitude. There are two women here who share with you the name Grace. Meet
the first mayor of Kisumu and the first woman Member of Parliament in Kenya,
madam Grace Onyango-Nya Bungu."
"Meet the second Grace," Grace Ogot, former Gem MP, says as they
hug.
"Gem is very important to me, Your Excellency (again!) because it is
where my wife comes from. My two sisters are also married in Gem, Your
Excellency (I give up!). My first two serious girlfriends were also from Gem
too," that's the minister.
Everyone around giggles as if wishing they too were called Grace.
Nyong'o then gets serious and challenges Maseno University to get
involved in soil chemistry and marine engineering to help the local
community sustain itself through indigenous local resources like fish and
cassava.
After Ms Sally Oduor performs a sterling tune on "The God of Kenya",
Graca interrupts the programme to call her, shakes both her hands and
whispers a word of congratulations.
Then back to serious business.
"We as the APRM team are happy to be here today. We have been in the
country for the last week and it has been an enriching experience to meet
the diverse faces of Kenya. We are amazed by the deep thinking on the
strengths, challenges and weaknesses of your country."
Graca says the peer review is a process that helps us to see whether all
the organs of a nation are performing well in a healthy environment.
"The process can be compared to the blood circulation of the body. How
do we, as small and big organs of a nation, communicate with one another?"
she poses.
"That is why we are meeting public officers, civil societies and
legislators; Those who have taken the oath to serve the nation on behalf of
the millions of Kenyans to be accountable."
She says people must themselves if theirs is a healthy nation, and
recognise that they are a diverse nation regionally and economically.
"How do we manage the diversity of the wealth, knowing one another,
respecting the differences?
How do the different regions share the wealth and resources among the
men, women youth and children? How do people express their voices and
opinions without being victimised?" she says.
With a population of 5 million in Nyanza, and 46 per cent below the
poverty line, and suffering from a high prevalence of HIV/Aids, she says,
how do we ensure that the community does not lose its adult members?
Nepad came into being as Africa's answer to her internal problems and
her marginalisation in world affairs.
It was formed after a prolonged debate involving African scholars,
activists and political leaders, and was adopted by the African Union Heads
of States and Governance Summit of 2002.
Graca says, the body represents the vision of a better Africa, free of
political conflict, tyranny, hunger and poverty.
Mama then excuses herself for a group discussion.

Original_Search_Title: Core Investor Divests From AP PLC
Headline: Nigeria: Core Investor Divests From AP PLC
Body_Text:
Lagos — Sadiq Petroleum Limited, the core investor in African Petroleum (AP)
Plc, is divesting its shares from the oil marketing company in line with its
recent repositioning exercise.
Also, the Nigerian National Petroleum Corporation (NNPC) has reduced by
43 percent, the number of fuel cargoes the corporation is placing orders for
in the first half of 2006, following a sharp increase in domestic production
of petroleum products.
THISDAY checks revealed that Sadiq Petroleum Ltd, which emerged the core
investor in the privatization of AP Plc, will be meeting the Nigerian Stock
Exchange (NSE) before the end of this week to officially indicate its plan
to sell its 30 percent stake in the oil company.
Senior officials of Sadiq Petroleum said the divestment was in line with
a restructuring of the company's business processes and more importantly, to
halt further investment in AP Plc whose growth has been stifled.
Sources however, told THISDAY that the core investor was pulling out as
a pre-emptive move against the eventual take over by the NNPC. But both the
NSE and Security Exchange Commission have insisted that NNPC's bid to
take-over AP is against the government's privatisation policy.
The Corporation had demanded that the sum of N11.1 billion debt owed it
by AP Plc be repaid by end of August or be converted into equity in the oil
marketing company.
NNPC and AP management are scheduled to meet again by the end of this
week, which according to sources, was to reconcile the debt that has
remained in dispute for more than four years.
While the NNPC is claiming that AP Plc owed the N11.1 billion for
petroleum products supplied to the company before it was privatized in 2001,
Sadiq Petroleum Ltd has insisted that the debt incurred by the previous
management of AP and made known to it by the Bureau of Public Enterprises
(BPE), is N1.2 billion.
The NNPC has since then cut fuel supplies to AP and even barred the oil
marketer from using its fuel receiving facilities throughout the
country.
Checks revealed that following the ban, AP's shares has plummeted
sharply from a peak of N69.00 per share to the lowest point of N35.82 per
share as traded on the floor of the NSE yesterday.
Official said a run on the fortunes of the company manifested at its
Annual General Meeting (AGM) held last month, when shareholders rather than
been paid dividend, were offered a bonus of 1 for 5.
"Given the precarious situation the company has found itself, and given
the NNPC insistence on the debt for equity, the divestment option for the
core investor was not out of place," an AP source said.
The NNPC debt for equity move may see the Corporation holding 47.2
percent of the oil marketer.
Sadiq Petroleum divestment bid according to sources, might also have
come as an option following the failure to get a political resolution of the
debt dispute through the institution of a public inquiry by the Federal
Government in 2001.
Government set up a tribunal headed by Hon. Justice Robert Nkop (rtd) in
August 2002, to investigate the claims by AP core investors that it
discovered a whopping N25 billion debt in the oil company that was concealed
prior to it making the purchase. The debt consisted of Commercial
Papers/Banker's Acceptance (BA) and the NNPC debt.
The tribunal in its report submitted to the Federal Government in
February 2003, indicted the BPE for failing to carry out pre-privatisation
governance issues Audit before selling government shares and also
recommended that the Federal Government should direct the NNPC to write off
N9.1 billion of the debt NNPC claimed AP owed it.
Meanwhile, the NNPC, which in itself has come under debt of about $1
billion (N130 billion) owed to oil trading companies for imported fuel, said
it will reduce its orders for fuel imports to 20 cargoes for the first
quarter (January to March) 2006, from the 35 cargoes imported for the last
quarter of this year.
Speaking to newsmen yesterday in Akure at the just concluded Ondo State
Economic Summit, the NNPC Group Managing Director, Engineer Funsho
Kupolokun, said the 43 percent reduction in imports was due to the
significant increase in production from the four local refineries.
Capacity utilization in the refineries according to Kupolokun, has
averaged 70 percent in the last three months, while the complete overhaul of
the fluid catalytic cracking (FCC) unit in all the refineries has raised the
volume of premium motor spirit (PMS) production.
"For the first time since 40 years (1965 when the first refinery was
built), the nation now has at least 40-day sufficiency of prime products,
which is the standard world wide," said Kupolokun.
"When we came in 2003, we (NNPC) were importing 70 cargos of PMS and
some 10 cargoes of kerosene. So in all we were importing about 80
cargoes. This quarter it has gone down to 35 indicating a 50 per cent
reduction and I see us in another quarter going down to something between 20
and 25 cargoes..
"We will leave it at that level so that we are also in the market. If
you are not in the market, you will not be able to know what is going on and
you won't be in a position to talk authoritatively. It looks like we have
come to the end of it," he added.
The NNPC chief executive said further that the Corporation has embarked
upon a Fuel Ethanol Programme with a view to creating a thriving domestic
fuel ethanol industry within the next five years. This he added, will reduce
the current dependence on oil and create viable commercial ventures through
its value chain.
"The benefits of the fuel ethanol programme include the development of
large scale cassava plantations, rural development, increased job
creationâ-oeas well as enhanced industrialization prospects," he said.
He stated further that the problematic Ore fuel depot which has been
shut down for several years due to the severe vandalisation of the
Mosimi-Ore pipeline, would be re-launched by March next year after the
rehabilitation of the damaged pipeline. The depot he added would come with
expanded storage tanks.

Original_Search_Title: Achieving Pragmatic Economic Reforms
Headline: Nigeria: Achieving Pragmatic Economic Reforms
Body_Text:
Lagos — AS a national policy, economic reforms are good for the purpose of
realistic development. They are similar to national development plans which
provide essential guidelines on how to project economic progression. Such
reforms stimulate appropriate vision and mission of the state in the maze of
conflicting factors. They also mobilise the people and engender accelerated
manpower development. They help to correct undue anomalies and duplications,
erase wasteful pursuits and boost productivity. A lot depends on how the
leadership identifies the relevant segments of reform programmes and propels
the methodology of their faithful execution. Reforms can take various
coloration in the context of the development charter and cover sectors such
as education, health, housing, employment, agriculture, food, co-operatives
and what have you.
The ongoing reforms may seem necessary in terms of our desire to advance
development efforts but many questions are being asked. How original are
these reforms? Are they actually relevant to our prime needs? Will they at
the end of the day lead us to the promised land of economic resurgence? So
much effort was invested in the preparation of Vision 2010, during the
Ernest Shonekan era of governance. The schemes incorporated in that national
development plan seemed perfect for our primary requirements. Unexpectedly
in line with Nigeria's culture of discontinuity, the "Framework for
Nigeria's Economic Growth and Development 2003 to 2007" was issued in
October 2002. Apparently, this was supposed to be carried along with the
National Rolling Plan 2003 to 2005. Like a belt from the blues, the National
Economic Empowerment and Development Strategy emerged in 2004 in
collaboration with the state counterpart State Economic Empowerment and
Development Strategy (SEEDS). Our prevailing reforms are entirely based on
the prescriptions of NEEDS and SEEDS.
The authors of NEEDS at the presidency affirm the novel plan is
Nigeria's home grown poverty alleviation strategy. It is intended to rest on
four key pillars-reforming the way government works and its institutions,
growing the private sector, implementing a social charter for the people and
re-orientation of the people with an enduring African value system. Nobody,
including the ardent preponents of NEEDS, have explained why recent national
development and rolling plans have been discarded and replaced by
NEEDS. Speculations are rife that NEEDS is the imposition of the World Bank
and International Monetary Fund as one of the conditionalities of approving
our request for external debt relief which our president has persistently
pursued.
There is a great deal of concern on the manner in which the economic
reform programmes are being implemented. Rather than concentrating on the
real sectors of the economy, emphasis appears to be placed on monetary and
fiscal measures such as bank capitalisation and insurance
consolidation. Most citizens will like to see the mega banks co-exist with
medium and small bank as in the United States of America, instead of
inflicting on us only mega banks with a base of N25 billion. It will indeed
be very sad, if small banks are eliminated by this odd policy which hardly
takes into consideration our current fledging state of development as a poor
country, recently declared by the United Nations Organisation as one of the
20 poorest countries in the world. Ongoing economic reforms are
