Then the unemployed skilled men and women will be expunged from towns and cities to leave them clean and manageable.
Nonetheless, this will come with a framework of dynamic mechanisms of delivering implementable policies and determined efforts to industries strategic parts of the rural areas to create jobs for the masses of unemployed people.
Then the urban drift will have began, hence the part-answer to equitably share the national cake!

Original_Search_Title: Keep Burning Illegal Guns
Headline: Uganda: Keep Burning Illegal Guns
Body_Text:
The National Focal Point on Small Arms has announced that it will soon destroy another 10,000 guns. These were recovered from illegal users in the Karamoja region.
The guns will be the second batch to be burnt publicly. The first batch of three thousand was set afire by interior minister Ruhakana Rugunda at a ceremony witnessed by many at Nsambya last month. They are mostly SMGs of the AK47 calibre.
Many Ugandans unaccustomed to such an act would wonder why a poor country like ours would destroy arms worth over ten million dollars.
But this is the right way to go. Those small arms have been responsible for thousands of deaths and if left in circulation they will continue causing more deaths.
The government does not equip its security personnel with weapons captured from criminals.
Purchases of equipment for government departments including security is part of a budgeting process, and does not depend on booty seizures.
These weapons must be destroyed publicly for all to learn that the state does not condone illegal possession of firearms.
It would have greater impact if these guns are destroyed at several sites in Karamoja itself, for this is where the idea would work best that possession of a firearm without license is a serious crime.
The people of Karamoja should witness the hills of AK 47 being set afire to realise how serious the government is over disarmament.
In any case, the average Karimojong needs to know that it is government policy not to license anybody for possession of an AK 47, and so purchasing it, whether by using cattle or cash, is a waste of time as it can never belong to you.
Public destruction of guns must continue especially in Karamoja, until the public accepts that it does not pay to buy a gun without authorisation of the Inspector General of Police.
The same should apply to other contraband seized from criminals. Tusks and other trophies seized from poachers and smugglers should be destroyed in public, preferably in areas where the activities are rife.
Similarly, narcotics seized from the airport and elsewhere should also be destroyed publicly.
Suspicions that security personnel help themselves to these 'exhibits', and that strong court safes contain cassava flour labelled as heroin, are not good for the credibility of the government.
Let us light more fires to burn illegal items, from guns, tusks to narcotics.

Original_Search_Title: Hijra Foundation Calls for Varsity Registration
Headline: Nigeria: Hijra Foundation Calls for Varsity Registration
Body_Text:
Lagos — The Hijra Foundation for Islamic Education, the promoters of Hijra
University, Egbemo-Ekiti, Osun State has reiterated its call on the National
Universities Commission (NUC) to schedule for a final visit to the
institution in order to give a license to begin operation.
Speaking to journalists at the weekend in Lagos, the Executive Secretary
of the foundation, Alhaji Haroon Adekilekun said as today, the foundation
has met all the necessary requirements "to earn us our license."
He explained that in order to enhance the registration of the
university, the planning committee had written an academic brief, which was
approved by NUC on July 7, 2004, as well as met and obtained for the same
purpose some 500 hectres in Igbemo Ekiti- an area predominantly occupied by
Muslims in Ekiti Land.
The proposed university, which the foundation has begun to recruiting
both academic and non-academic staff, Adekilekun said has received the
approval of the government of Osun State to establish the university in the
state as well as promised to provide facilities and infrastructures for the
permanent site.
He therefore called on the NUC to make real their promise to visit the
site of the university by the last quarter of this year in preparation to
wards the final approval of the institution.
"For long sustainable and self financing project of Hijra University
magnitude, the foundation has just cultivated 200 hectares of land, planted
maize and cassava. The proposed university has commenced the commercial
printing in the temporary site, printing and translating of some books from
other languages such as French and Arabic to English Language.
"We have also installed a cyber café for information technology related
course and computer training as well as standby generator installed incase
of power outage, Adekilekun who is an engineer said.
Already, he said the foundation has commenced the School of Advanced
level courses in pure science and social sciences under the auspices of the
British Council and GCE London.
Explaining further on the proposed university, the executive secretary
of the Hijra Foundation said when approved, it is the determination of the
institution to produce academically and professionally sound graduates who
will imbibe the spirit of morality in their choice of career and become
creators of employment and not certificated carriers.
He said Hijra University is established to rank among the world best
institution for the pursuit excellence in knowledge through learning and
research, character building and service to humanity.
"The mission of Hijra University is to provide a conducive teaching,
learning, research and development where staff and student can interact and
compete effectively with their counterparts nationwide as well as
internationally in terms of intellectual competence and to provide
resourceful graduates with high moral standard. It's an institution of
learning where both Christian, non-Christian and Muslims have equal
opportunities," Adekilekun further stressed. He said the board of trustees
(BOT) of the university is peopled by eminent Nigerians including the Emir
of Kano, Dr. Ado Bayero who is the chairman, Otaru of Auchi, Alhaji Aliru
Momo, Alhaji Musliu Smith, former Inspector General of Police, Alhaji A.O.G
Otiti formerly with the Central Bank of Nigeria (CBN) and Dr. S.O Babalola,
chairman of Solbil Group.
Others include Prof, Nurudeen Alao, former vice chancellor of the
University of Lagos, Prof. F.O.A Egberongbe of the University of Lagos,
Alhaja Adiat Fahm, a retired deputy director of finance Federal Ministry of
Information, Alhaji Sheik Ibrahim Sale and Alhaji Haroon Adekilekun of the
Hijra Foundation.
The foundation said the university will take off at Ede town on a
property of 32 classrooms and two big halls in a prime location of the
town.

Original_Search_Title: Sesame Seed Initiative: What Government Can Do
Headline: Nigeria: Sesame Seed Initiative: What Government Can Do
Body_Text:
Abuja — A few weeks ago, specifically late in August, the Vice President, Alhaji
Atiku Abubakar led a formidable official rally that graced a national forum
on sesame seeds, representing government's renewed interest and endorsement
of the latest initiative by stakeholders. The forum was planned to mobilize
resources to address the issue of high-level value addition to sesame seeds
production and processing. This happened at the forum that was facilitated
by the Raw Materials Research and Development Council, RMEDC and the
Strategic Sesame Seeds Project in Abuja. To address this symbolically, the
step taken by Vice President Atiku Abubakar represents a milestone, and
indeed, a giant leap, in creating the framework for a strong policy support
initiative in the sesame seed sector.
The private sector platform and the research agencies that have
severally promoted this course are, no doubt, bolstered and motivated by
this high level show of interest. A momentum has been created. They were all
highly elated at this symbolic show of solidarity by people in
government. Apart from the Vice President, the forum also attracted the
presence of legislators from the National Assembly, ministers and state
governments.
The level of participation at the forum even by foreign entrepreneurs
and investors reveals a bit about how strategic Nigeria can develop her
potentials to be a leading operator in the global sesame seeds market.
Experts in the processing and by-products chain of the sesame industry
in Israel and Italy were present to demonstrate what they have and how
Nigeria could benefit from their experiences. They outlined the steps but
what stands out in all of the counsels that came from these intending
investors is the need for governments in Nigeria to show leadership and
support to the drive and initiatives of the private sector
stakeholders.
This counsel is not only reflective of the position of government in
similar circumstances; it indeed captures the desires and dreams of the
private sector operators, farmers and researchers in the sector of sesame
seeds of the Nigerian economy, which explains the tone of the communique
that captures the anxieties and aspirations of stakeholders at the
forum. The communique recommended that "Sesame seed should be accorded the
same priority as one of the strategic crops on the presidential initiative
programme. This will enable the government to provide an enabling
environment for a sustainable development of the crop".
What this touches on is the need for the sesame sector to receive
similar presidential interest, platform and promotion as is currently
accorded cassava, cocoa and the like. In this respect the the presidential
initiative will begin with the inauguration of a Presidential Committee on
Sesame Seeds that reflects the aspirations of stakeholders as well as drive
the initiative for high-level value addition to that sector of the
economy.
Because of the existing lacuna on access to credit facility for medium
enterprise stakeholders in the sector, it would be needful to equally
empower the envisaged presidential committee to act as an intervening agent
that bridges the existing transactional gap between entrepreneurs and the
lending agencies. It is necessary that govenment commit itself to the
process that speeds up the adaptation and establishment of production lines
within the country to fully take advantage of the existing resources in
Nigeria.
The advantages of this step are obvious but require reaffirmation
here. The farmers will be empowered and supported by the knowledge that a
sustainable and competitive market exists locally for their produce. The
labour chain is enhanced and unemployment is reduced. The effect of this is
the govemment's ability to confront the Millennium Development Goals target
of halving poverty in the country is increased.
Government will equally need to give further impetus to research and
developnient as well as encourage strategic offshore exchange training with
established interests and institutions, particularly in Israel. This is
because capacity utilisation is a question of efficiency, investment flow
and quality of human resources. Just like an entrepreneur on sesame
production line, Rony Lerner observed during the forum, "modern economics
and nations are built on three macro-platforms viz national stratetic
resources, national interest and national cohesion and solidarity.
"Any nation that gets these three platforms right is automatically
perceived as a world power. One of the challenges in developing economies
like Nigeria is to develop the capacity to integrate these platforms into a
working whole." The lesson from this, nonetheless, is that government must
act upon the momentum created at the forum. In doing this, the existing
linkage partnership between the agencies of government and the Strategic
Sesame Seeds Project must be deepened and strengthened for the good of the
economy.
Mr. Willie-Nwosu, coordinator, Strategic Sesame Seed Project.

Original_Search_Title: Govt Donates $21.5m to FAO And WFP for Food Aid And Agricultural Inputs
Headline: South Africa Donates $21.5million to FAO And WFP for Food Aid And Agricultural Inputs
Publisher: Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations (Rome)
Body_Text:
Johannesburg — South Africa will donate 140 million rand ($21.5 million) to FAO and the
World Food Programme (WFP) to support the agencies' operations in southern
Africa, FAO/WFP announced today.
The donation is the third major contribution from South Africa to the UN
agencies since 2003 and comes at a critical time in the gearing up phase of
programmes ahead of the region's lean season, which starts as early as
November in some countries.
FAO will receive 98 million rand ($15 million) for agricultural and
livestock inputs, and WFP will get 35 million rand ($5.4million) for food
aid. An additional 7 million rand ($1.1million) will be donated for the
Southern African Development Community (SADC) Regional Early Warning
System.
"Yet again the Government of South Africa is playing a leading role in
trying to help its neighbours during their time of critical food need," said
Mike Sackett, WFP Regional Director for Southern Africa. "Already many poor
people have very little to eat and their needs are only going to escalate
the closer we get to the lean season, but because it's a cash donation,
we'll be able to buy food locally and quickly transport it to the people in
need."
In 2003 South Africa donated 170 million rand to WFP which was used to
buy 100 000 tonnes of maize in this country. In 2004, the Government donated
100 million rand, which was divided between the FAO and WFP to help people
meet their food security needs.
FAO has supported farmers and their families with agricultural inputs,
the provision of poultry and goats, tools for small-scale irrigation and
vaccines against animal diseases, such as foot-and-mouth, and the creation
of cassava and sweet potato nurseries and backyard gardens, among other
activities.
"Large numbers of people in the central-eastern part of southern Africa
including Zimbabwe, Zambia, Malawi and southern Mozambique are at risk of
food insecurity due to reduced harvests, lack of purchasing power and the
devastating effects of HIV/AIDS," said Anne M. Bauer, Director of FAO's
Emergency Operations and Rehabilitation Division.
"Rural families will need further support to broaden the basis of their
often very poor incomes," she said. "Vulnerable households require better
access to agricultural and livestock inputs. FAO will provide assistance in
livestock surveillance, soil and water conservation and management, gardens,
small-scale irrigation, training in labour- and time-saving technologies,
school gardening and Junior Farmer Field and Life Schools," Bauer said.
Funding shortfall
WFP launched a regional appeal in January this year for $621 million to
assist the region's most vulnerable in Lesotho, Swaziland, Malawi,
Mozambique, Zambia and Zimbabwe over the next three years. However, WFP
still faces an immediate funding shortfall of $152 million to feed up to 9.2
million people through to the end of the next lean season in March/April
2006.
FAO has asked for $16 million to finance its emergency activities in
southern Africa. To date, the agency has only received $3 million.
The situation in southern Africa is considered so serious that in early
August, UN Secretary General Kofi Annan wrote to 27 Heads of State, the
European Commission and the African Development Bank to raise the alarm for
urgent funding to "avert a catastrophe".
"Maize prices are already rising dramatically in most countries in the
region, months ahead of the lean season, which means that many people we
assumed would be able to fend for themselves will need food aid earlier,"
said Sackett. "The South African donation comes at the right time to help
many people who would otherwise face an extremely difficult and long period
without enough food to eat."

Original_Search_Title: Agbogbomefia Calls for Attitude Change
Headline: Ghana: Agbogbomefia Calls for Attitudinal Change
Body_Text:
Ho — The Agbogbomefia of the Asogli State, Togbe Afede XIV, has called on all
Ghanaians to uphold and improve upon good governance, the rule of law
through greater accountability, transparency, tolerance and good attitudinal
values.
This, he noted, would ensure participatory governance, help attain
required developmental goals by way of rural and national development,
encourage the need for strict application and enforcement of laws that would
also ensure discipline throughout the country.
The Agbogbomefia, made this call on the occasion of this year's Asogli
Yam Festival celebration, which coincided with his second anniversary, last
Saturday in Ho.
Speaking on the theme "Attitudinal Change- An Important Prerequisite for
Development", Togbe Afede stated that, the nation's development challenges
are not caused by external factors and neither is debt cancellation the
solution: But a total transformation of attitudes, depoliticization of
development and upholding views of the minority and most vulnerable in
society; hence the need to take into consideration their situation in the
decision making process.
Stressing the importance of the theme, which he said would be his focus
for the year ahead, he said, " My experiences and analysis point to one
impediment- our attitudes.Our development aspirations will prove illusive
unless we change our attitudes."
He indicated that the ideals of good governance has been undermined by
selfishness and greediness among others, demonstrated by few people who
enrich themselves through misappropriating the nation's scarce resources and
in the long run destroying state owned enterprises at the detriment of the
poor.
According to him, " This is why a lot of money is sunk into projects
that are never completed. Local examples include the Ho Sports Stadium and
the Ho- Kpedze road, into which billions of cedis have been sunk over the
past thirty years". Togbe Afede XIV questioned why Sub-Saharan Africa still
suffers relative stagnation while the rest of the world such as India, China
and Malaysia are fast moving forward in development.
He therefore called for a change in the national attitude to education
through an understanding of its purpose of attaining knowledge and
intellectual development, and eliminate the notion that education merely
provides the individual with a number of certificates.
The Agbogbomefia noted that, an equal change in attitude to work was
also essential;hence the need to inculcate in the citizenry virtues of hard
work, positive thinking and perseverance, that would bring about national
development and go a long way to ensure individual success, stressing that "
Praying for divine and ancestral guidance does not assure success."
He intimated that the achievement of the 2015 Millennium Development
Goals (MDG) could only become a reality when the goals are backed by
appropriate plans through right decision taking, a sense of responsibity
that demands that leaders are held accountable for their actions and the
need for them to fight against the culture of dependency in the country, in
a way that would be reflected in their work, actions and
pronouncements.
He said further that, the attainment of national and individual goals
are based upon the elimination of all forms of discrimination and
superstition which impede the ability to accept change and
responsibility. He added that"We need to be less selfish and appreciate that
altruism is the best form of egoism which is consistent with the biblical
teaching, "Do unto others as you would like them to do unto you."
Togbe Afede XIV, who won the 2004 personality of the year award, advised
his fellow traditional rulers to join hands in unity and work together to
provide inspiration, strength and quality leadership that the people expect,
since it was imperative to confront the urgent developmental needs of the
people, adding that, " These are the needs of our time and must be met
during our time."
He urged them to take great pride in their work rather than their
regalia and lead the fight in attitudinal change to ensure sincere pursuit
of national interest that guarantees freedom from all forms of
discrimination and prejudice.
This, he stressed, would enable all to take pride in being Ghanaian, in
order to leave behind a good legacy for generations to come, assuring his
people of a new beginning for prosperity, peace, unity and development among
them and all other tribes in the country, during his reign.
"I want to emphasize again the need for us to change our attitudes lest
we continue to bask in unproductive slogans such as The Gateway to West
Africa, The Energy Hub of West Africa, and the like", he concluded.
On his part, the Deputy Minister for Trade and Industry and Guest of
Honor for the occasion, Hon. Kofi Osei- Ameyaw, noted that, the theme for
the celebration reinforces the conviction of the ministry ,which is that a
drastic change in vision and mindset is the key to development. The minister
emphasised the need to strive for unity that would serve as the bedrock for
economic transformation of communities from poverty to prosperity.
According to him, the survival of cultural and traditional practices
depends to a large extent on the economy and the well being of the people,
making it imperative for the citizenry to exploit economic and business
opportunities within their environment and a successful mobilization of both
human and material, as well as capital assets that are appropriate for
positive change in the country.
The minister announced that the Asogli State has been selected among
others for the processing of cassava into chips and pellets for local
consumption and exports under the Corporate Village Enterprise Scheme (DOVE)
as part of an economic development strategy.
He entreated the District Assembly and strategic private investors to
team up and support the communities to own these private industries and
appealed to the citizens and residents of the area, especially traditional
authorities to take keen interest in the project and make available land for
the cultivation of the raw material base, which is a critical for the
sustainability of the industry.
Hon. Osei-Ameyaw expressed hope for the project, saying, " I am
optimistic that the people of Asogli will accept the challenge and resolve
to mobilize both human and physical resources coupled with current
management and transform their community into a prosperous one."
Present at the festival were Ministers of State, Members of Parliament,
President of the National House of Chiefs, Okyehene and Members of the
Diplomatic Corps.

Original_Search_Title: NDDC Seeks Stakeholders' Support
Headline: Nigeria: NDDC Seeks Stakeholders' Support
Body_Text:
Asaba — Niger Delta Development Commission (NDDC) has concluded arrangement to go into full implementation of its programmes, and called for the full support of all stakeholders in the region.
NDDC Managing Director and Chief Executive Officer, Hon. Emmanuel Aguariavwodo, said this while commissioning the 10.7 kilometer Olomoro road.
He said "to achieve this, the commission is looking forward to more support from all our stakeholders, the oil companies, the state governments and local governments so that we can go into the full implementation of our master plan."
Aguariavwodo said NDDC has earmarked about 50 hectares of land in Isokoland for the cultivation of cassava in support of the presidential initiative on cassava.
On youth development in the region, Aguariavwodo said the commission was involved in a lot of collaborative efforts with state governments, adding that "our sports programme is going to start very soon where all the youths of this region are going to participate, in football, athletics, wrestling and rowing."
All these, according to him, "are to keep the youths off the roads as well as curbing the incessant cases of restiveness," noting that "they are projects earmarked by the commission that would occupy the youths as well as engaging them on ventures that would lead to sustainable means of livelihood."
He said that he was aware that Isoko youths are already engaged in skills acquisition programmes when at completion, they are expected to replace the many foreigners that are brought to this part of our country. He said these youths are already involved in fabrication and welding, jobs that were ordinarily meant for foreigners.
Aguariavwodo commended the oil companies operating in the region for meeting up with their payments schedule to the commission.
Chief Agwariawodo then hinted that the commission was working in conjunction with oil companies operating in the region, noting that: "They trust us in whatwe are doing, they support our efforts, we are growing from strength to strength in our collaboration with the oil firms and that is something we are so happy about. We are encouraged by their assistance."

Original_Search_Title: 'We're Being Ignored,' Say Voters Outside the Capital
Headline: Liberia: 'We're Being Ignored,' Say Voters Outside the Capital
Body_Text:
Gbarnga — Liberians living outside the capital are complaining about being neglected by presidential candidates, bent on pouring resources into capturing the Monrovia vote but making only fly-by-night visits to the rest of the country.
"Our would-be presidents think that Monrovia is the whole of Liberia," said Johnny Smith, a shoe repairer in the central town of Gbarnga. "None of them has taken time off to tell us in detail how Liberia or our county will be developed."
Further north, in the village of Palala, 59-year-old Kemah Farzue was also critical of candidates making fleeting visits.
"When they are passing in their cars, they either drop T-shirts or photos, but none of them has stopped to sit or talk with us," she told IRIN with a sigh.
Observers say it is not surprising that politicians have focused so much attention on the capital.
Firstly that's where a large chunk of the votes are.
Of the 1.35 million Liberians registered to vote in the 11 October polls, more than a third are in Monrovia and the surrounding Montserrado County, partly because thousands of people who flocked to the capital during the 14-year war stayed on when peace arrived in August 2003.
Another major factor in politicians getting out to all areas of the country is access.
Liberia, a heavily-forested nation, has only two paved roads, rains have turned its other dirt tracks into muddy quagmires, bridges are still in ruins and some people are forced to travel by canoe just to reach a doctor.
"Candidates were already talking in May about concentrating their campaigns on the 'Monrovia-Ganta' corridor," the Brussels-based think tank Crisis Group said in a report this month.
The four counties of Montserrado, Margibi, Bong and Nimba that lie along this route -- one of the two paved roads -- are home to two-thirds of registered voters.
However, an IRIN correspondent who travelled from Monrovia to Ganta this week found that even along this presumed campaigning axis, once you got outside the capital people felt they were being sidelined by the wannabe presidents.
And complaints came not only from residents of the smaller villages dotted along the road but also from those living in the main towns of Gbarnga in Bong County, a key stronghold for former president Charles Taylor, and Ganta in Nimba County.
Posters and convoys
"They only sent their campaign posters to us here," said Jimmy Dahn, a youth leader in Ganta.
Dotted around the town are posters for just five of the 22 presidential candidates -- former international soccer star George Weah; veteran opposition leader and former UN official Ellen Johnson-Sirleaf; one-time deputy Senate leader Charles Brumskine; Varney Sherman, a corporate lawyer and confidant of the interim head of state; and Roland Massaquoi, the man carrying the flag for Taylor's old party.
"When they (the candidates) do come, they just ride around in long convoys with loud speakers saying 'Vote for me' without telling us what they have for Liberia," Dahn said.
Analysts from the Carter Centre and the National Democratic Institution, who are supplying some of the 200 international election observers, note that most of Liberia's political parties have developed around the personalities of key leaders rather than around policies.
"Party organisation is centralised within Monrovia and very few parties have sufficient resources or organisational structures to campaign at the grassroots level," they wrote in a pre-election report earlier this month.
"With so many registered political parties participating in the elections and a centralised system with limited resources to disseminate messages, many voters are confused as to the differences among the parties," the report said.
Almost every resident you meet in the interior says they are yearning to see the candidates' platforms made public, particularly when they cannot attend the mass rallies where pledges are made in Monrovia.
"We too here in the interior are voters and we cannot be treated differently," said Martha Bartuah, taking a break from selling cassava at the main market in Gbarnga.
Peace, jobs, roads, schools and hospitals. The list of what Liberians want is identical wherever you go. But while everyone in the interior can reel off their wishes and hopes for this war-scarred country, ask them who they will vote for and many will admit they still haven't decided.
"Those who are running for the presidency must now start to make their platforms public so we can choose who really wants to transform this country," said cassava seller Bartuah.
Some residents worry that the lack of interest in more rural areas in the build-up to the election will be translated into indifference to their plight after the ballot.
"When they are lucky to be elected, they may not even step here again," said Smith, the shoe repairer from Gbarnga. "How will they know our problems?"
[ This report does not necessarily reflect the views of the United Nations ]

Original_Search_Title: FG Expands School Feeding Scheme
Headline: Nigeria: FG Expands School Feeding Scheme
Body_Text:
Abuja and Lafia — President Olusegun Obasanjo yesterday explained why Nasarawa State was
chosen to lead the pilot programme of the national school feeding
programme. He also stated that government was set to expand the
programme.
He said positive results of the pioneering efforts of the Nasarawa State
government on the initiative encouraged the Federal Government to endorse
the programme, he added.
The president who spoke at the formal launch of the school feeding
programme at Laminga Primary School near Keffi, Nasarawa State, commended
the governor Dr. Abdullahi Adamu for what he described as "the bold steps he
has taken through sending samples for laboratory tests preparatory to local
production".
He disclosed that Federal Government has already mapped out a blueprint
"based on the expected concrete results to be derived from the Nasarawa
State experiment for the transformation of the school feeding programme
nationwide."
Obasanjo who poured encomiums on Governor Adamu, said it takes great
courage, vision and commitment to undertake a pilot project on such a
critical issue, which seeks to address malnutrition of the population,
especially in children.
He stressed that the issue became more critical when recent studies have
shown that a substantial number of primary school children in Nigeria do not
eat enough to ensure proper school attendance, attention and performance at
school, while almost half of school children aged between 7-13 years are
underweight.
Government, he said, considered the situation unacceptable and
constituted a number of presidential committees on dairy development, citrus
and cereals production to boost food security, and nutritional status of
pupils in public schools, with Nasarawa State setting the pace.
The president tasked the governor to intensify his current efforts at
local production of the imported south African "Nutri-sip" food supplement,
pledging that the federal government will fully back at the processes for
the local production.
A lighter mood at the occasion came when the president, accompanied by
Governor Adamu, and visiting Rivers state governor Dr. Peter Odili joined an
artiste and danced for a few minutes to a sustained applause by the large
crowd.
Governor Adamu, in his opening remarks disclosed that the programme,
when fully operational, will target 27 million school children, is part of
the visions of President Obasanjo's promise to make Nigeria a great land,
modern nation.
He declared that it is only local production of the food supplement that
can guarantee survival of the programme adding that importation is capital
intensive.
To achieve objective of local production, he disclosed that his
government signed a memorandum of understanding with technical partners -
TETRA PAK in November last year for the supply, and to ensure local
production, using agricultural produce.
He disclosed that laboratory tests of powder samples of millet, maize,
sorghum, cocoa and cassava sent to the United States of America (USA), shows
that cassava, cocoa and yellow maize are suitable for the formulation of the
nutritional feed, he added.
The state government, he insisted is willing to throw its doors open to
all local and foreign investors interested in the project, pledging to
provide them with necessary facilities and assistance for the smooth
take-off of the project.

Original_Search_Title: MP Wary of GM Crop Trials
Headline: Kenya: MP Wary of GM Crop Trials
Body_Text:
Nairobi — An MP yesterday asked the Government to suspend trials on Genetically Modified (GM) crops, pending development of strong biosafety policies and legal framework.
Mr David Nakitare (Saboti) also said further field trials on genetically engineered crops should be stopped until the technology was proved safe to the environment.
He said many people in Africa would be affected if the technology was found dangerous to man and the environment.
"At least 85 per cent of people in the continent practice small-scale agriculture. This is why it is important to tread cautiously on GMO crops," he said
He praised the Government for ordering the destruction of GM maize under field trial. The MP added that the same policy should be implemented against genetically modified cotton, cassava and sweet potatoes.
Nakitare pointed out that this was necessary because the country did not have bio-safety laws.
He also urged stakeholders, farmers and consumers "to take more interest to understand the issues around GM crops and participate in the debate on genetic engineering of crops."
The MP was speaking at a civil society strategy workshop on GMOs in African Agriculture at the Kenya School of Monetary Studies, Nairobi.
"I urge you to explore other practical and sustainable alternatives to increase food production in Africa and, most important, how the increased food can be brought to the table of poor people in Africa."
The meeting was attended by representatives of over 20 countries in Africa and international organisations.

Original_Search_Title: FG Urged to Establish Export Village
Headline: Nigeria: FG Urged to Establish Export Village
Body_Text:
Lokoja — Kogi State Commissioner for Commerce and Industry, Mr. Dan Yinusa has asked the Federal Government to establish an export processing village in the state, which would cater for several tons of agricultural produce that was turned out annually by farmers.
The commissioner gave the advise when a team of the Nigerian Export Promotion Council visited him.
Yinusa said the state was exceptionally loaded with deposits of solid minerals and agricultural products but lamented the absence of important market centers where farmers could sell their produce and maximise their gains.
The absence of such facilities, resulted in farmers transporting their produce to neigbouring states which now boasted of being the major producers of such produce.
Meanwhile the governor had mapped out plans to site cassava processing machines in each of the senatorial districts in response to NAPEP's request for increased outlets for the marketing of cassava.

Original_Search_Title: Cost of Doing Business - Ghana Ranked 82nd in the World
Headline: Ghana: Cost of Doing Business - Ghana Ranked 82nd in the World
Body_Text:
In the just released 2006 Cost of Doing Business Report, the World Development Report ranked Ghana 82 out of 155 countries as a good place for doing business, compared to the 86th position it holds this year.
While this may be below expectation, it represents a definite improvement in the country's business environment. Indeed, Ghana's position on these rankings has been improving since 2004.
The same World Development Report further shows that, while the procedures for starting a business in Ghana remain the same, the time taken to register a business has reduced from 85 days in 2005 to 81days in 2006.
The Minister for Private Sector Development, Kwabena Bartels, in an interview, told the Business Chronicle that the cost of starting a business has also been reduced from 87.5% of income per capita to 78.6%. The cost of registering a property has also reduced from 4.1% of equality to 3.7%.
Touching on some positive results of the business environment in Ghana, he said MAERSK was to build a container Trans shipment Depot in Takoradi and this facility will be the biggest of its kind in sub-Saharan Africa.
He announced CTTC was also building the biggest spaghetti production factory in Tema. Government has given approval for the first diamond-cutting plant by a private company and the training of personnel for the facility has already begun.
Gold Fields Ghana has also given 5kg of gold to the college of Jewellery in Accra for training and the department of jewellery in KNUST and the ministry of mines and forestry is establishing a jewellery village in Tema.
To achieve all these, the government introduced the presidential special initiative (PSI), which is a key pillar of government's vision for the development of the private sector.
According to the minister, market reforms alone would not get Ghana to its destination.
The government, according to the minister, recognizes the problem to be partly one of time lag, as the impact of the market reforms may not be immediately manifested.
The PSIs were therefore conceived in 2001 as growth poles to address this dilemma by fast tracking the growth and development of targeted sectors, while implementing the much-needed market reforms whose impact may be slow in coming.
He further said there were four-PSI areas presently implemented. These, he said, were cassava starch, oil palm, salt, textiles and garments. New PSIs being explored include cashew, sorghum, cotton and maize.
Moreover, government has developed a policy framework for the PSIs that clearly articulate government's facilitation, handholding role and the private sector's role in implementation.

Original_Search_Title: Nasarawa: Obasanjo Launches Pupils'feeding Scheme
Headline: Nigeria: Nasarawa: Obasanjo Launches Pupils'feeding Scheme
Body_Text:
Lafia/Abuja — BARELY 24 hours after kick-starting the Home-Grown School feeding programme in Abuja, President Olusegun Obasanjo will today at Lapunga, Nasarawa State launch the project in the state. He revealed that seven million children were still out of school nationwide.
The feeding programme is an attempt by the Federal Government to "increase the physical and cognitive development of younger generation in primary and junior secondary school level for the advancement of learning and education.
The programme which began in Nasarawa State last July, according to the state governor, Abdullahi Adamu has progressed very well.
At the first National stakeholders workshop which held at Ibrahim Abacha Youth Centre, Lafia, Gov. Adamu said the state has gone into partnership with a South African firm, Tetra-Park (West Africa Limited) for production of a nutritional supplement called "Nutri-Sip" for supply to schools across the state.
The food supplement comes in pack of 250 ML and contain 30 nutrients of vitamin A,B,C and E as well as iron, zinc and Selenium for the healthy development of children.
Though the food supplement were imported, efforts by state to make the programme indigenous and ensure its sustainability through local production has reached advanced stage.
The break-through in the process according to the Governor was achieved this year when sample of maize, millet and cassava were tested by one of Tetra-pack partners, Solea in St Louis USA.
The company is currently formulating cassava into basic, flavoured prototype drink for use in school, to ensure the programme's sustainability in the country.
Meanwhile, flagging off the programme in Abuja yesterday President Obasanjo disclosed that a total of 2.5 million pupils in 12 states are expected to be covered under the pilot phase.
According to him, the 2.5 millionj pupils representing 10 per cent of the total population of primary school children who will have access to one highly nutritional meal per day.
He explained that the move was borne out of the need to meet targets of Education for All (EFA) and Millennim Development Goals (MDGs) by 2015.
Though over 25 million children are currently enrolled in public primary schools, the president revealed that more than seven million were still out of school with girls population put at 4.3 million or 62 per cent.

Original_Search_Title: Nigeria's Lower Life Expectancy
Headline: Nigeria's Lower Life Expectancy
Body_Text:
THE 2005 Report of the United Nations Development Programme, UNDP, on
the decreasing life expectancy in the country reflects the wobbling state of
the nation. In the Report, Nigeria led the pack of 20 lowest Human
Development Index ratings in the world. All the countries behind Nigeria are
African countries. Niger Republic that is presently battling with famine is
last.
Of 177 countries rated, Nigeria is a disappointing 158. Nigerians' life
expectancy is now 43.4 years - a considerable reduction of seven years from
its rating of 51 years last year. In sub-Saharan Africa, Seychelles ranked
first and 51st among 177 countries. The sweeping poverty of the past six
years, which government keeps denying, was detailed in the Report.
Despite this disturbing revelation, the government says the situation is
under control. There are indicators that the nation might further slide in
future ratings.
The Gross Domestic Product, GDP, reflects the poor standard of living in
the country. The welfare of the people has been worsened by a poor policy
driven food crisis, shown in the cassava export craze.
Nigerians can no longer afford to eat well. Sadly, the government is
satisfied that foreign reserves are getting fatter while Nigerians are dying
of hunger, diseases and burgeoning illiteracy. The effect of these portends
an ominous sign on the well-being of the Nigerian.
As a result of the unstable mind of Nigerians, their blood pressure rise
by the day and they are afflicted with so many avoidable diseases without
effective health care delivery policy to take care of them. Education, law
enforcement, security, housing, transportation, and drinking water are other
sectors where poverty reigns. Inadequate or obsolete facilities in these
areas are veritable contributors to the short life span among
Nigerians.
We expect the Federal Government to initiate policies that would create
a comprehensive social services delivery system, instead of the constant
denial of international statistics that point at the stark realities of the
Nigerian situation.
Ordinary Nigerians, not the politicians, are interested in living a
better life within the resources that God has bestowed on this country. The
mismanagement of these resources, the non-consideration of the peoples'
interests in the formulation of policies that are supposed to benefit the
people, all contribute to the low HDI rating.
The UNDP report is not about politics, it is all about peoples' lives,
Nigerian lives, in this case, and a Nigerian government that claims an
interest in the people, should act on it speedily.

Original_Search_Title: South West : Workshop Validates 33 Projects
Headline: Cameroon: South West : Workshop Validates 33 Projects
Body_Text:
- A workshop grouping top management functionaries, stakeholders, politicians and traditional rulers has validated 33 projects aimed at improving the quality of life for the people of South West Province.
Validated projects include: protection of water catchments, intensification of cap side (small insects on cocoa) control campaign, making available medium size oil mills, supporting traditional micro-financing houses, encouraging processing of fruit juice and cassava, standardizing and marketing, community to farm roads avoiding head-loading The list will be forwarded to the Ministry of Agriculture and Rural Development (MINADER) for intervention.
The validated list according to the out-going delegate of Agriculture and Rural Development, Mr. Mboge Mboge was conceived and formulated by the Provincial Delegation of MINADER with assistance of the six Divisions of the Province.
Addressing workshop participants at the Pan African Institute for Development West Africa, Buea during the opening session 12th September, the Permanent Secretary of the South West Province, Mr. Ndode Mesape stressed the need for validation of projects aimed at reduction of poverty, encouraging economic growth and sustainable development. "There should be designed to meet up with problems, needs and expectations of the population. Particular attention be paid on increasing production and productivity, improving marketing, creation of jobs, farm-to-market roads, self employment and sustainable management of natural resources".
Reiterating on human development, the Secretary General said "human development involves the strengthening of the personality and accusation of knowledge and information with the poor to regain confidence in themselves and the ability to their way up from poverty".

Original_Search_Title: LG Gives 150 Indigent Women Soft Loans
Headline: Nigeria: LG Gives 150 Indigent Women Soft Loans
Body_Text:
Abakliki — More than 150 indigent women, made largely of widows, have benefited from soft loans offered by Ishielu East Local Government Area in order to help women start small scale businesses.
This was made known by chairman of the local government area, Hon. Joseph Nomeh when he briefed newsmen who toured the council area on yesterday.
Nomeh said the greatest problem that faced the council was poverty and explained that the main objective of the council was how to enhance the economic well-being of the people of the local government area by providing them with the opportunity to improve themselves and find ways to better their lot.
He disclosed that the first batch of beneficiaries of the loan scheme received N35,000 each and although it was called a loan, the council did not expect that the women would pay back but said it wanted to ensure that the loan was judiciously utilised to start one small business or another.
Nomeh expressed happiness that most of the women had opened businesses with theirs as petty traders and restaurateurs.
He said another batch of 150 women would also receive their loans before the end of the year and noted that if the council helped women to stand on their own economically, they would be able to support their children in their academic pursuit and other endeavours.
The local government chairman also stated that more than 90 percent of the people of the council were subsistent farmers and to promote their efforts and increase their yield, the council had given them fertilisers free and has encouraged them to concentrate on cultivation.
of cassava, which has become a cash crop, so that they would make more money after selling their produce.
To enhance easy evacuation of farm produce, the council has graded access roads in various villages in the local government area so that farmers could now take their crops to bigger markets in Abakaliki and other nearby metropolis for sale.
It was to enhance easy movement that the council decided to repair the major bridge that links the local government and its Onicha counterpart at the cost of N3.5 million.
Nomeh said that he was prompted to repair the bridge, which has claimed five lives, so that people could move easily from one area of the council to other parts of the state.

Original_Search_Title: 'Agagu is God's Gift to Ondo
Headline: Nigeria: 'Agagu is God's Gift to Ondo
Body_Text:
Lagos — Aleader of Alliance for Democracy (AD) and member of House of Representatives, Hon. Jaiyeola Ajatta has commended the development initiatives of Ondo State Governor Olusegun Agagu.
He described him as a gift to the people of Ondo State. "Your Excellency, you are a gift to Ondo State. You have proved your mettle. Be rest assured that we are solidly behind you and your administration and we will always support your course."
Ajatta stated this while on a visit to the governor in his office in Alagbaka, Akure. The lawmaker, president of Akoko Development Group led a delegation of prominent Akoko indigenes to show their support for Agagu's administration which Ajatta said " has restored public confidence in governance through quality service delivery and has championed relative peace which exists in the state as at today."
The Akoko Development Group, which has been in existence for over forty years, prides itself as a socio-cultural and developmental organisation with membership that cut across the length and breadth of the Akoko nation in the Northern senatorial zone of the state.
"Over the years, our group has partnered meaningfully with successive administrations in the state and has variously offered our ideas and criticism as a way to entrench purposeful governanc."
Therefore, Ajatta told the governor " we have followed with keen interest your action plan for development of Ondo State as whole and Akoko nation in particular and we are delighted at the progress recorded by your government in the area of development of infrastructure, the micro credit schemes and most especially the cassava cultivation revolution. These efforts are critical in the fight against poverty and are right steps towards building a virile and stable economy"
Ajatta praised the governor for the promotion of Free Trade Zone , Deep Sea Port and the Millenium Development Village project
In his reaction, Agagu thanked the group and enumerated some of his administration's policies and achievements which he said were made possible by transparency and judicious use of available resources.

Original_Search_Title: Private Sector Fails to Exploit Business Environment - Kwamena Bartels
Headline: Ghana: Private Sector Fails to Exploit Business Environment - Kwamena Bartels
Body_Text:
Although the improvement in the business environment is to empower the
private sector to drive the growth of the economy, firms in Ghana have not
been able to fully exploit the opportunities created to achieve this.
They are constrained in their ability to participate in markets because
of weak competence and capacity, including lack of skill and knowledge.
People who do business invest in their companies to expand production,
ensure that their products meet international standards, and adopt
up-to-date technology, management and marketing systems.
"It is only with a proper structure and system that the private sector
will be able to take advantage of the opportunities that an improved
environment presents," the minister for Private Sector Development and
President's Special Initiative (PSI), Kwamena Bartels said this at the 'Meet
the Press' series in Accra yesterday.
The minister stated that government's intervention through the PSI, is a
vision for the development of the private sector and it recognizes the fact
that market reforms alone will not get Ghana to its economic
destination.
"Indeed the assumption of policy reforms to free productive forces, has
clearly not worked very well for developing countries like ours," he
hinted.
According to him, four PSI areas are presently at various stages of
implementation. These he said are cassava starch, palm oil, salt and
textiles and garments.
Touching on the public-private dialogue in the country, he disclosed
that to achieve these, corporate income tax rates have been lowered to 28%
this year from 32%, promising this will be further reduced to 25% in
2007.
To further free up working capital from firms and to improve their cash
flows, the withholding tax rate on the supply of goods and services will be
reduced from 7.5% to 5%. The national re-construction levy is also to be
lowered and gradually phased out by 2007.
Again, he added that to free up loanable funds for the private sector,
the bank of Ghana has been persuaded to reduce the secondary reserve from
35% to 5%.
The minister said his ministry has supported the implementation of the
business sector advocacy challenge fund to strengthen the quality and
quantity of the dialogue.
Mr.Bartels also disclosed that government is working to increase the
private sector participation in the provision of public infrastructure and
service.
Cabinet has approved policy guidelines designed by the ministry for the
implementation of public-private partnership in Ghana. The approved
framework takes into consideration the public procurement act, financial
administration act and requirement for project design and
implementation.
Another point the minister highlighted was on the impact of reform
efforts on the economy, saying the BoG survey on business confidence
indicates that business sentiments about their own prospects and those of
the economy in 2005, remain generally optimistic.
"The central bank's composite index of economic activity increased by
17.3% between June 2004 and June 2005."
Figures from BoG also indicate that the bank credit to the private
sector has been increasing quite rapidly, relative to GDP. At the end of
June 2005, credit to the private sector stood at 12.5% of GDP, up from 11.5%
in June 2004. This is the highest in five years.
The Ghana investment promotion centre registered 54 new businesses in
the first quarter of this year, compared to 48 in the first quarter of
2004.
Some larger businesses have folded up in the face of global competition
and unfavourable terms of trade.
However, many smaller businesses have been established during the
period.

Original_Search_Title: Nigerian Children: Goodbye Malnutrition, Welcome Manna
Headline: Nigerian Children: Goodbye Malnutrition, Welcome Manna
Body_Text:
A new chapter of hope opens for Nigerian children next week as the national school feeding programme kicks off in Nasarawa State. But how far can the programme go in meeting the needs of the average school pupils who are endangered by malnutrition?
A recent news report that Nigeria has been ranked the second poorest country in the world by the World Bank came to many Nigerians as a shock bordering on bewilderment. The shock was heightened as Nigerians tried to come to terms with claims that their beloved country could only fare better than Ethiopia which was seriously ravaged by drought and starvation a few years ago, while lagging behind such countries as Madagascar, Chad, Mozambique, Guinea Bissau, Nepal, Niger, Congo Republic and Burundi.
"How can they rank small countries like Guinea Bissau, Chad and Niger ahead of Nigeria in economic development?" queried a Lagos-based legal practitioner, Moses Odigie, who considers the claim as not only misleading and mischievous but also insulting to the image of Nigeria as the giant of Africa. He did not stop there. "Is it not these same countries that we have been feeding all these years and assisting financially anytime they're in trouble, like we are presently doing in Niger where starvation has taken the centre stage due to acute shortage of food? In spite of the economic problems we have had in the past was there anytime that we have had problem of food shortage such that we cried out to the world to bail us out?" he had asked repeatedly in an aggrieved voice.
But while the ranking remains a debatable issue, one issue that is not in dispute is that successive years of worsening economic down- turn have taken a serious toll on the living condition and well-being of the average Nigerian families such that for them the recommended three-square meal has become a mirage rather than a reality. At a time many families can barely make ends meet, preaching to them the essence of balanced diet is like trying to sell contraceptives to a eunuch.
The tragedy of the situation, in the estimation of most observers, is that children are usually the worse for it since it is required at this growing stage of their lives that they be fed on rich and nutritionally balanced diets. Indeed a visit to any average Nigerian home may confirm the shocking reality that children are fed on what is readily available or what the family can afford at any point in time and not according to their nutritional requirements.
A similar visit to any public school, whether in the city or rural community, may also reveal the poor feeding profiles of Nigerian school children. Take the case of 10-year old Lasisi who is a pupil of a primary school in Agege, Lagos. Every morning before he joins the exodus of other school children trekking to their respective schools in the area, he is given the paltry sum of N10 which he spends either to buy buns or "puff-puff" along the way or to buy rice without meat during break time at school. When he returns from school at about 2.pm later in the day, Lasisi will be lucky to get an additional N10 to buy cooked beans with which he and his siblings will eat a meal of gari soaked in water. What passes as a full meal can only be provided in the evening or night when the whole family assemble for the usual supper-cum-dinner.
With this kind of feeding profile, Lasisi certainly cannot be blamed for having a stunted look, a stature which some people in the area say does not do credit to his age. And if the claims of relevant experts on the link between nutrition and mental health are anything to go by, Lasisi needs not worry when his teachers call him "Olodo" due to his declining academic performance. The fact that he and most of his school mates are poorly fed and malnourished should be blamed for their being dull in rising to the challenges of their school works.
The good news for Lasisi and other school children like him in Nigeria at the moment is that the dawn of an end to their suffering is around the corner as government is presently spearheading an initiative to provide them a new lease of life through feeding. By next week, precisely on Tuesday, September 27, 2005, the pilot scheme of the much-talked about national school feeding programme is expected to kick off in Nasarawa State, with its official launching by President Olusegun Obasanjo. Under the programme as espoused by relevant officials, government intends to provide one nutritionally-balanced meal each school day to primary school pupils in the country, beginning with the Nasarawa State pilot scheme which is expected to cover 2.8 million children.
Said to be the brain-child of President Obasanjo who has also been described as its moving spirit, the programme is being touted as a home-grown offshoot of the New Economic Partnership for Development which is targeted at ensuring that at least 30 million Nigerians have access to a nutritionally rich or balanced food everyday. In fact, the Nasarawa State governor, Dr. Abdullahi Adamu, made bold in vouching for the commitment of the President towards the success of the programme. "I am privileged to know that Mr. President is anxious to make a positive difference in this vital sector of the our national development. I also know that he has put his shoulders to this plough and he is not looking back," Dr. Adamu had informed during a workshop on the national school feeding programme last month in Lafia, the Nasarawa State capital.
But what consideration(s)gave rise to this initiative and in what way will it benefit Nigerian school children? "The primary objective of the national school feeding programme is to help nurture healthy children to grow into healthy adults mentally and physically equipped to take on the onerous responsibilities in the public and private sectors of our national life in the future," so says Governor Adamu who is also the Chairman of the Presidential Task Force on Dairy Production.
Speaking further, he said the programme, especially as being packaged as a pilot scheme in Nasarawa State, was intended "to specifically prevent hunger and correct known deficiencies amongst pre-school and school-going children". According to him, the programme is also envisaged to increase enrolment and retention of school children in schools, raise the attendance levels in schools, enhance the performance of children for sustainable educational development and reduce morbidity and mortality rates among school children. "Our commitment to the programme is consistent with our belief that what we do with the health and education of our children today would determine the kind of legacy we have for the future generations of our people," the governor has said.
The programme actually took off last June with a test-run which was anchored on the objective of providing balanced diet to the school children. "We are using ultra-high temperature treated(UHT) 250ml nutritional supplement called Nutri-Sip for the programme. Nutri-Sip contains 30 trace elements that meet the daily requirements of growing children. Our ultimate aim is to balance the consumption of such staple foods as maize, millet, sorghum, cassava and other starchy foods with high carbohydrate content with Nutri-Sip. We strongly believe, on expert advice, that although this approach is simple, it is consistent with the implementation of similar programmes in other countries," Dr. Adamu informed.
Speaking also on the programme, its coordinator in Nasarawa State, Dr. Dan Iya explained that the benefits to accrue from its implementation would further the objectives of the National Economic Empowerment and Develop-ment Strategy (NEEDS).
According to him, the fallout in the improvement in the nutritional status of school children and increase in school attendance would also indirectly lift the economic and social status of the community. "For example, the produce that is being used for this nutrient supplement is to be localised here in Nigeria.
And this localisation will lead to the boosting of agriculture in our state and the country at large. So, we see the school feeding programme in a totality of the picture of our children developing mentally and for the total good of the society at large," Dr. Iya said.
He also informed that the programme started off in the state with 7,000 pupils in about six schools, adding the plan was to extend it to other schools in the state with an estimated total primary and junior secondary schools population of about 450,000 pupils.
On the safety and health benefits of the nutrient supplement Nutri-Sip, Dr. Iya who incidentally is a medical doctor assured that it was both safe and nutritious. "The Nutri-Sip contains protein, carbohydrate and so on for the development of the child. And it has several advantages in that it is treated so that the bacteria are killed. It is packaged in such a way that it has a shelf life of at least six months to one year. It is also packaged in such a way that whatever a child gets in town A is the same quantity and quality that another child gets in town B. What I mean is that it contains the same elements, so that a child in one village is eating the same thing as the child in another village," he informed.
Meanwhile, the state according to Governor Adamu has so far taken delivery of about 3.6 million units of Nutri-Sip out of the 10 million units it ordered which are currently being stored in different warehouses in the state from where they will be distributed to the schools.
But while Nasarawa State is preparing to go full blast on the school feeding programme from next week, 12 other states in the country are also expected to follow suit this month and may decide to use the Nasarawa State experience as a guide.

Original_Search_Title: Irate "Bayam Sellam"Sollicites Governor's Help
Headline: Cameroon: Irate "Bayam Sellam"Sollicites Governor's Help
Body_Text:
They were last Wednesday chased from the roadsides by the forces of law and order.
A group of female business woman popularly known as "Bayam sellam" last Wednesday stormed the office of the Governor of the Littoral Province demanding help. The "Bayam sellam" from the "gare market" in Ndokoti are protesting against a decision by the Divisional Officer for Douala III, Yampen Ousmanou to forcefully chase them from the area. According to Madam Yonta Sylvie, "The misunderstanding between the "Bayam sellam" and the administration began when the D.O issued a decision demanded that those involved in the sales of foodstuff such as tomatoes, cassava, onions, carrots etc should move to the new market site called "Marché Syncatex" situated a few metres from the area. After several months of sensitisation, some of the "Bayam sellam" voluntarily moved to the area. While there, they realised that things were not moving. Not only did most customers it difficult to get to the market because of bad roads, their security and that of their goods was find not guaranteed. Furthermore, they incurred a lot of expenses while trying to transfer their goods to the new site. Because of these difficulties, some of them decided to comeback to their original positions.
While the D.O and his collaborators were still trying to convince them on the necessity of staying at the Syncatex market, some shop owners at the road site, decided to start selling foodstuff in the stores and road sides where some of the "Bayam Sellam" left. This move sparked protest from the "Bayam sellam" who have decided to stay. They saw it as a move to prevent customers from getting to the Syncatex market. "After a protest by the "Bayam Sellam" in the market, we issued and order demanded that all shop owners should stop selling foodstuff. After weeks of grace, some of the shop owners and a handful of "Bayam sellam" who were still dragging their legs refused to obey. Reasons why, a crack down campaign was carried out during which shops were close and reluctant "Bayam sellam" at the road sites chased away. The D.O explained.
It is therefore this corrective act which led to the arrest of some shop owners and "Bayam sallam that pushed Madam Yonta Sylvie and her colleagues to march to the Governors Office in Bonanjo last Wednesday. Without letting them in order not to perturbed the serene working atmosphere, the protesters where blocked at the entrance of the gate by police. A few minutes after, an official in the governor's office asked them to put their complain in writing so that they can be forwarded to the chief administrator of the province for action.

Original_Search_Title: How Edo Man's Dignity Was Restored, By Lucky Igbinedion
Headline: Nigeria: How Edo Man's Dignity Was Restored, By Lucky Igbinedion
Body_Text:
FAR from being just a socio-cultural event, the 2005 Convention of Edo National Association in the United States and Canada, provides an opportunity for an aggregate evaluation of the welfare of its members in North America, and more importantly for synthesizing appropriate responses to and intervention in the exigencies back home. The theme of the convention, "Excellence for the new Millennium", is indeed an interesting one. Were it merely a matter of numbers, it would seem extravagant in the first case that we are proposing to define the next 1,000 years, which is what a millennium is. A century is long enough, and by current life expectancy, it is doubtful that any one of us here today would be around at the dawn of the next century. Yet, there is inherent in theme of the convention, a desire to look beyond the here and now. It is at once indicative of our desire to shape our collective destiny, to hand over to succeeding generations a legacy and a history upon which they can build and catapult our collective heritage into a higher orbit.
Excellence for the new millennium, therefore, means that we must face up to a new world, a world of challenges and of opportunities. This we must do with fortitude, with determination in our hearts, and with a patriotic fire that burns brightly and helps to illuminate our paths and bring glory to Edo land and its people. From the regular feelers that I get, there are two issues that bother people most about Edo State. These are roads and security. My government has already taken positive steps to address these problems. A number of urban roads in the state capital have been fixed. The notorious "Morocco and Spain", as Upper Sakponba was once referred to, is gone. The flooding at "Teachers' House" at Upper Siluko is being tamed. Upper Mission, Medical Stores, Uwelu, and Uwasota have been rehabilitated. In July, the state Executive Council approved almost N2 billion for the second phase of road rehabilitation.
As soon as the rains abate, work will commence. As part of our measures to reduce the cost of road projects, my administration is partnering a firm in Trinidad and Tobago on the production of cold-mix asphalt that is an important component of road resurfacing.
In addition, because the ownership of roads is shared among the Federal, State and Local Governments, my government has directed local councils in the state to construct a minimum of two kilometres of asphalted roads each year. Ordinarily, we have no control over Federal roads, such as the Uselu-Ugbowo Road. But road users do not want to know who owns the road; they just want it motorable. This is why I took steps to visit and implore the Federal Minister of Works to expedite repairs on the failed portion of Uselu-Ugbowo Road. Work has begun on that road, and the traffic snarl has eased considerably. The other happy news is that the Federal Government recently approved the completion of the Benin bye-pass, which would take inter-city traffic out of the inner city.
The security situation is a concern to you, but to us all. But the problem is very often exaggerated. What is true, though, is that we could do with better policing. We operate a Federal system of government, yet the police force is run as though we were a unitary government. The police force is not decentralized. This, together with inadequate training as well as welfare, hampers the efficiency of the police. Edo State presents a unique scenario. We are a junction state that provides access to the north, south, east and west. A number of the crime incidents involve cross-boundary outlaws who are chasing luxury buses some of whose passengers carry large amounts of cash. Indeed, last December when some of these criminals operated in Benin City, it was clear that they were from out of state. We were not and have not been helpless. My administration set up a military-police joint patrol task force, a move that cost the state government at least N6 million per week. Happily, the crime situation has abated considerably. We are not the crime capital of the world.
One useful lesson to draw from the reactions to the experience under review is for me to suggest that the Edo National Association should send, from time to time, an official delegation to Edo State, to see things for themselves, hear our own side, and exchange ideas with us on the best forward for our state. That way, we can be true partners in progress. Knowing ourselves the way we do, we recognize that baseless rumours, especially about government and its officials, are traded freely, and this can very often be an impediment to efforts at mobilizing support for public causes.
Common ancestry
Let me place on record my deep appreciation of the immense contributions by the sons and daughters of Edoland, to the amelioration of the economic situation back in the land of our common ancestry. The widespread manifestation of their positive intervention in this regard can be found in their regular remittances to dependants. However token the amount, the distortions in Nigeria's macro-economic policy environment are such that every dime or dollar goes some way in meeting certain needs. In addition, frequent remittances have spawned beneficial consequences in ways other than mere reliance on handouts. In Edo State today, as in most other parts of Nigeria, the money transfer business has become one of the more visible products of banks. Indeed, so lucrative is this slice of banking business that one of the older generation banks has dedicated an entire branch in Benin City solely to Western Union Money Transfer.
Upon assumption of office in my first term in 1999, I made the restoration of the dignity of the Edo person a priority. In this regard, I had the ready support of my wife, Eki, who then formulated it as her pet project under the banner of Idia Renaissance. This became one of the early sins of my administration, as allegations flew around that my government had come to deny the people their means of sustenance. This was not unexpected, considering the ignorance and short-term profit that drove the illicit and inhuman trade. Regrettably, this initial resistance to extirpating human trafficking in Edo State conditioned some people in their response to other governmental policies and programmes that were ultimately for their own benefit. We have never been deterred, though. And I am happy and proud to say that our relentless drive to restore the dignity of the Edo person has begun to yield rich harvest.
With a law in place, coupled with an aggressive re-orientation programme, fewer and fewer of our girls now find any attraction in heading to Europe to trade their bodies. In recent times, as His Royal Majesty, Omo N'Oba N'Edo Uku Akpolokpolo, has rightly observed, girls who are being repatriated from the prostitution ring now falsely claim Edo as their state of origin. Upon rigorous interrogation, they are revealed to hail from elsewhere. A critical adjunct of our campaign against human trafficking is a rehabilitation programme for the returnees as well as skill acquisition to discourage potential recruits. In the past four years, more than 1,000 graduands have been produced by Edo State Skills Acquisition Centre on the outskirts of Benin City, where courses are available in computing, catering, fashion design and hairdressing.
The very important lesson to draw from the foregoing is that we must keep our economy in shape and expand employment opportunities for our teeming youths and others in the labour market. At least from 1963, when the then Mid-Western region was created, Edo State, like old Bendel before it, was referred to as a civil service state. That appellation reflected not just the quiescent and relatively placid nature of our dear homestead, it was a tag that showed the absence of or minimal commercial activities and industrialization. Regular jobs were to be found in the civil service. Unfortunately, years before I assumed office, the ranks of the civil service had been decimated by frequent rounds of retrenchment, and those who remained had little motivation for quality service delivery.
Therefore, I recognized early that unless we set Edo State on the path of industrialization and increased commercial activities, we would remain largely a backwater state that is often the first to sink when buffeted by adverse economic conditions such as we find from time to time with the vagaries of the macro-economic environment. My administration has been a facilitator in opening up the economic space. Nobody visiting Edo State for the first time since 1999 would fail to notice that commercial activities have picked up, although it is still early in the day.
I must admit that, on the face of it, it seemed incongruous that at a time of privatization and general divestment of government from business activities my administration was forging ahead with the establishment of industries. However, our guiding philosophy was, and remains, one of demonstrating what is possible in Edo State. We do not intend to establish and run the industries forever; instead, our plan is to within the shortest possible time privatize them, with the hope that, as private sector operators run the enterprises with their typical shrewdness, the businesses would remain afloat, jobs would be created and sustained, while the government's revenue base would increase with the payment of taxes. We evaluated the Nigerian economy, as well as that of our state, and came to the conclusion that the economy is in dire need of value addition. We are still too much of primary producers who then open up our foreign reserves to finance the importation of finished goods. It is interesting, in fact, that the petroleum sub-sector, which is the nation's chief revenue earner, still suffers from low value addition by indigenous entrepreneurs. Whether in the upstream sector or downstream, local content is quite low, which explains why for more than a decade now Nigeria has suffered the embarrassment of dependence on the importation of refined petroleum products, because the state-run refineries are unable to utilize their installed or rejuvenated capacity.
For us in Edo State, we targeted some of our primary produce and minerals for value addition. Thus, there is a Fertilizer Blending Plant at Auchi; a Cassavita Plant at Uromi, and a Fruit Juice Factory at Ehor. Recently, I set up an eight-member inter-ministerial committee to facilitate the completion and commissioning of these industries and other on-going projects. We are well on course, and in the months ahead, these industries will become operational and subsequently put on the block for the highest bidder. This again will be in accord with our orientation that it is possible and profitable to do good business in Edo State. Nevertheless, in the light of government's inadequacy to go it alone, there will have to be increased and sustained partnership with the private sector. The Edo State Government will ensure a conducive policy climate for private sector investment to flourish. There is protection of investment. We are in a democracy, which is being strengthened and deepened. Property rights are constitutionally guaranteed and respected. With a population of over 120 million, Nigeria is the largest market in Africa, and the exponential growth in the telecommunications sub-sector bears testimony to the potential volume of business in the country. Labour is cheap and available.
The polity is generally stable, the occasional hiccups notwithstanding.
But in this regard, I must say that it is acknowledged that Edo State has been, and will be, one of the most peaceful states in Nigeria. For us as indigenes, we must also recognize that where there are no businesses to absorb those on the job queues, crime can only be reasonably managed. Therefore, my quest for a better Edo State is a call for professional, patriotic and business participation.
With Edo people's extensive contacts in the Americas and elsewhere, they can do for Edo State, and Nigeria at large, what outsourcing has done for China and other Asian economies.
