needed to control the further spread of the grasshoppers," said Okasai
Opolot, the assistant commissioner of crop protection in the Ministry of
Agriculture.
The grasshoppers feed on cereals like millet, sorghum, maize and rice
and attack heads, leaves and soft parts of the stem. They are also affecting
pasture grasses. Infestations are concentrated in a trench of cultivated
land about 3km from cattle grazing areas of Mogoro, Gariam, Toroma and
Omodoi sub-counties.
In Mogoro, infestation covers about 320 acres, while in Gariam, it is
affecting over 500 acres. Toroma, the latest to be affected has 80 acres
infested.
"The pest is spreading into the cultivated fields," said Okasai.
He said farmers will experience serious food shortage because cassava
and sweet potato growing in Magoro and Gariam sub-counties is minimal due to
Karamojong cattle rustlers, who uproot the crops prematurely.
Okasai said they were controlling further spread of the grasshoppers
adding that they had already distributed insecticides and spray pumps to the
affected villages in addition to sensitising the masses on how to apply
it. However, the insecticide reportedly kills the nymphs, but is ineffective
on adult grasshoppers.
He said breeding spots for the grasshoppers have been identified in the
uncultivated sandy and loam soils adjacent to the grazing land and a natural
enemy has been identified as a wasp (Tachysphexerynnis, Turner).
"Farmers should dig a trench between grazing grounds and crop fields,
which will check the movement of the hoppers from their breeding spots,"
said Okasai.
He also advised farmers to use aerial spraying by low flying aircrafts
or tractor mounted sprayers. Okasai said this will effectively kill the
grasshoppers on the crops and in their breeding grounds.

Original_Search_Title: Nigeria, Brazil Sign Bilateral Agreements
Headline: Nigeria, Brazil Sign Bilateral Agreements
Body_Text:
Brasilia, Brazil — The relationship between Nigeria and Brazil yesterday took a major leap with the signing of bilateral agreements by Presidents Olusegun Obasanjo and liiz Inacio Da Silva.
Both leaders signed the agreement after a private session and official talks shortly after the arrival of Obasanjo at Planalto Palace which bordered mainly in four major areas of trade and investment, technical cooperation, cultural revival, and regular political consultations.
Obasanjo's Senior Specail Assistant on Media Matters, Mrs. Oluremi Oyo, told newsmen at the end of the meeting that Brazil was "interested in solidifying the cultural ties between us (Nigeria) in the exchange of teachers to teach African history, cassava and tropical fruits."
Obasanjo in his speech, however, during the lunch hosted by Da Silva, said "we are happy to note that we are on the march towards re-igniting the golden years of Nigeria-Brazil relations of the 1980s. The value of our bilateral trade then peaked at over $2 billion and our joint cooperation profile covered virtually every facets of human activity
"The successes achieved so far especially in our collaboration on the production of anti-retroviral drugs in Nigeria and the joint agricultural research projects in cassava and tropical fruits production are reassuring and sure to expand the frontiers of our cooperation," he added.
Obasanjo said "Brazil under President Lula has been a true friend of Africa and a development partner. Let me therefore with all sense of candour salute the life-long personal commitment of President Lula to fighting the course of people weighed down by poverty, hunger, disease, racial discrimination, injustice and other forms of social and political inequities and inequalities."
Obasanjo noted that "in the same vein our renewed diplomatic efforts to revive the south Atlantic Zone of peace and cooperation and our commitment to convene the inaugural summit of leaders of Arica, the Caribbean and South America must not waned.
These will certainly have positive implications on the peoples of Africa, South America, the Caribbean and the rest of the developing world.
Obasanjo on arrival was received by Da Silva at the Presidential Palace. He was treated to a colourful parade by the Brazilian military after a 21-gun Salute.
While in Brazil, Obasanjo is expected to be part of Brazilian Independence celebration. He would thereafter meet with African Ambassadors in Brazil.

Original_Search_Title: Nigeria's Search for Alternative to Petrol
Headline: Nigeria's Search for Alternative to Petrol
Body_Text:
According to the U.S. Department of Energy, if the worldwide rate of consumption of oil remained constant at the current amount of 24 billion barrels of oil per year, we would run out of oil in 2040. As the amount of oil within the earth continues depleting the price will continue to rise with social-political storms trailing it, like the trouble brewing in Nigeria over the recent increase in pump price of petrol.
But many countries with far visions have since found plant-based fuels alternatives to fossil fuels and a few of them are already smiling across the seas exporting them and earning billion of dollars.
In France, ethanol is produced from grapes that are of insufficient quality for wine production. Prompted by the increase in oil prices in the 1970s, Brazil introduced a programme to produce ethanol for use in automobiles in order to reduce oil imports. Brazilian ethanol is made mainly from sugar cane. Pure ethanol (100% ethanol) is used in approximately 40 percent of the cars in Brazil. The remaining vehicles use blends of 24 percent ethanol with 76 percent gasoline. Brazil consumes nearly 4 billion gallons of ethanol annually. In addition to consumption, Brazil also exports ethanol to other countries. It has been estimated that Brazil would export between 1.9 to 2 billion barrels of ethanol at a price of $25 per barrel, even as rising crude prices may lead to significant increases in ethanol.
It was therefore not surprising, the enthusiasm that energy watchers received the news recently that by January next year, Nigerians' cars would be guzzling millions of litters of ethanol blended with Premium Light Spirits (PMS) saving the country not less than $245 million (about N32.8billion) from the importation of petrol, in the preceding months.
The Group Managing Director (GMD) of the Nigerian National Petroluem Corporation (NNPC), Engr. Funsho Kupolokun, said that from next year 10% of the fuel consumed in Nigeria would be blended with ethanol imported from brazil, but that shortly after, it will be followed by development and commercialisation of ethanol fuel in Nigeria, which will involve cultivation of large-scale plantations of sugarcane and cassava and construction and operation of distilleries for the ethanol production of fuel-ethanol.
This would be made possible through an MoU singed between the NNPC and Petrobrass (Brazilian equivalent of the NNPC) and COIMEX, Brazil's largest exporter of ethanol.
The GMD had explained at signing venue of the MoU that the objective of the Nigerian ethanol programme was to firmly establish a thriving industry using sugarcane, cassava, etc, as a means of triggering significant employment generation, while improving the quality of automotive gasoline in Nigeria.
"Ethanol is emerging as an important motor fuel in view of the Kyoto Protocol Agreement. As a high-octane, water free oxygenate, fuel ethanol is one of the tools available for fighting air pollution from vehicles.
"It is also a greenhouse gas emission friendly fuel which reduces global warming, and ensures complete elimination of the use of octane enhancing tetra ethyl lead which is a toxic pollutant."
He also noted that besides providing improved income for farmers, the use of ethanol fuel would generate additional revenues for government, create new jobs, while the residue, 'bargasse can be used for power generation within distilleries with the potential to export surplus power to the national grid.
But the real news is that Ethanol, the major content of Ogogoro( the Nigerian local parlance for illicit gin) would be produced in millions of litters per day in Nigeria from Cassava and Sugar cane in an industry that is expected to enjoy sharp growth due to an already established market, favourable climate, abundant workforce, and most of all, available capital.
In an exclusive chat with Vanguard, the Group General Manager, Renewable Energy Division of the NNPC, Engr. Onochie Azubike Anyaoku affirmed that the MoU signed with the Brazilians would enable both sides evaluate soil and climatic conditions as well as help in fashioning policies in that aspect using the vast experience Brazil has garnered as the world's largest producers of ethanol with experience of over 30 years.
He enthused that in the next four months, the NNPC would have upgraded their storage facilities to blend the two fuels. He said the responses of banks and other interested investors have been very encouraging.
"The Nigeria Cereal Research Council, Nigeria Raw Material Development, and agricultural research bodies in Nigeria have long concluded studies in this area to show that Nigeria can successfully develop a thriving Industries from these crops" adding the Brazilians would merely add value to their research.
He, however, posited that we should expect to produce ethanol fuel locally from within 24 and 30 months.
He affirmed that all cars manufactured between 1979 and now can are compatible with at least 10% ethanol blended PMS.
Apart from reducing the cost of petrol fuel, Engr. Anyaoku said that the idea is to create employment opportunities in the rural areas and bring infrastructure to rural folks, to meet the aspiration that President Olusegun Obasanjo has for rural dwellers.
He affirmed that Nigeria has relatively more suitable soils and climate than Brazil with the workforce that is idle.
Sugar Cane in Nigeria is grown in the savannah belt of the country just Brazil has its expansive 240 sugar cane estates and 400 varieties in its Pampas.
The advantage of building ethanol distilleries is that the sugar cane waste provide the energy that drive electricity production in the factory, cutting out the notorious power problem in Nigeria.
For Cassava, the ethanol fuel programme may just be timely.
Nigerian cassava production is by far the largest in the world; a third more than production in Brazil and almost double the production of Indonesia and Thailand. Cassava production in other African countries, the Democratic Republic of the Congo, Ghana, Madagascar, Mozambique, Tanzania and Uganda appears small in comparison to Nigeria's substantial output, studies have shown.
And there is already an initiative to ensure that Nigeria hit a cassava export based US$5 billion a year by 2007 through the cultivation of 150 million tonnes of cassava by the end of 2006.
And that may not be an empty ambition as gari, made from cassava has jumped from N6 in 1999 to N100 now, all due to its unprecedented export value.
If the ethanol projects gear up, the price may further drive up, and farmers would hate that Biodiesel
But, apart from Cassava, Sugar Cane and other starch based crops that can produce ethanol for blending with PMS Nigeria is also a potential heaven for Biodiesel.
Biodiesel is an alternative fuel source derived primarily from oils found in the nuts and seeds of plants, corn, and soybeans (vegetable oil.) It is made from 80-90% vegetable oil, 10-20% alcohol (methanol or ethanol), and 0.35-1.5% catalyst (sodium hydroxide or potassium hydroxide.) It is a stable fuel, performs well on all diesel engines while producing cleaner emissions, and can be mixed with petroleum diesel fuel.
Biodisels usage has proven that without fossil fuels diesel based engines can run efficiently and much more environmentally friendly.
Research and development of such options are likely to become more desperate as galloping crude oil prices and the anxiety over green house gases have forced developed nations to keep finding cheap and secure alternative to fossil fuels.
According to a Time report, studies in the growing of Sunflower in Europe for biodiesel, fore example is five times for expensive than in Equatorial Africa.
This generally puts Nigeria as a veritable potential producer of these oil, if we get our acts right.
Today, the World is looking at Brazil with envy as it is set to net $50 billion from ethanol export.
That would come from its 330-ethanol plants. But that is nothing compared to the $6 billion dollars that investors are planning to plough in Brazil in the Next five years.
And that is the reason to be optimistic about setting up an ethanol-fuel industry -- private investment to that tune or more could flow into Nigeria too.

Original_Search_Title: Nigeria in a 'Cat And Mouse Game'
Headline: Ghana: Nigeria in a 'Cat And Mouse Game'
Body_Text:
Accra — as calls for negotiation on trade embargo intensifies
The Nigerian trade embargo on Ghana is still in place. And the revised list of Ghanaian items that are outlawed from Nigerian markets, as at June 30, 2005, now includes water.
But intense efforts by Ghanaian government officials over the past two months to get their counterparts from Nigeria sit at the negotiation table have drawn blank response.
Information reaching Public Agenda indicates that an official letter which the Ministry of Regional Cooperation and NEPAD sent to its counterpart ministries; the Federal Ministries of Commence and Industry, two months ago, met a wall of silence.
A Senior Economic Officer at the ECOWAS Secretariat of the Ministry of Regional Integration, Mr. Bonaventure Adjavor confirmed this in an interview with Public Agenda recently.
"We have written a letter to the Nigerian government requesting negotiations on the ban, but they are yet to reply," he said.
But the barefaced refusal by the Nigeria government to negotiate on the trade embargo is reportedly raising tensions between the two nations. The inability of ECOWAS to amicably resolve the trade rift between the two countries has also left many people questioning its relevance. Some Ghanaians had even called for the withdrawal of Ghana from the regional body.
Though Hon. Kwabena Adusa Okerchiri, Chairman of the Parliamentary select Committee on Foreign Affairs and Hon. John Mahama, Minority Spokesperson on Foreign Affairs and MP for Bole Bamboi had said such an assertion was far fetched, they agreed that there was urgent need for the heads of states of the two nations to initiate negotiations to avoid the healthy relationship that exist between the two nations going sour.
The Executive Secretary of ECOWAS, Dr. Mohammed Ibn Chambers is recently reported to have also stated that other ECOWAS States were not comfortable with the ban and had urged Nigeria to open the gates for negotiation. But the ECOWAS scribe himself was not sure what the outcome would be even if Nigeria accedes to negotiate.
As at June 15 this year too, Ghana was to contest the ban at the review meeting of the Head of States on the African Peer Review Mechanism (APRM) in Abuja, Nigeria.
But though the APRM review has come and gone, the trade embargo is still in force.
Patience is however said to be running out and tempers flaring as the threat of collapse of many Ghanaian companies as a result of the Nigerian ban become a near reality. Textile companies have taken the lead, with Juapong Textiles already gone, while Akosombo Textiles Limited (ATL) which recently downsized its workforce by about 60% is in distress.
But it is not only the textile companies which are feeling the pinch of the trade embargo. Other companies are too. The Nigerian ban is extensive and affects almost all industries in the country.

Original_Search_Title: Dr. Elizabeth Acheampong - a Scientist Par Excellence
Headline: Ghana: Dr. Elizabeth Acheampong - a Scientist Par Excellence
Body_Text:
Accra — Deep in the heart of the University of Ghana, among the tall huge green
trees adorning the premier University and lying behind the Balme Library is
the Botany Department.
Here a little forest for research remains. A few green houses meant for
breeding and developing different seeds are tucked neatly in the
forest.
Inside the green house Senior lecturer and Botanist Dr. Elizabeth
Acheampong takes students round pineapple suckers which are being developed
for Ghanaian pineapple farmers and exporters.
She stops every now and then to explain the different types of
pineapples and the temperatures required to make the seedlings develop well
to the students.
She also tells them how to prevent plants from catching infections in
the nursery.
Dr. Acheampong is one of the few Ghanaian female scientists who have
been very successful in their chosen careers.
Born in December 2, 1950, Dr. Acheampong has become a force to reckon
with especially among pineapple growers and exporters.
Unlike some scientists whose research work remains on the shelf,
Dr. Acheampong has been a woman of the field, supervising the development of
different species of food crops and ensuring that they get the best quality
of produce for international as well as local consumption.
In an interview with Public Agenda, Dr. Acheampong said "Research has
brought to the fore all the nutrients plants get from the soil and these are
now produced in the laboratory."
According to her, different salts are mixed and together with vitamins
and other chemicals. They are then taken through several processes including
sterilization to produce the tissue crops which are nursed in a laboratory
and then later transferred into a nursery for farmers and crop
breeders.
"Others are for purposes of storage," she said, adding, "We do not want
to lose our good food crop material such as the different types of yams and
as such we preserve them for posterity."
Together with her assistant Mrs. Bernice Asante, they have been
producing several pineapple suckers for commercial farmers.
According to her, a few years ago, Bomart Farms, a pineapple exporting
company approached the department to produce a new breed of pineapple known
as MD2 for their clients in the international market who had then given the
company two years to change their export from the Smooth Cayenne pineapple
to the MD2 or lose the market.
"This was because the price of the Smooth Cayenne which is common in
Ghana had failed at the world market and there was high demand for the MD2
which is touted as having a very deep yellow colour, good taste and nice
aroma," Dr. Acheampong explained further.
"At the same time the Horticultural Association of Ghana (HAG) also
approached us saying they also wanted the MD2 pineapple suckers developed
for them," Dr. Acheampong said.
To facilitate the production of the MD2, Bomart Farms provided her
outfit with a Laminar Flow Cabinet and other equipments which are needed for
the production of the suckers valued at 70,000 dollars and also hired
additional staff.
The HAG also provided an Autoclave, a machine used for sterilization
which cost 18,000 dollars.
In spite of all these measures, the department could not meet the market
demand for Bomart Farms due to staff strength.
As a result, the management of the farms decided to establish a private
commercial laboratory near Nsawam under the supervision of
Dr. Acheampong.
The farm is now producing over 300,000 MD2 suckers a month.
Recounting how she started her research at the University of Ghana,
Dr. Acheampong said there was no tissue crop Laboratory at the department
when she started work and as a scientist trained in tissue culture, she
needed to do something about it.
"The Japanese government gave us one equipment and we also borrowed some
from other departments," she said adding, "We started with one room and one
Laminar flow," she said adding, I also begged for chemicals from a friend at
a Conference I attended and we had to borrow bottles and tissue culture
vessels from other departments."
With only two shelves at the Laboratory at the beginning, the department
can now boast of a modern Laboratory which has been established in a new
building put up for the department by the UN University's Institute for
Natural Resources for Africa, to help train its students from South Sahara
Africa in plant tissue culture technology.
So far, Dr. Acheampong says 49 students have been trained from 12
countries.
A product of Aburi Girls Secondary School, Dr. Acheampong is married
with two children.
She had her first degree in Botany in 1976 at the University of
Nottingham, in the United Kingdom. In 1978 she did her Masters degree at the
University of Birmingham. Here she received training in Conservation and
Utilization of plant Genetic resources. Her project work was on germination
studies in Yams.
For her Ph. D in 1982, also at the University of Birmingham in the
United Kingdom, Dr. Acheampong's research project was on multiplication and
conservation of Cocoyam Germplasm by application of Tissue culture
methods.
In 1988, she was a participant observer at the Association of
Commonwealth universities Workshop on Management Development for women in
Bombay India.
She also attended a workshop in 1993 on Fundamental aspects of
recombinant DNA technology at the University of Ghana Medical School.
Dr. Acheampong was in 1995 at the advanced training programme for
partner countries on Biotechnology and related techniques for the
Conservation and use of Plant Genetic Resources and Improvement of Crops
organised by the German Foundation for International Development (DSE) in
Germany.
Dr. Acheampong acted as a consultant for UNESCO where she prepared a
paper on Biotechnology survey of Ghana for UNESCO's Regional Office of
Science and Technology in Nairobi, Kenya.
Again she was a consultant for the Canadian International Development
Agency (CIDA) where she was commissioned with Evelyn Twumasi, to write a
paper on "Women's Programme Needs Assessment Survey," for the Association of
Commonwealth Universities in London.
In 1992, she was again commissioned by the International Tropical Timber
Organisation (ITTO) and the Forestry Department of the 31st December Women's
Movement to write a paper on Land and Tree Tenure Systems -Implications for
Rural Forestry Activities which was presented at an international workshop
on women and forestry in Ghana.
Her research work includes Invitro mass propagation and delivery systems
for pineapple, protocol development for oil palm tissue culture, protocol
development for cyro preservation of yam and Frafra Potato and Tissue
culture methods for rapid multiplication and preservation of yams, cocoyam,
plantain and cassava.
She was also a member of a Team of Experts at Ad Hoc Panel Meeting on
Project Proposal on feasibility Study for the Development of Tissue culture
Conservation Facility, UNU/INRA, Accra, Ghana.
Dr. Acheampong has also worked for the Food and Agricultural
organization and has worked closely with Friends of the Earth, Ghana. She is
a member of several associations and serves on many boards.
At the University, she teaches Physiology, Plant anatomy, floral Biology
and biological interactions at the undergraduate level.
Dr. Acheampong who is also the Deputy Hall Warden for Volta Hall also
teaches tissue culture, biotechnology, pollution and climate change at the
M.Phil in Botany level, while teaching cyropreservation and Plant tissue
culture at the Ph.D level.
"Our department needs a bigger standby generator, and screen house to
ensure that Ghanaian farmers get the best of crops," Dr. Acheampong
says.

Original_Search_Title: Nigeria to Earn $1m From Ginger Export - Maj-Gen Nom
Headline: Nigeria to Earn $1m From Ginger Export - Maj-Gen Nom
Body_Text:
Lagos — AT least one million dollars can be realised anually from ginger export, if the commodity is well harnessed, the National President of Ginger Association, Maj.-Gen. Yunana Nom. has said.
Maj-Gen. Nom told newsmen in Nagoya, Japan, that Nigerian ginger had been rated as one of the best in the world because of its oil content and pungency.
According to him, statistics show that between 300,000 tonnes and 400,000 tonnes of ginger is traded at the world market at a price of 1,300 dollars to N1,500 dollars freight on board.
He said Nigeria could capture a large share of the market for the product if well-developed.
He said one major problem of the association was the unhygienic way in which members handled the product due to ignorance.
Nom also said that because of the nature of the product, mechanical methods of cleaning and slicing the ginger was required to meet international standard.
He, therefore, called for government's assistance in procuring the machines for distribution to the farmers as loan.
The President said it had also been observed that foreigners formed the habit of going to villages to buy the commodity themselves.
He urged the government to help discourage the practice, saying such an action is not allowed anywhere in the world.
On the investment forum, held on the sideline of the exhibition, Nom expressed disappointment over the turnout, saying, "most of the Japanese businessmen we expected were not actually there".
"The Nigerian embassy ought to have contacted the right people knowing fully well that the various commodity associations were invited.
I expected to have seen match-making sessions so that people can sit down and talk," he said.
"If we are unable to strike a deal I'm not sure the money we spent in coming here would have been justified."
The commodity associations that sent representatives included, coca, cassava, rubber, sesame seed and wheat. The President of the Enugu Chamber of Commerce Industry Mines and Agriculture was also there.
Ginger is grown in commercial quantities in Southern Kaduna and thrives in other states of the federation as well.

Original_Search_Title: Anambra: 'Tribunal Verdict Vindicates Me'
Headline: Nigeria: Anambra: 'Tribunal Verdict Vindicates Me'
Body_Text:
Lagos —  George Moughalu was the gubernatorial candidate of the All Nigerian
Peoples Party (ANPP) in Anambra State in the 2003 elections. He lost out and
was in the tribunal to challenge the declaration of the result which gave
victory to Dr. Chris Ngige of the Peoples Democratic Party (PDP).
The tribunal threw out his petition, but proceeded to declare that All
Progressive Grand Allianceâ??s candidate, Peter Obi, actually won the
election. At an interactive session recently in Abuja, Moghalu puts the
tribunal judgment in perspective among other issues. Chukwudi Nwabuko was
there. Excerpts
Following the recent verdict of the tribunal in Anambra, declaring the
APGA candidate as the winner of the 2003 governorship elections, what is
your reaction to that verdict?
Like I have said at least for more than two times, let me repeat it
again, that the judgment has further re-affirmed my position that there was
no election, abinitio in Anambra State in the proper context of the word
election. There was none, so this has gone further to reaffirm that
position. Basically that is it, just to confirm that their claims about
winning elections in Anambra State is false, which means it is applicable to
many other places.
If there was no election, how come that even the ANPP made claims to
having won the elections?
If you look at it from the perspective of participation, involvement and
the few areas there were voting, because we have instances. For example,
your colleague sitting here was in Nnewi during the elections in 2003 and as
at 12 o' clock, I was going to vote with him and a lot of other colleagues
of yours who came to monitor the election and on our way, they were
announcing the results of Nnewi North local government area and that the
turnout was incredible.
The same way they announced Ekwusigo result. Meanwhile we went to
Ekwusigo and there was nobody. At the end of the day, results were turned
out and in a situation like that, you call it an election? If my own case
was so bad that I couldn't vote for myself and I went within the time limit,
voting was supposed to stop at 3p.m, I went there by 12.15 p.m, if there is
no other truth I have said, I was in the company of journalists, who were in
the position to say it is not true or it is true. At the end of the day,
before we could understand what was going on, they had already started
announcing results on radio.
I know that you went to court immediately. Most Nigerians are not aware
of the facts, what evidences did you produce at the court?
In fact, the amusing part of the court issue is that I was not even
allowed to present evidences because the moment we filed our case, it was
struck out by the tribunal on the basis of non-joinder. I know, though I am
not a lawyer, that the Supreme Court had ruled that the issue of non-joinder
is not a ground to strike out a petition. But I was a victim, even at that,
in the phase of that glaring fact, the tribunal still went ahead and struck
out my case.
So when your case is struck out, how do you present facts and figures
that is available to you?
Because we had a lot to say, we had a lot of documents to tender, and
there was a lot of information we had, but the point is that I wouldn't want
to be misunderstood, somebody mentioned, 'is it because you want to be a
governor?'. For me like I said ab initio, running for governor is not a do
or die game, but for me, it is an opportunity to express a passion, a
passion for service and I have always said you can serve in other
capacities, not necessarily as governor. I have been availing myself of that
opportunity at various times as opportunities come up. I take advantage of
it to try and be of service. So there is no desperation at all, but the
truth has to be said.
If you watch my position since 2003 up till date, it has been consistent
that the entire thing called election in Anambra State, is a selection
process that is marred in controversy, a charade, and a miscarriage of
justice.
If there was no election, how come APGA candidate Peter Obi was able to
present some documents showing the election results?
The tribunal was acting based on information available to it. At the
same time also, the PDP man had documents to show, but don't also forget
that one of the principal actors in this entire episode had come out to say
he rigged the election. I wasn't the only one he told. I read it in the
newspapers.
What is the way forward now that APGA candidate has been declared
winner?
I wish them goodluck. My concern is for Anambra State. My pain goes out
to my people and that is why I am one of those who keep praying for the
state, praying for the actors, including myself, because we need to pray and
ask for God's intervention.
As for Ngige, he has to express his right. In this situation, you cannot
advise somebody not to take full advantage of the law. It is not fair advice
to give. You allow the person to take advantage of opportunities available
to him. If at the end of the day, it works for him, fine and good, if it
doesnâ??work for him, it is also well and good.
What you just said now cut across the country, because on that election
day around 10 o'clock or 12 noon, election results came from Rivers State
and many other states. Why was it so easy for Nigerians to accept this?
Because we were the intimidated group. We had no choice and the
machinery of government, those controlling the security apparatus, made it
imperative that you had to be quiet. There was nothing you can do.
Do you envisage a repeat of what you called a charade in 2007?
I don't pray so. Because we may get to a point where you see people's
patience being elastic. When it is pushed to the limit, you cannot
guarantee, it will get to a point where it will snap-off and people will say
if it means dying, let's die to protect our position. Because the worse part
of corruption is electoral corruption, that is the worst part of it. Because
this is the only time you refuse the people's wish, the only time you make a
nonsense of the desire of the people to choose their leader. It is the
biggest pain the people can go through.
Now that we are in this situation, what is the way out? You talked about
electoral reforms, what is the proper way to go about it to ensure that
there are no more rigging?
First of all, the principal actors must make up their minds that we want
to change the system. Like I said even before the elections, when some of us
said we had no confidence in INEC, a lot of people misunderstood us, they
thought we were being personal. We were not being personal, but we are
looking at their antecedents. We are looking at the actions they had taken
and there is no way you can plant cassava and expect to have yam during
harvest. That someone is a repentant thief for example does not mean that
you never stole, what it means is that you have changed, but it does not
change the fact that you were once a thief. So it is a matter of the people
who are going to play the role in the electoral process to be determined
that they want to offer Nigerians a sincere opportunity to choose their
leaders at all levels. It behoves also on the actors themselves, including
myself to come into this contest with an honest desire to serve. If in your
attempt or your offering yourself to serve and the people say no, wait, it
could be your turn next time, we must develop a culture of being honest,
being sincere, being committed, being firm in our positions and at the same
time also, if you are the winner, be magnanimous in victory because whether
you like it or not, the loser has something to offer. At the end of the day,
you find out that if all resources are pulled together, it goes to the
benefit of the people, because I have always said that in any situation of
governance, both the man in government and the opposition are both in
government. The one in government is in government to keep to electoral
promises made. The other one is in government by way of ensuring that
promises made are kept, in both situations, they are both in
government.
At the federal level, how has ANPP acted as a check on PDP to ensure
that the party in power keeps its promises to the elctorate?
I think we can still do better, but I always use any opportunity I find
to appeal to the media to help the opposition by giving it a teeth to
bite. If the media does not give equal reporting opportunity to the
opposition, there is no way the opposition can play its role to everybody's
satisfaction. If we finish talking now, it is not published, it is as good
as a joke, but if a governor smiles in his house or quarrels with his wife,
it comes on front page news. I am not saying that it is not right, because
the positions they occupy provides them standard opportunity over and above
their contemporaries, which is alright, but I am appealing to the media to
endeavour to balance it, both the print and electronic, because in balancing
it, you are protecting the overall interest of the average Nigerian. You
will be doing a great deal of favour to the man on the street, but if you
don't balance it, if it is skewed in favour of the one in government, then
there is no position for the one outside to check the one in government, at
the same time, it means therefore you are doing a lot of disservice to the
people.
How can we mobilise to ensure that electoral malpractices are
eliminated?
We have to go the extra mile to educate the people massive
enlightenment, to educate them on their rights, that one thing is to vote,
the other is to protect your vote so that, that which you want to see is
allowed to manifest in concrete terms. Because if an enabling playing ground
is provided, candidates will have the opportunity of reaching out to the
people they want to lead and enter into contractual agreements and
obligations with them.
Like when I was running for governor in Anambra State, I had the
privilege of visiting the 177 communities that make up the state. I visited
all the local governments, the markets, talked to the people, and visited
traditional rulers. In the course of these visits, you sell your manifesto,
as you sell your programme. They make demands. You enter into contractual
obligations and when you now enter into office, you are bound to keep these
things, so that when you come back the next time, either asking for support
for yourself or supporting a candidate, they will believe you because that
ís the man they trust. Because the agreement you reached with
them was kept. But a situation where people sit in a bedroom and write
results and a man who is abroad comes back on Friday and wins on Saturday,
like we had in 2003, people who didn't even participate in election, who had
no posters, people who did not even come back to their local constituency,
some of them were in Abuja, they flew into their states on Friday night and
won on Saturday. Even Jesus Christ campaigned to convince the people. He
went to them. He took a message to them and they listened to him and that is
why some of us are very worried about 2007.
The biggest problem we are going to face in 2007 is voter aparthy,
because their votes don't count again and if your vote doesn't count, why
should you keep on wasting your time?
You can as well not spend tax payers' money to organise elections. You
can as well share the money amongst the people, sit back in your office,
write results, announce and use the machinery of government, the security
agencies to force it down on the people like we had in 2003 What kind of
scenarios are you looking at in 2007.
Some people are saying some parties will dissolve, or that PDP may
disintegrate?
I think there will be constant evolution. What you must understand is,
one, you are talking about people's interest, you are talking about human
beings, you are not talking about fixed structures, you are not talking
about tables, you are talking about human beings and their interests, so it
will continuously evolve, the parties will keep turning up and down because
people will hold firm to their positions, people will insist on their
interest carrying the day, so there must be negotiation, so there will
continuously be interest coming up to the fore to be discussed in 2007, so
in that process, the build up to 2007 will be as expected because you must
not lose sight of the fact that people are becoming a little more wiser now
and very unfortunately, like a third world economy, government is the only
business, peoples interest are getting more aroused. So I see a kind of
evolution, I see a kind of revolving process within the political parties,
all aimed at achieving relevance or aimed at taking power at various levels,
all aimed at allowing one group or the other take control, so it is a matter
of who takes control, but the truth is that there is going to be movement,
but whether it is going to lead to eclipse of any political party, I may not
be in a position to determine that since I am not God, but I know that there
is going to be too much of activities. Infact we have started, every party
is reorganising internally with a view to positioning themselves with 2007
in mind.
There are also speculations that there are attempts to lure you away
from ANPP?
There has always been this rumour of my being lured, but everyday I
still remain firm and committed, I am not a come and chop politician, that
is the difference between me and a lot of other people, I am somebody who
believes that you can be in the opposition today and be in government
tomorrow. The present president of Ghana was leader of the opposition for
some time, today he is in government. So you find that it is not compulsory
that you must be in government of the day to be relevant. You can be in
opposition at any given time and still be relevant, but it is a matter of
how do you manage yourself, how do you manage your interest, how do you
manage your views and what do you stand for most importantly. If the entire
thing is just for you and what you get as a person, it is easier to
negotiate a movement, but what you realise is that there are people who have
sacrificed too many following you, there are people standing by you since
you started your political life, what are you going to tell them? It is only
unfortunate that people without credibility will be in this party today,
criticise the other party and the next morning they are back to the other
party.
That is why when people come here and tell me about ideology, I say no
there is no ideology. In Nigeria, political parties are just seen as
vehicles for the actuailisation of political power. It is sad because we are
not building any democratic culture. Democracy is not only wearing civilian
dress, it goes beyond that, it is more in the people, it is more in the
practice, it is more in our ability to argue, disagree at the end of the
day, we agree on something, it is more on having divergent views on how to
achieve something that will be in the interest of the people, but at the end
of the day the target is achieving it.

Original_Search_Title: We Must Work to Avert Food Crisis in Nigeria-- Aib Igiehon
Headline: Nigeria: We Must Work to Avert Food Crisis in Nigeria-- Aib Igiehon
Body_Text:
Although he is an architect by profession, the name Aib Igiehon has over the years almost become synonymous with entertainment and the promotion of a national food culture in Nigeria. So,under the aegis if his Aibtonia organisation of which he is chairman, the architect turned food expert is presently spearheading a campaign to ensure that Nigerian children are fed on our local food. He also told Vanguard Features in this interview that government should take urgent steps to avert a looming food crisis in the country.
You have long been in the vanguard of promoting food culture in Nigeria. Would you say we presently have a national food culture? And if not why?
The matter of food is of great concern to countries that mean well, that know what they are doing. It's not the way we are doing it here. A country without a food culture or without food is doomed. Nobody is saying that you shouldn't import food. We need food, we need to bring in food, we need to be able to afford the food that are brought. We've got to be able to afford good food. A country without a food culture cannot develop.
Whenever I talk about USSR, people get worried. The USSR was disorganized because they didn't have food culture. Look at what is happening in Niger now, because they don't have vision.
Nigeria is naturally endowed but we are not able to utilise what God has given us. We know that food is very important that was why we came to this country, to sensitise the government, to let them know that food is not just a kitchen palaver or to have a garden. Food is so important that any country that doesn't have vision can be in crisis as far as food is concerned. Nigeria is not a strong country yet. We have the money but we don't plan well. Let them prepare for the future because Nigeria is exploding as regards population. 2010 is another year that UNICEF has predicted will be difficult for Nigeria, that there's going to be population explosion in Nigeria. But we have to be prepared, we shouldn't be careless. If you see the development of every country that is developed, you'll see that food is constant. The American economy is so beautiful because there is food, the Chinese economy is so beautiful because they have food. Even India, food is there now and the economy is rising. Nigeria is not there yet. So we are not really free, we eat one type of food day-in day-out. There are some people that just eat eba, or you see children go to school with Gari and kokoro in their pockets and they die like flies.
If you want to know how children are dying in this country, just go to schools. If you look at the registers of children that started school this year, before two years time, go and check you will notice that half of them are gone, because of food. Who will bother when the poor boy or girl dies like flies. So, Nigeria is really in crisis. Let's get ourselves organised on this, let's get food culture, let's develop our food culture, let's tell them that there is danger in it in the future.
The President is a farmer. How do you assess the input of this government in the agricultural sector?
Well, the government is trying its best to grow food, but we don't eat the same food. The government is just wasting its time because if you produce food, it's not just investing in farming because you don't know what Nigerians eat. Nigerians don't eat any type of food; they are selective, because we don't have a food culture. Everybody grows cassava, as if that is the only food, but there are other things we have to grow. But we don't have the culture that will enable our businessmen go out to farm on a large scale to the extent that they can keep it in a refrigerator or store it in the storage for the time of need. But we just eat from hand to mouth and say okay, what do we grow now? So, a lot of people are even afraid to invest in it. It's not wise to invest in it except you want to farm for other countries.
You have just said how important cassava is to us. But fears are presently being expressed in many quarters that with the interest of China in our cassava, the popular cassava-based food, gari, may soon disappear from the table of the common man in Nigeria. How do you react to this scenario? .
You know how Nigerians behave. They know that there is need for cassava. I think we will be able to meet the demand. So, with all the grants that government will give, a lot of Nigerians will go back to the farm. But the initial stage, like the next two or three years, the demand for Gari from other countries will actually cause scarcity. You see, we are talking of gari now, just gari, but there are other things we have that other people need, a lot of foods that we need in Nigeria. But for the fact that we don't have food culture, we don't know what to eat. The government can continue doing what it's doing but it should start right now to feed our children in school on Nigerian food. They should now select the most nutritious, the most delicious foods from all Nigerian food cultures. They can do their research and put it together and say, okay, this one is better, this one is better and so on. Select about 20 types of food and then start to teach our children.
This takes us to the Food for School programme that your company designed some years ago. What was the motivation?
The motivation is that after we've done our research, we are trying to make sure that we encourage Nigerians to eat Nigerian food. So, we discovered that we couldn't move ahead because most Nigerian adults are already hardened, and we couldn't really force them to eat each other's foods. The Yoruba 'll say oh Igbo foods smell. The Igboman 'll say oh this and the Hausaman too 'll say oh they are always burning onions. In fact, there was a governor from the north who told us that he had some Igbo visitors who came to his house and he was eating Quaker Oat, which is a European food made from corn. So, he now called them, saying come and eat with me my brothers, and the Igbo visitors said 'we don't eat Hausa food'.
What I'm trying to say is that, I don't look at it as ordinary food. You know Nigerians don't appreciate things, but food is very, very important. What we are trying to say is that if we cannot eat each others food, then where are we going? So, we started working and we discovered that it's our children that we have to train to eat Nigerian food and we have to select the best food from all parts of Nigeria. If we are going to select 20 types of food or 30, we 'll now tell Nigerians to start producing them. The foods we are going to select are going to be nutritious and delicious, full of vitamins. So, they 'll have the same menu at every given point. If they are eating one type of food, everybody will be eating it all over the country.
. So, that will sustain the Nigerian economy. But we have to first of all, continue to put part of the gains into feeding the children. When you feed them, they 'll grow up to know how to eat Nigerian food, so they continue to buy the Nigerian food and the economy will change for the better. And these children will grow up with healthy brains, healthy bodies, that's another advantage. They 'll grow up bigger because you just feed them once a day. And you feed them on Nigerian food, don't go and feed them on American food, because if you feed them on American food you will be supporting the American economy. If you feed them on Chinese food, you 'll be supporting the Chinese economy. Because these people will go ahead to look for that Chinese food and they will buy it at any cost. If you feed them on American food they will grow up and have the economic power and they 'll go after the American food. But if you feed them on Nigerian food, they 'll grow up and go after Nigerian food with all their might and the Nigerian farmers will become richer and more productive and jobs will not be in short supply any more because food is the most important item in anyone's life. So, the Nigerian kids who are being fed in school will now learn how to eat Nigerian food.
Recently, President Obasanjo was reported to have said that the Federal Government will feed school children. Do you think the government, that is the Federal and state governments have enough political will, to implement this?
As bad as our leadership is, the only person that I think can implement such a thing as far as I'm concerned is Obasanjo.
I first got worried. I said how is he going to feed these children in schools? Is he going to feed them on American food? Has he selected the type of foods he is going to feed the children with? We have been talking about this thing, we are consultants in this programme, we've worked on it and worked on it for years, we have not been consulted, nobody has called us. But when you are making such plans, they should call us to come and put our plans on the ground. We've done more research than anybody else in this country, because we've been talking about this thing for the past 20 years and we have brought out our blueprint. We've told the government about the advantage of feeding the children in school. We've even approached states governments, to encourage them that if the Federal Government cannot handle this thing, let the states handle it. But, it will not even be good for Nigeria if the state governments handle that. It's the Federal Government that is supposed to handle it so that they can have a pattern to build nationhood, to build our children as Nigerians, not as Northerners, not as Southerners, or not as tribal people. They should build them as Nigerians, so that wherever anybody chooses to live can be his choice.
Aibtonia is said to be planning a Februarifest. What is it all about?
Februarifest is an opportunity for a well-tested strategy which is the All-Nigeria Food and Beverage Festival to be enlarged to global dimensions, enabling Nigerians to have access to other foods. We will encourage foreign companies which see us as a consumer nation to come in and invest, enter into joint ventures with responsible entities and ensure that the foods Nigerians love eating are produced in Nigeria. One problem our farmers have is the lack of post-harvest technologies, lack of farm-gate technology which enables people process the foods from the point of production, of harvest before moving them to where the consumers are. If they (foreign companies) show us what they have in terms of technology, we can choose and identify those things that can help move us forward as a people. We have good, arable lands; divergent ecological zones. We can grow apples, grapes etc. What are those foods that Nigerians eat that 'll provide nutritional and taste value for our people? Identify what is coming into the country that we love. We then create a genuine Nigerian food pool...... Februarifest which will come up next February promises to be explosive, successful and engaging. We're planning well for it.

Original_Search_Title: Memo to World Igbo Congress
Headline: Nigeria: Memo to World Igbo Congress
Body_Text:
It is gladdening to know that the WORLD IGBO CONGRESS has continued to hold her annual congress. Igbo associations have a tendency to collapse after the initial euphoria. That the world Igbo congress has lasted so far raises the hope that it could be a veritable institution for the improvement of Igboland. I, therefore, wish you fruitful meeting in California. The issue I address is very apt to your theme this year: Oganihu Site Na Njikota.
I was at the 2003 congress in Nashville. I came from Nigeria, self sponsored as an academic interested in the advertised program for that year which was education, agriculture, industrialization etc. The focus that year was not on politics. There were a number of participants from Nigeria who are non-politicians. Many were somewhat dismayed that politicians hijacked the events and changed the focus of that congress. The media in Nigeria was awash with political commentary. The important sessions on education and agriculture were not reported. Sadly the meetings with the University of Tennessee faculty of Agriculture raised much hope but delivered very little. On return to Nigeria we were often asked if we went to Nashville to discuss politics. Politics of course is important, but the World Igbo Congress has to be given more meaning and impact the life of a majority of Igbo People resident in Igboland.
I have a number of suggestions, which the World Igbo Congress, and indeed Igbo Diaspora organizations anywhere in the world may consider in order to, be more useful to our people. These are related to some practical problems affecting Igboland. These include;
1. Revival of the oil palm economy
2. Documentation of Pogrom and Biafra War casualties
3. Support for tertiary Institutions
4. Environmental and Erosion Management
1. Revival of the Oil Palm Economy
The oil palm (Elaeis guinnesis) is the most important crop in the agro-ecology of southeast Nigeria. It has been and remains the most important source of rural liquidity in southeast Nigeria (followed by local and international remittances since the 1990s). The economy of the former eastern region was built on the oil palm. The advances in education, infrastructure and industrialization in eastern Nigeria in the first republic was funded substantially from oil palm earnings, through the former palm produce board.
However, since the 1970's, serious macro-economic changes in Nigeria led to decline in the oil palm industry (as with other export crops, such as rubber, cocoa, and groundnut). Specifically, developments in Nigeria led to massive rural-urban migration, and shift in labour supply from agriculture to non-agricultural sectors. Higher returns to labour in the non- rural and non- agricultural sector was the inducing factor. Within the agricultural sector, as population grew, so did domestic demand for food. Consequently, resources (land and labor especially) shifted towards food- crops production, especially cassava in the south and maize in the north.
Inconsistent trade policies added to the woes of the oil palm economy. Importation of vegetable oils and tallow for industrial purposes depressed domestic demand and prices of oil palm. Return to labor was low. Processing of oil palm fruits, being labor intensive, was discouraged because of low labor productivity. Low earnings led to low rural liquidity. This is a major contributing factor to rural poverty in southeast Nigeria, because real income has declined compared to the pre-1970 scenario.
The Problem
In addition to the discouraging macro-economic conditions espoused above, there are other problems that can be addressed at micro and macro- levels for the revival of the oil palm economy of southeast Nigeria. These problems exist at the farm- household and community levels. These include:
1. Over - grown wild stock of palm trees. An overview of southeast Nigeria will show dense vegetative cover, dominated by the oil palm, but these are over grown and wild. In some cases the heights are over 15 meters. Yield of these wild trees are low when compared to improved high yielding varieties, which are products of our research institutions and universities. The very tall trees are a nightmare to harvesters, most of who are old men with age of up to 80 years. Lack of labour to harvest palm fruits is a major constraint presently in southeast Nigeria. Lack of harvesting labour is reinforced by relatively high charges by harvesters, sometime up to N50 per fruit bunch. This is a problem where there is lack of cash.
2. There is a problem with stagnant technologies. When one reads the description of Igboland of the 18th century by Olaudah Equiano, he describes a hoe and cutlass - technology agricultural sector. Today, after three centuries, the dominant technologies of agriculture in southeast Nigeria are still hoe and cutlasses.
For crops, which the colonialist decided to encourage, to the benefit of their home industries, they introduced some incentives, including technologies to boost their production. The colonialist introduced the manually- operated palm fruit press. The colonialist introduced the oil palm mills, which you find in many communities in southeast Nigeria. That is virtually the state of oil palm processing in southeast Nigeria since the last century. Yet the colonialist operated in an era of surplus rural farm labour. With serious rural farm labour scarcity arising from massive migration (only about 13% of Igbo people reside in their ancestral homes (Nwajiuba, 2001), most rural farm labour in southeast Nigeria are old men and women. These old men use cutlasses to harvest the over- grown low yielding trees. Women and children use baskets to carry the harvest home, the men use cutlasses to thresh the fruits, and the family picks the fruits and takes it to the neighborhood oil mill. This is parboiled in drums using firewood (increasingly scarce). To digest (or pound) the fruits, pestles and mortars are used. This is a huge disincentive. Thereafter, the manually operated mechanical press (which the colonialist introduced) is used to extract the oil. A tin of oil may cost N1,000 in June, N1,200 in April, and N1,500 in July.
Thereafter, the women and children manually separate the kernel from the fiber. The oil mill owner may be paid in cash (N200/tin), or in kind (1 paint of oil/tin, that is 5 litre/20 liters), in addition to some fiber and nuts. The family takes home oil, fiber and nuts.
The nuts are cracked using stones (usually) to release the kernel. One (1) paint of kernel fetches N350, which the family sells to buy "soup" materials. This can be a very critical source of cash for petty household purchases that are important for household food security. The above process, which is prevalent all over southeast Nigeria, keeps the people at a low equilibrium peasantry level. It is however possible and desirable to reverse this.
What can be done
There are possible points of intervention with respect to the oil palm economy for the benefit of southeast Nigeria. These include:
1. Replacing low yielding over-grown wild stock. There are about 8 universities and many colleges of Agriculture and Colleges of Education engaged in agricultural teaching and research. In Imo State for instance, there are at least four of these: Imo State University (IMSU), Federal University of Technology (FUTO), Alvan Ikoku College of Education (AICE), and the Michael Okpara College of Agriculture (MOCA). This year, Imo State University Faculty of Agriculture bred about 1,000 oil palm seedlings for sale to the public at N85 each. This has a value of N85,000 (or $600 ca.). This university has a capacity to produce about 10,000 oil palm seedlings each year. So can the other three institutions in Imo State.
In effect it is possible to produce 40,000 seedlings each year in Imo State using these institutions. It is therefore possible to produce 200,000 oil palm seedlings in southeast Nigeria each year using the institutions in Imo State. If this is done each year for five years, we can produce 1,000,000 oil palm seedlings in southeast Nigeria.
We can distribute these seedlings through our communities, town unions, and Women Organizations. Governments in southeast Nigeria could provide (and sell as they now do, fertilizers - urea and MOP, rather than mostly NPK). Governments in southeast Nigeria could use existing community health or environmental inspectors to insist after 5 years on cutting down all overgrown palm trees of a specified height, say 15 meters, and replacing it immediately with improved varieties.
This scheme will replace wild low yielding stock with high yielding shorter growing stock, reduce the problem of harvesting and boost production. In addition, it will keep our universities and other institutions productively relevant, and help in the training of manpower.
2. Mechanized palm fruit digesters. These are already available in isolated cases in southeast Nigeria. They are also locally fabricated. Some cost about N50,000. It probably would have made more sense if Nigeria's billion naira poverty alleviation program commenced in 1999, which simply granted pocket money to some people was devoted to this. It would make sense to commission indigenous fabricators with proven capacity to manufacture palm fruit digesters to be distributed to communities in southeast Nigeria. The colonialist, last century, promoted oil press.
Massive extension and diffusion of locally fabricated fruit digesters will generate employment and income, reduce poverty and crime. It will encourage palm fruits processors and boost output. It reduces drudgery and raises labour productivity. Output per unit of fruit is increased (Nwajiuba, 2004).
3. Palm Nut Crackers: Mechanical devices for palm nut cracking are also available in a few communities in southeast Nigeria. These are also locally fabricated. These cost about N10,000 each. It should be possible to commission local fabricators to produce this for distribution to communities. This will boost palm kernel production, contribute to household income and welfare.
4. Value adding industries producing lubricant, detergents, soaps, pomades, creams and other products.
Lessons from the Colonial History
The colonialists used various strategies to promote the oil palm, cocoa, groundnut, rubber etc. Most of those strategies are still very relevant and we can learn from that.
1. Establishment of research institutes (NIFOR, CRIN, Rubber Research Institute, Agricultural stations at Samaru and Moore Plantation at Umudike).
2. Aggressive extension of the products of these Institutions with planting materials distributed free.
3. Technology advancement in the post-harvest treatment for instance oil press mills digesters. Note that this level of technology has not been extended beyond what the colonialist left.
4. Healthy economic climate which assured stable and good earnings by farmers and which encouraged farmers move massively into the production of these crops.
Summary
A number of factors affected the oil palm and led to its decline. From the harvesting to post-harvest treatment, the oil palm is affected as follows:
1. Lack of labor to harvest the over-grown trees. Young people desert the villages leaving only old men to climb the over grown trees.
2.Manual harvesting, digesting, separating, nuts cracking etc.
3. Old trees, too tall and low yielding.
How these can be done
1. Lobby the Nigerian government and influence macro-economic conditions to favour production rather than dependence on imports, for even goods, which we have natural agro-ecological advantages to produce.
2. Nigerian government should be convinced that agriculture should be supported, not just for itself but also for wider societal well-being, including income and employment, and for social reasons as crime control. It is noteworthy that industrialized countries spend up to $300 billion each year on subsidies (Rogoff, 2003).
3. Replace all overgrown, low yielding and the wide varieties of oil palm within the next 5 - 10 years.
4. Provide improved techniques as follows: harvesters, digesters, fiber separators, nut crackers and separators.
5. Ensure medium to long - term stability in earnings and farmers income.
Documentation of Biafra War Victims
The Issue is that posterity does not forget those who died fighting, as well as victims of the pogroms that preceded that war. Very serious societies such as the Jews treat their people and history with much seriousness. This generation runs the risk of being regarded as an ungrateful people. One of the weaknesses of the Igbo is improper reward or disoriented honour system. We are certainly having a diminishing capacity to document these. This further diminishes with time. It is therefore urgent as we are probably loosing those who can recall those events. The objective therefore is to conduct a village-by-village documentation of Biafran War heroes and victims.
Methodology
Igboland has seven (7) states (including Anioma parts of Delta State and the upland of Rivers State). The basic identity and clear structure of these seven states comprise of local governments, which are composed of council, wards, and further composed of Autonomous Communities. Based on this, no community will be omitted. We can illustrate this with Imo State that has 27 LGAs, of which Ehime-Mbano is one. Ehime - Mbano LGA has 15 Wards, one of which is Ihitte-Nsu. Ihitte - Nsu ward has two Autonomous Communities. The survey will be done at the level of the communities.
Selection of Respondents: Each Autonomous Community has a traditional ruler, chiefs and an elected (usually) community union, and women group leaders. They will be the prime source of information. Where necessary, groups of villagers will be assembled for discussions.
Data Collection: Two methods will be employed. The first is direct discussions with traditional rulers/chiefs, community/town union executives and women leaders. The second will be "Focal group Discussions", at which villagers will be invited. Some communities at the end of the war in 1970 organized "funerals" for those who died. It could be that records on this exist in such communities.
Instruments for Data Collection: A simple questionnaire/Interview schedule indicating name of community, LGA and State, serial number, names of victims, names of parents, date /period of death (if known) location of death \ injury (if known). Note that not all the injured are at Uturu / Oji River).
3. Support for Tertiary Institutions
This may involve either of the following:
a) Syndicating academics and researchers in the Diaspora to spend sometime (at least a semester) or/and sabbatical in Universities in Igboland.
b) Supporting research, journals, and facilities in universities in Igboland.
An example of such research is an exploration of the problems and prospects of scientific publication in Igbo language. Various arguments have been advanced for learning in indigenous languages. Very little has however been done in implementing this especially in the less developed parts of the world. The advantages of the promotion of indigenous languages are numerous. These include the preservation of diversity as well as national consciousness and pride in the indigenous language and culture. This research should explore the prospect and problems of scientific publication in the Igbo Language.
An exploratory investigation of the subject of interest was conducted based on abstracts from the Journal of Agriculture and Food Science (JAFS). JAFS is a registered Journal of International standard for agriculture, food and related sciences published by the Faculty of Agriculture and Veterinary Medicine of the Imo State University, Owerri. The Journal has an ISSN 1597 - 1074 and is obtainable on the World Wide Web (www.imsu-jafs.com). It is a peer-reviewed journal. For our purpose two abstracts of volume 1 No 1 of April 2003, were translated into the Igbo Language. Preliminary indications on this confirm the possibility of scientific publication in Igboland language. However some problems are revealed, which linguistics should address. These include lack of Igbo equivalent of scientific terms, the need for Igbo Scientific dictionary and technical difficulties associated with the absence of computer hard and software suited to the Igbo language.
Prof. Chinedum Nwajiuba, is Associate Professor of Agricultural Economics, Imo State University, Owerri, Nigeria.
Examples: Abstracts in English and their Igbo equivalents are presented along with the authors of each article as follows:
1a. Screening Six Musa Genotypes for Resistance to Some Plant Parasitic Nematodes (M. O. Onuh Dept. of Crop Science and Biotechnology Imo State University, Owerri, Nigeria)
ABSTRACT: Six Musa genotypes (Obino l'ewai, TMPX7002-1, TMPX6930-1, TMPX4698-1, Yangambi Km5 and Calcutta 4) were screened for their possible resistance or susceptibility to some plant parasitic nematodes (Meloidogyne incognita, Helicotylenchus multicinctus and Radopholus similis). The experiment was conducted at the Onne branch of the International Institute of Tropical Agriculture (IITA) between 1999 and 2000. The results indicated that Calcutta 4 variety showed resistance more to Radopholus similis and Helicotylenchus multicinctus, than other genotypes. On the other hand, Obino l'ewai and TMPX7002-1 were the susceptible cultivars observed. Differences in their resistance or susceptibility to the parasitic nematodes were attributed to their genotypic differences. keywords: Musa, nematode, genotype
1b. Inyocha udi Obara agburu 'Musa' isii maka igbochi Ufodu Oria Owereahu Ndi Osisi Na-aria. (M.O. Onuh, Ngalaba Nke Sayensi Ihe Okuku na Nka Ndu, Mahadum Steeti Imo di n'Owere)
NSERELU: Udi Obara agburu musa isii (nke a kporo "Obino L'ewel, TMPA7002-1, TMPA6930-1, TMPX4698-1, Yanganbi km 5 na Calcuta 4)" ka e nyochara maka inwaputa ikikere mgbochi ma o bu nnagide ha nwere nye oria Owereahu osisi na-aria (nke gunyere "meloidogyne incognila, Helicotylenchus multicinctus na Radopholus similis"). E mere ihe nnyocha a na ngalaba Ulo Mmuta Oru Ugbo Mba Uwa niile nke do n'onne, nke bu (IITA) n'aha mkpirisi bekee, n'agabata afo 1999 na afo 2000. Rizootu gosiri na udi nke 'Calcutta gosiputara mgbochi nye 'Radopholus similis' na 'Helicotylenctus multicinctus' karia udi obara agburu ndi ozo. N'aka nke ozo 'Obino l'ewal na. TMPX 7002-1' bu ndi nwere nnagide a choputara nye oria owereahu a, bu nke e kwenyere na o sitere na ndiiche di n'obara agburu ha.
ABSTRACT: The hundred day-old chicks (Anak strain) were used in a 4 - week experiment to determine the replacement value of rubber seed cake for groundnut cake on performance of broilers at starter phase. Graded levels of rubber seed cake (0%, 25%, 50%, 75% and 100%) replaced corresponding levels of groundnuts cake in five treatments (A, B, C, D and E respectively), which were further replicated three times in a completely randomized design. Feed and water were supplied ad libitum to the experimental birds while vaccination and necessary medication were promptly administered to prevent ailments and sustain good health and immunity. Body weight and anthropometric parameters were measured weekly in all the treatments. The average weights of the broilers at four weeks were 382.2 ± 7.75; 356.9 ± 7.95; 492.4 ± 6.27; 336.7 ± 9.45 and 32.6 ± 10.40 for treatments A, B, C, D, and E respectively. Results showed that treatment C, significantly (P 0.05) improved body weight, shank length wing length, body length, thigh length, girths and head circumference.
Keywords: Anak broiler, replacement value, Rubber seed cake, groundnut cake.
2b Uru Nri Mkpuru Roba Bara Karia Nri Mkpuru Ahuekere nye Okuko Broila Ndi Na-ebido Ndu.(F.N. Madubuike, E. A. Agiang, B. U. Ekenyem na E.O. Ahaotu. Ngalaba Nke Sayensi Anumanu Mahadum Steeti Imo, P.M.B. 2000 Owere, Naigiria).
NSERELU: E wetara umu okuko 300 gbara otu ubochi (Anak strain) make ihe nnwaputa izu ano e mere iji mata uru nri sitere na mkpuru roba nwere kaira iri mkpuru ahuekere n'anu ha na mbidondu ha. Nri mkpuru ahuekere n'anu ha. Nri mkpuru roba a haziri n'ogo (0%, 25%, 50%, 75% na 100%) ka e jiri gbanwee nri mkpuru ahuekere di n'ogo ahu n'uzo ise nke (A, B, CH, D na E n'otu n'otu). E mere nhazi nlezi anya a ugboro ato aghara aghara. E nyere umu okuko ndi a nri na mmiri n'oge o kwesiri, e nyekwara ha ogwu mgbochi n'oge ya, maka igbochi oria na nye ha ahe ike ha choro. A tutu n'ihe otutu aru ha nakwa ngwa ahu nke antropometriik ha kwa izu. Mgbaetiti aru anu ahu umu okuko ndi a n'ime mkpuru izu ano di otu a 383.2 ± 7.75; 356.9 ± 7.95; 492.4 ± 6.27; 336.7 ± 9.45 na 325. 6 ± 10.40 nke bu maka nleru anya n'onodu A, B, CH, D na E. n'otu n'otu. Rizootu gosiri na nke CH, (P< 0.05) gbara abuba dikwa aru karia, aku ya, ukwu na isi ya dikwa aru too egologo kaira ndi ozo.
Environmental problems including urban pollution as shell as sheet and gully erosions are very serious problems in Igboland.
These are my humble suggestions that could positively affect the lives of our people.

Original_Search_Title: NDDC Has No Ghost Projects - Presidential Committee
Headline: Nigeria: NDDC Has No Ghost Projects - Presidential Committee
Body_Text:
The Presidential Monitoring Committee set up to keep an eye on the Niger Delta Development Commission, NDDC, has declared that the interventionist agency has no phantom projects, contrary to allegations and speculations.
Chairman of the Presidential Monitoring Committee, Ambassador Lawrence Ekpebu, made the assertion Thursday in Port Harcourt while addressing members of the NDDC Board and top management during a visit to the Commission's corporate headquarters, at the end of their monitoring exercise.
In a statement released recently, Mr. Anietie Usen, the Commission's Head of Corporate Affairs disclosed that Ambassador Ekpebu was accompanied by the nine other members of the Committee.
"We have not found any ghost projects. We have found all the projects reflected in the NDDC records," Ambassador Ekpebu said.
Established under the NDDC Act, the Presidential Monitoring Committee is authorized to monitor the management of the Commission's funds and the implementation of its projects.
Describing the NDDC as a crucial instrument for peace and development in the Niger Delta, the Committee also commended the Commission for its efficient use of the limited resources available to it. Ekpebu said, "With limited resources NDDC is engaged in virtually every aspect of development in the Niger Delta."
However, the Presidential Monitoring Committee decried NDDC's involvement in projects that ought to be executed by States and Local Governments. "We feel this is not correct. We feel NDDC should wind up what it is doing in some areas and let the States and Local Government Areas handle them," said Ekpebu.
The Committee suggested that the NDDC should limit itself to the construction of major roads and bridges, dredging of waterways, reclamation of flood-free land for agriculture, industries and habitation. According to Ekpebu, it is the responsibility of the Federal Government to work hand-in-hand with the NDDC in the execution of these kinds of major projects.
He said that once this kind of demarcation is made, NDDC would be more effective.
The Presidential Monitoring Committee also urged the NDDC to get more involved in the development of high-level manpower especially for the oil and gas industry through the award of "automatic scholarships to Niger Delta indigenes so as to give them higher levels of competence to enable them compete for higher level jobs in the petroleum industry."
The Presidential Monitoring Committee pledged that in carrying out its watchdog function, it will not witch hunt anyone. Its Chairman said: "We are products of the NDDC Act and we intend to work for peace and development in the Niger Delta. Through our collaboration the work of the NDDC will be enhanced."
Earlier, the Chairman of the NDDC, Ambassador Sam Edem stated that the Commission's new focus is youth development and empowerment. He said the NDDC plans to generate at least 200,000 jobs for youths across the Niger Delta region through various programmes, such as massive cultivation of cassava.
Edem urged the Presidential Monitoring Committee to make a case for increased funding of the NDDC and the utilization by the NDDC member states of their 13 per cent derivation funds in oil producing areas.
Noting that the NDDC has become so omnibus that every problem in its member states is referred to it, Edem said the Commission is willing to develop the Niger Delta but the resources are not sufficient. "There is need for the State governments to commit 13 per cent of the 13 per cent derivation funds to the affected areas in the states."
The NDDC Chairman promised that the Commission would partner with the Presidential Monitoring Committee to ensure that the goals of the Niger Delta Regional Development Master Plan are attained."

Original_Search_Title: Debt Relief As Tragedy
Headline: Nigeria: Debt Relief As Tragedy
Body_Text:
No serious Nigerian with real money believes that the people in government know what they are doing. They go along with government shenanigans for personal competitive advantage over others, in order not to lose out in the quicksand of Nigerian power-play. As for the Nigerians without real money, they are so convinced that the economic managers are swindlers that they would rather cut corners than wait for the proverbial trickle down that the World bankers and IMF undertakers in our midst expect as follow-ups to the misery of their bitter medicines.
So how is confidence to be built all around? The whole issue centres on the simple matter of managing the Naira like a proper currency. Who is going to bring hard earned money to an economy where the currency, arrantly-devalued, is doing mambo and shakey-shakey? Since the managers believe less in Nigerians than in creditors who are laughing at us behind their handkerchiefs, they have been displaying such unguarded disrespect for the Naira. Serious foreign investors take a cue from them. Unless such an investor is assured of a quick kill based more on government deals than serious market orientation, they demand guarantees that no Nigerian would have needed to ask of a Nigerian government that is genuinely concerned about productivity. The foreign investor who can see how the ideology of reforms is deforming everything simply needs to look at the disrespect for the national currency and he and she will bid some time. Take the case of the President's enterprising choice of a Minister of Finance and sundry lower level interveners in Nigeria's economic decision-making who are on dollar salaries paid by creditor countries.
There have been many mealie-mouth attempts to make light of this business of appointing someone who will not agree to earn the Naira but who must be saddled with the task of managing the Naira. Unless it is explained by the policy of appointing indigenes to do the job of proconsuls, we are being asked to suspend disbelief as to why it should happen at all. Arguably, the Naira is obviously regressing to the level of toilet-paper and therefore cannot meet the international commitments of high-flying Nigerians who must be kept in accustomed comfort. The pity is that rather than raise the Naira to the level of the currency that the international jet-set with globalized commitments would be proud to earn, Mrs Okonjo-Iweala has spent all her time as finance Minister brewing up one scheme after another for devaluing the national currency.
