At any rate, they have decided to make assurance doubly sure by insisting that Nigeria must pay 12 billion dollars in two installments in order to qualify for debt forgiveness of 18 billion dollars. Even the least knowledgeable Nigerian can see that this is targeted at the oil windfall that has netted Nigeria about $24 billion dollars in foreign reserves. Suppose Nigeria did not have such an amount? And, by the way, many Nigerians should be wondering where Nigeria would be finding that kind of money if the rest of the world had listened to President Obasanjo O'S consistent arguments against a rise in the price of oil at a time some OPEC countries were pushing for it.
This is only part of the whimsicality that has governed Nigeria's search for debt forgiveness. Besides, the fact that President Obasanjo has already agreed to paying so much money without seeking the say so of the National Assembly and the state governments which own vast proportions of that money is a tell-tale of how much incivility is being built into the process. Which country in the world but Nigeria has ever allowed herself and her money to be so parted? Which country with Nigeria's potential would fail to find what to do with 12 billion dollars in order to be able to produce the means for liquidating the 30 billion dollars that is dragging Nigeria to her knees!
Of course, I concede that an economic establishment already too sold on the moribund theories of devaluation, liberalization and privatization as the way of saving a third world country, cannot be relied upon to know how to spend 12 billion in a way that brings prosperity. After all, Babangida got twelve billions and used it to worship at the shrines of frippery. Which is to say, that those who wish to advise Obasanjo to do the right thing must know that his team has been so trained to doing the wrong thing that if they try to do the right thing, they may botch it. The National Assembly should therefore be well advised to let the President know that the money he wishes to give away in his gamble -- yes it is a gamble - does not belong to the Federal Government alone but to all the states and local governments as well. No Nigerian wants to spend that kind of money in pursuit of a lie.
It may be just as well to remind the President of his own appreciation of the fact that Nigeria is not in the same boat as those countries that got debt forgiveness without conditionalities. From postwar Germany, post-Hussein Iraq, Poland, Yugoslavia, Kyrgyzstan, Pakistan and Afghanistan to others in Eastern Europe -- these were countries in a crisis of survival. Many Nigerians remember the comic relief of Obasanjo threatening, obviously in frustration, that he was prepared to resign if the fall of a President was all it required for a country to get debt-forgiveness.
It was good to hear him admit before the National Assembly that those luckier countries that got debt forgiveness have more geo-cultural, political, economic military, strategic significance for the creditor countries than Nigeria has. What he does not add is that the very reason that Nigeria is not of such strategic significance is the reason that we should not rely on the goodwill of the creditor nations. They have no reason to do Nigeria a favour. For
them it is warfare-trade by other means. This makes it rather embarrassing that the President of Nigeria should be expressing so much faith in either Tony Blair's Government or his Commission.
The hard fact is that Britain has her own problems and Tony Blair needs to serve Britain's national interests. As Mark Curtis notes in his book WEB OF DECEIT (Vintage 2003): "Two decades of Conservative rule left Britain with the worst poverty record in the developed world, according to the OECD. Poverty affected 20 percent of the population between 1991 and 1996. This was a worse record than even the US -- where 14 percent were affected -- and compares with 10 percent in Germany and 7 percent in Sweden. A UN report found that a fifth of adults in Britain were functionally illiterate and that 13.5 percent were permanently living in poverty, a worse record than all other developed countries, except the US and Ireland". (P.223).
Speaking objectively, this is to say that Tony Blair has a patriotic duty to raise Britain out of the morass by helping countries like Nigeria to continue to buy from Britain. Obasanjo and his economic team ought therefore to know what to buy and what not to buy or take from Britain, in order for Nigeria's national interests to be well served. Whereas Tony Blair must remember that his political party, the Labour Party was once blamed for taking Britain to the IMF like a poor third world country, he would certainly not want to be blamed for not knowing how to use third world countries, like Nigeria to escape that fate during his tenure. Or after. Blair does not have to be as virulent as Margaret Thatcher whose miserable monetarism set the stage for the impoverishment of Britain while savaging her third world trading partners. Obasanjo ought to know this, He ought also to know that, whether as a former military or civilian dictator, he will always be blamed for allowing so called developed countries to use Nigeria to bail themselves out of bad patches rather than angle for terms of mutual prosperity. If truth be told, it makes sense for Britain to forgive countries like Burkina Faso, save them for another day, while ensuring that a country like Nigeria supplies what will be lost in the process. At any rate, why does Obasanjo expect British workers to subsidize the folly, if not profligacy, of his country without demanding a pound of flesh in return?
So to say, I think that Britain and the other creditor countries would be acting irrationally to give debt-forgiveness to a profligate and cowardly elite like Nigeria's which would rather beg for crumbs thai juild the Naira into a hard currency and make a bold lurch for her hard earned money stashed away in foreign vaults. In my view the rest of us ought to find ways to make the Nigerian government turn a new leaf and face up to the reality that it is not in Nigeria's interest to throw 12 billion dollars away in pursuit of a vacuous debt forgiveness which, no matter how it is looked at, promises tragedy. If we are so keen on meeting our international obligations lets meet it squarely. Demand the return of Nigeria's looted funds whose exit into offshore havens all the creditors have connived at. And insist on saving the Naira by putting it where our oil wealth, not non-oil expectations, have put us in world affairs. If our insistence on saving the Naira requires that we pay 20 % of our national income for debt serving purposes, why not. Simply consider the saving graces of a proper management of the Naira which, in a shot gun manner, can relieve the country of more than 80% of liabilities arising from following the moribund undertaker economics imposed by the creditors.

Original_Search_Title: Market Cassava Wherever
Headline: Nigeria: Market Cassava Wherever
Body_Text:
Abeokuta — Ogun State governor, Otunba Gbenga Daniel has charged Nigerians in the diaspora to market cassava products wherever they were for economic growth of the country.
Daniel who took his crusade abroad, made the charge in the United States where he received the Odua Award of Excellence at the 10th Odua Excellence Award organised by National Association of Odua Descendants (NAOD) in Canada and North America, which was held in Atlanta, Georgia.
According to him, cassava products had become the next gold of Nigeria being promoted by President Olusegun Obasanjo.
He explained that his government had accorded due recognition to the promotion of agriculture.
Governor Daniel stressed that the cassava revolution in the state was in realisation of the foreign exchange earning capability of the crop and other economic potentials.
"I want to urge our citizens abroad to bring cassava to the fore front, it is one of the wonder crops that can grow our economy and it can create wealth and alleviate poverty among our people."
He said his government had been able to reduce the rate of unemployment from 67 percent to 10 percent in the last two years.
Daniel added that various infrastructure had been put in place to attract industrialisation and economic development through private sector participation.
According to Governor Daniel, the major challenge before his administration was to restore credibility and transparency in goveranance, calling on people to join in the crusade against corruption and bad governance.

Original_Search_Title: Ghana Stops Importation on GM Foods
Headline: Ghana Stops Importation on GM Foods
Body_Text:
On July 29, the Food and Agriculture Minister, Mr. Ernest Debrah announced - categorically - that Ghana would reject, without hesitation, the importation of any GM foods, crops and materials into the country, despite the fact that the new technology might help solve some of the country's food production and famine problems.
In reality, as stated by The Chronicle, "this statement implied that the government had resolved to oppose anything to do with GM foods."
One important missing element from the article at the ghanaweb.com site was that the Honorable Minister's reasons for such categorical rejection were not included in The Chronicle's report, or perhaps the Minister's decision was a unilateral one - for the government.
At the July 28 workshop on GMOs hosted by the Science and Technology Policy Research Institute (STEPRI), of the CSIR, Professor Emmanuel Owusu Bennoah, Director General of CSIR, while acknowledging the risks involved in any science and technology, strongly advocated for Ghana to take advantage of the GM technology to improve its agriculture and food production for food security in the country. He stated it best when he warned that Ghana ran the risk of being left behind in this new era of science and technology, if it failed to take part in it.
RESOURCES
Similarly, the Minister of Environment and Science, Ms. Christine Churcher equally acknowledged the controversy surrounding the GM technology but she pointed out also that it had enormous potential to address the country's food security problems. Finally, Dr. Joseph Gogo, Director of STEPRI assured of several follow up training programs to build capacity of stakeholders to enable the public make informed choices over the GM technology and GMOs.
Earlier, on July 27, addressing a two-day capacity building seminar at Agona Swedru for members of the parliamentary select committee on environment, Ms. Churcher once again, called attention to the rapidly diminishing forest and other natural resources of the country. According to her, Ghana has already lost over 75% of its forest cover in the last few decades and that the country lost over 2 million cedis per annum through environmental degradation - especially illegal logging and mining.
The Food and Agriculture Minister's announcement thus comes as a surprise and great disappointment at a time when attention should be focused on how to save and also restore what has been lost. Perhaps we need to remind our honorable ministers that the loss of such natural resources are irreplaceable and can only lead to desertification, which also means that about 50 years from now, Ghana could potentially face severe droughts and most of its land mass could be reduced to desert.
This is a time when most African countries are fast and seriously engaged in the GM technology research to assist in their quest for food security for their countries - in the long term. Currently, Kenya, Nigeria and South Africa, also Egypt and others have taken the lead in Africa by applying the GM technology research to fighting crop disease affecting staple foods such as cassava, potatoes, corn, banana, rice, oil palm, coconut and many others. In fact, Kenya has already begun trading in its GMOs with other countries both within and outside the continent. The East African countries have formed an organization called Association for Strengthening Agricultural Research in Eastern and Central Africa (ASARECA) in their determination to harness the new technology in food production to meet the needs of their populations now and in the future.
COMMITMENT
At the recent ECOWAS Ministers meeting on Biotechnology in Bamako, Mali, Professor Walter Alhassan, coordinator of the Program for Biosafety Systems for West and Central Africa, stressed that the introduction of Biotechnology was a tool to complement traditional agriculture for food security and poverty reduction and facilitate the production of new varieties of plants and breeds of animals - and not to replace the conventional way of farming.
Indeed, he pointed out that only Nigeria had shown commitment by promulgating a national policy and creating a Center of Excellence for Biotechnology at Shresto, near Abuja. Nigeria has also established a National Biotechnology Development Agency and Advanced Laboratory, showing that country's strong commitment.
But the Professor also warned that the West African sub-region lagged behind other regions in the advancement of biotechnology.
This is why Mr. Debrah's statement is disturbing. Does he want Ghana to lag behind without making any attempts at all to research into the new technology? What are his main concerns about the GM technology that elicited such a statement from him? Perhaps the Minister should realize that other African countries - despite the many unresolved issues surrounding the GM technology - have rather chosen to embrace it and find solutions to any problems that may come up as they go along in employing it to solve their agricultural and other problems. Ghana should not hold onto just the negatives being preached by the zealous activists who only see just the one side of the issue and never bother to look at the other side for any balanced viewpoints.
RESEARCH
At the West African conference on GMOs, once again, Ms. Churcher reiterated her call for capacity building to ensure the sub-region's use of the new technology and warned that Africans "should swim with the tide otherwise we will be left behind as did the Green Revolution."
This article is not about Ms. Churcher but it has become obvious that she appears to be deeply committed to seeing Ghana - and Africa as a whole - become involved in developing its science and technology capacity to facilitate social and economic growth. Just last week, she called for research scientists to be rewarded for their inventions and research innovations for national development, and it would help for such attitude to be shared by our leaders, particularly in the area of agriculture and food security. This is the age of science and technology and the western societies, led by their able scientists and private companies and investors, are rapidly harnessing the unlimited potential of biotechnology to improve the quality of life and maintain their food security programs for their people.
Perhaps Africans - including Ghanaians - need to pause to ask how and why western societies have managed to feed their growing populations and still have surplus to feed Africans who have land and unlimited labor supplies. The answer is simply that they have been engaged in constant research to seeking the optimum crop yield to feed the bulging world populations, including those of Africa. While African governments throw banquets to toast the news that their countries have been awarded the HIPC status and their politicians and others wallow in corruption and spurious riches, other so-called Third World countries, including India and China - are slowly but steadily becoming self-sufficient and self-reliant, thus reducing their dependency on western nations. It rather appears that African leaders never want to let go this idea of dependency since this is how they enrich themselves.
Ghana should not remove itself from the GM technology race or it would be too late to take part. Whatever made the Food and Agriculture Minister make that statement, should be seriously reconsidered to avoid taking the country back many years in terms of the use of science and technology for modern day social and economic development.

Original_Search_Title: Red Cross Distributes Non-Food Items to Bangui Flood Victims
Headline: Central African Republic: Red Cross Distributes Non-Food Items to Bangui Flood Victims
Body_Text:
Bangui — The Red Cross of the Central African Republic distributed on Sunday non-food items, high energy biscuits and medicines to some 461 families affected by floods in the capital 17 days ago, the society's coordinator, Alphonse Zarambaud, said.
"Five thousand people received the items," he said on Sunday in Bangui, the capital.
This round of distributions went to families with more than 10 members each in the southern neighbourhoods of Bangui. The items consisted of blankets, pots, soap and jerry cans. UN agencies provided lamps, the high energy biscuits and the medicines.
"We are waiting to get more lamps for distribution," he said.
At the moment, he added, the Red Cross only had 480 lamps, fewer than the number of people needing the devices. However, the Red Cross has distributed potable water.
Faced with the possibility of a disease outbreak, due to overflowing latrines and possibly contaminated boreholes, the government has been advising the public to drink water only served by the national water plant. At least 4,000 jerry cans have been given out to the affected population.
Social Affairs Minister Marie Solange Pagonendji Ndakala described Sunday's effort "a good start". She said China, France, Japan, Libya and the United States had already expressed their intention to give money for the flood victims. Echoing that, CAR Red Cross Chairman Antoine M'Bao Bogo said the Japanese embassy in Yaoundé, Cameroon, had promised 52 million francs CFA (approx US $97,000) for non-food items.
"The non-food items will arrive by air and will be available next Thursday," he added.
He also said the Red Cross would inspect villages close to Bangui, from which there had been reports of damage to cassava and maize farms.
[ This report does not necessarily reflect the views of the United Nations ]

Original_Search_Title: Let Resource Control Be
Headline: Nigeria: Let Resource Control Be
Body_Text:
Lagos — AMONG notable issues I had looked forward to be sorted out at the
National Political Reforms Conference were resource control, the tenure of
elected executive office holders, disbandment of states as federating units,
and a return to regional government as was the case in the beginning.
We are all aware that it is over ten years since this agitation for a
conference that should be independent in nature without the interference of
the government. I was stunned when President Olusegun Obasanjo, at the
eleventh hour, gave his nod to the convocation of the conference, but I
immediately caught cold on hearing that government would nominate whoever
would go to the conference. It was at this juncture that those of us with
unbiased mind, began to understand that at the end of the day, the whole
farce was going to be an "owambe" gathering "Come Chop" and for this reason,
I believe, made it possible for great minds like Prof. Wole Soyinka and Pa
Anthony Enahoro to stay away, because they knew that nothing good would come
from out of Nazareth. Today, they have been vindicated. Any wonder why
Dr. Paul Unongo, a former minister and a vocal critic of Obasanjo government
and an Obasanjo nominee to the confab, in an interview granted to Sunday
Champion in February this year, concluded that the confab could make or mar
Nigeria, and warned that unless the Aburi issue of 1967 was re-examined and
given a befitting response.
With the hue and cry from the former Eastern and Midwestern regions that
their people be part of decision-making on how resources from their zones
should be distributed, it is a pointer to the fact that, there should be a
rethink and the time is ripe. We should go back to what we had in the
beginning of this journey as enshrined in the 1960 and 1963 Independence and
Republican constitutions which provided for fiscal federalism that gave rise
to the principle of derivation on which the Nigerian tripod stood.
A premonition of what the confab would look like became obvious when it
was announced that those grey areas of Nigeria's life should remain
"no-go-areas" confirming the prophecy of our elder comrades like Pa
Enahoro. It a situation where there was a predetermined consensus, was there
any need for the gathering, other than loot sharing? The South South region
which constitutes majority of oil producing states in the country were of
the view that it would be better, and helpful, if we went back to the maiden
constitution which empowered regions to have control over whatever resources
both human and natural in their territory.
In a paper delivered by Dr. Arthur Agwuncha Nwankwo of the Eastern
Mandate Union (EMU), to his fellow delegates at the confab, he went down
memory lane to bring to fore the Raisam Formula on resource control which
provided for 50 per cent to the region of origin of whatever resources, and
20 per cent to the Federal Government as its contribution for maintenance of
the integrity of the nation while the remaining 30 per cent was to be shared
equally among the then three regions of which the Eastern region, which was
the region of origin was a beneficiary, bringing its share to a total of 60
per cent. Between 1967 and 1970, Nigeria and the former Eastern region,
fought a civil war caused by inequality and insecurity which claimed about 2
million people of which about 95 per cent were easterners. For the war to be
well prosecuted against the Easterners, the derivation formula was abrogated
with nothing going to the region of origin of the resources.
For whatever reason, the Federal Government should be informed that the
war is over, or is Nigeria still fighting? Maybe. With the killing of the
Apo Six in Abuja and Peter Ede, the okada guy in Ikeja, Lagos and the
continuous hounding of Igbos who went to watch a football match and others
who are languishing in different prisons, the war may not have been
over.
Comparing the social situation today, with what it was in the 60s, one
would agree with me that had we continued with the old practice of resource
control and true federalism, our palm produce, cocoa products, groundnut,
cotton, and coal, to mention a few that sustained us, would have been
exploited the more. The federating units and new emerging geo-political
zones, would also have continued to look inwards, purposely to explore and
exploit their immense endowments even in agricultural sector to boost food
production that will serve the purpose of internal consumption and for
export, at least now that cassava has become a foreign exchange earner and a
hot cake in the Asian market.
The South South in their summation at the confab were of the view that
it would be better and helpful if we went back to the maiden constitution
which granted regions and territories autonomy over their land and whatever
resources therein, so that people can help themselves in developing their
areas.
This writer, as someone who with time, has studied the country is aware
that the country if properly harnessed should be the most endowed country in
the world, particularly in natural resources. If you will permit me, I will
take you a little into what this country can boast of both in natural and
human resources, possibly zone by zone or state by state. South East: gold,
salt, limestone, lead/zinc, oil and gas, clay, iron ore, lignite,
glass-sand, phosphate, gypsum, marcasite. South South: clay, limestone,
lead/zinc, uranium, salt, lignite, oil and gas, gypsum, manganese, marble,
glass-sand, iron ore, kaolin, gold, dolomite, phosphate and bitumen.
South West: Kaolin, feldspar, tatium, granite, syenite, glass sand,
clay, bitumen, sandstar, oil and gas, gemstone, gypsum, coal, bauxite, talc,
tantalite, tourmaline, columbite, marble, sillimarite, gold, cassiterite,
aquamarine, dolomite, phosphate. North West: Barytes, kaolin, gold,
limestone, phosphate, gypsum, silica-sand, clay, laterite, potash, flakes,
granite, gold, salt, sapphire, serpentine, asbestos, amethyst, kyanite,
graphite, sillimarite, mica, aquamarine, ruby, rockcrystal, topaz,
fluorspar, tourmaline, gemstone, tantalite pyrochlore, cassityerite, copper,
glass-sand, lead/zinc, and marble.
North East: kaolin, bentonite, gypsum, magnetite, barites, bauxite,
gemstone, lead/zinc, diatomite, soda-ash, amethyst, uranium, clay,
limestone, oil and gas. North Central: gold, marble, iron ore, cassilerile,
columbite, tantalite, feldspar, mica, bergy, dolomitie, sapphire,
tourmaline, quartet, amathyst, topax, zircon, llmenite, galena, barytes,
limestone, mica, cooking coal, talc, clay, salt, chal write, lead/zinc,
coal, bauxite, gemstone, gypsum, oil and gas, kaolin, bitumen, emerald, tin,
granite, bentonite, pyroclore, wolram, bismuth fluoride and
molybdenite.
With the abundant natural resources who says we are not blessed? The
continuous parasitic feasting on oil alone is not healthy and is not a good
sign of a people that want to survive. I am of the opinion that this country
should be regionalised so that people can go home and start thinking on what
to do and how best to develop their areas.
This over-concentration and centralisation in Abuja and Lagos is not the
best for us. It is possible that, had Nigeria not engaged the former Biafran
territory in a civil war, may be their technical might would not have been
put to test. That resulted in the production of the mysterious Ojukwu Bucket
(Ogbunigwe).
The issue of resource control seems to be at the centre of the latest
hostility. In the current political brouhaha that has divided the country
along North/South lines. Since this country is richly endowed with all sort
of natural resources, which Americans and Europeans cannot boast of, then
where lies the animosity? Alhaji Umaru Dikko, the leader of the Northern
delegates to the just "unconcluded confab," told the South-South that they
the North brought up the South-South, adding that before the discovery of
oil, the South South was parasitic on the agricultural produce from the
North.
He also went further to remind the South-South that without them (the
North), the Igbos would have reclaimed their houses in Port Harcourt in the
abandoned property saga and regretted that South South could forget so
easily. Adding that, if the South-South wanted history, they were ready to
remind them. He concluded by telling them that in every relationship, they
must know that there is a senior partner. Now, with all these issues on the
table, I want to ask what happened to agriculture that used to be the
mainstay of our economy. Was it outlawed by any decree?
It should have been on this basis that the issue of regionalism be
reconsidered, so that people can go back home and begin to resuscitate their
economic mainstay. For the avoidance of doubt, those who are afraid of
resource control and Nigeria's break-up (even though I don't believe in it)
should note that resource control encourages competition and elevates
capability.
Today, Nigeria is 45 years; the old Eastern Nigeria has held the
executive headship of the country for six months, the Western Nigeria nine
years so far, while North had been there for 35 years. A state like Nigeria,
where the winner takes all, where there is a senior partner, a state where
you love us or hate us we cannot be ignored with is number of years at the
helm of affairs, I think the northern populace should have been the
after-sought bride in education at this age, as the world has become a
global village through technology, Umaru Dikko's view should be treated with
"bugaboo."
Information abound that the north constitutes the majority in education
disadvantaged states and issues of this nature should have bothered people
like Dr. Umaru Dikko even though he had claimed that the North is well
educated just like their southern counterparts adding that the only
difference is that while the Southerners went to western school, the north
went to Islamic school and can even write better in Arabic.
He asked if Germans, French or Chinese speak English upon all their
exploits particularly in technology, which I have been looking forward to
seeing from our northern brothers. People like Major Abubakar Umar (rtd)
should lecture Umaru Dikko and bring to his knowledge that what the northern
youths need is libration from the shackles of oligarchic grip. Let this
serve as a letter to my fellow northern youths because the earlier they ask
their leaders questions about their plight, the better because they stand to
loose.
If I am to paraphrase the words of Abubakar Umar, he said that "the
north also has been lacking in development compared with the west, we have
not developed any resources in the north. The feudal masters themselves have
been masters of the poor. They have enslaved them, and used them for their
own material benefits. Up till today, I have not seen any part of the north
that has benefited from the feudal class.
On education and infrastructural development, he said that the north
today has the lowest standard of education and infrastructure development. A
sound mind, well-educated person is better than an ignorant one that does
not understand his environment.
As it is today, Nigeria is a federation where the Federal Government
holds the ace but at this time, it is high time the north took a second look
at the word of former American president J. F. Kennedy who said that people
should think of what they can do for their country and not what their
country can do for them. Please let us stop being lazy.
Today Nigeria cannot produce a toy gun because of fear of the
unknown. As I conclude, let us not forget the prediction of experts that oil
is an exhaustible natural resource, if the fifteen years period suggested by
them is anything to go by. That is, it is expected that the oil reserve will
dry up by 2020. Unfortunately, as a people with short memory of history, in
June this year, America informed us that there was the likelihood of Nigeria
distintegrating in 15 years' time thereby tallying with the same 2020.
Then, what will be our lot and the generations to come? With all these I
think it is high time we started mending our roofs because nobody knows when
the rainy season will set in.

Original_Search_Title: Odili, in-Laws: New Yam Festival Like No Other
Headline: Nigeria: Odili, in-Laws: New Yam Festival Like No Other
Body_Text:
Lagos — Last Monday, Mbaise in Imo State played host to people from all parts of Nigeria at the celeberation of this year's new yam festival and conferment of chieftaincy title on their son-in-law, Governor Peter Odili of Rivers State.
The roll call was a testimony to how important the event was. First the state governors: DSP Alamieseigha, Bayelsa; Ayo Fayose, Ekiti; Sam Egwu, Ebonyi; Abdullahi Adamu, Nassarawa. There was also Walter Eneji, deputy governor of Cross River State. And of course the host governor, Achike, who has never missed the new yam festival of Mbaise people every August 15 since his assumption of office six years ago.
From Rivers State, there were Dr. Frank Eke, Second Republic deputy governor of the state; Chief Alfred Diette-Spiff, one-time military administrator of River State; Alabo Tonye Graham-Douglas, former minister for Culture and Tourism; commissioners, traditional rulers and members of the State House of Assembly.
Even corporate Nigeria was well represented. Mrs. Cecilia Ibru, managing director of Oceanic Bank came with a retinue of staff. Dr. Raymond Dokpesi, chairman of DAAR Communications was conspicuous with a bandage in his hands. The event was covered live by Africa Independent Television (AIT). Chief Emmanuel Iwuanyanwu, publisher of Champion Newspapers, was also present.
For royal fathers, titled men and prominent indigenes of Mbaise, it was a day to remember. From Uvuru came Eze Peter A. Uduhirinwa of Isiala Uvuru and Chief Emmanuel I. Amuchie, Ugo Eji Ejemba of Uvuru. Mr. Fan Ndubuoke, former president of the Sports Writers Association of Nigeria (SWAN) and chairman of Mr Fans was present.
Ugoeze B.S. C. Nzenwa, mother of Justice Mary Odili, was present to witness the honour being bestowed on her daugther and son-in-law
They all were there to rejoice with Governor Peter Odili of Rivers State, who had come to be honoured by his in-laws, the Mbaise people and to rejoice with the people on this year's new yam festival.
As part of this year's new yam festival, called Iri-ji Mbaise, traditional rulers from all the five clans of Mbaise had resolved in their meeting held at Afor Oru, the headquarters of Ahiazu Mbaise on July 25 that as part of the new yam festival for this year, Odili, would be conferred with the chieftaincy title of Obinwanne Mbaise by the Mbaise Council of Traditional Rulers.
The traditional rulers' council jointly organised the event with Ezuruezu Mbaise, a group of professionals of Mbaise origin and Ezeji Mbaise, a group yam title holders in Mbaise.
As would be expected, the event was marked with pomp and pageantry. No one would expect anything less in an event that brought together the creme de la creme of the Nigerian society.
Odili came resplendent in his trade mark Ikwerre traditional attire with cap to match. He was accompanied by his wife, Justice Mary Odili of the Court of Appeal, their son and two of their daughters.
The Rivers State governor went round to shake hands with guests after which he was guided to his seat.
The chairman of the occasion, Chief Innocent Nwoga, former Secretary for Establishment, in his remark, said it was the first Iri-Ji Mbaise to record the presence of six sitting state governors. According to him, there was hardly any other event that speaks as eloquently as the new yam festival on the unity of Mbaise people.
Nwoga said the presence of Odili and his wife gives Mbaise people immense joy and told the couple that they were welcomed any time as Mbaise was their second home.
He also commended Udenwa, whom he said has never missed any edition of Iri-Ji Mbaise since his assumption of office as governor.
The chairman, Mbaise Council of Traditional Rulers, Eze E. O. Osuagwu, in his address, although traditional rulers, cultural organisations and individuals celebrate the festival according to their peculiar circumstance, Mbaise has given the new yam feast a pride of place in cultural history of Imo State and Nigeria.
For that reason, he said, August 15 every year has become a household day set aside by "the people of Mbaise and their friends to assemble at an appointed venue to taste the new yam and give thanks to God for not only bounteous harvests but also for enabling us to see another year of harvest."
He called on local and state governments as well as Mbaise sons and their friends to institute annual award for the best yam and cassava farmers in Mbaise. He said this would act as a catalyst to agriculture, which is one of the components of the tripod vision of the Udenwa Administration.
Eze Osuagwu expressed gratitude to the Udenwa for the various projects which have alleviated poverty in the state especially the network of roads traversing all parts of the state.
However, he drew attention of the governor to the deplorable state "of the Afor Oru-Attah-Ukwu Orji road, which has remained neglected by past administrations but which provides the shortest link between Mbaise and other parts of the state."
He continued: "We do not need to mention the Owerri-Mbaise-Umuahia road, particularly, its Mbaise ends, for we know that you know this more than we do. We plead with you to look into these roads."
Turning to the guest of honour, Odili, the traditional ruler said: "We lack words to appreciate your presence in our midst today. You have continued to make us proud through your dynamism, philanthropic disposition, intelligence, simplicity, godliness and political sagacity. You have continued to prove that we did not make a mistake when we offered you, our cherished daughter, Justice Mary Odili, in marriage.
"As worthy son-in-law, we want to assure you of the good wishes and prayerful support of the Mbaise people in your political calculations for the future. We implore you to also factor the political interest of Mbaise and its people in all your undertakings."
Furthermore, Osuagwu requested Odili to put in place a memorial for the remembrance of his (Odili's) father-in-law, the late Eze B. S. C. Nzenwa.
Finally, he called on the governors present to assist the Mbaise Council of Traditional Rulers to equip its office with generator and modern office gadgets to be able to function effectively.
Thereafter, Dr. Charles Amanze, chairman, Governing Board, University of Nigeria Teaching Hospital (UNTH), Enugu and publicity secretary, Ezuruezu Mbaise, was called upon to read Odili's citation.
Amanze told the audience how Odili read medicine at the University of Nigeria, met his wife there. She, too was a student but in the law faculty. Now, they have children who went to the same university to read law and medicine.
Odili, according to Amanze, worked at the University of Benin Teaching Hospital before coming home to become chief medical director of Pamo Clinic, Port Harcourt.
He told the audience of the achievements of Odili in his six years as governor of Rivers State. Most importantly, while acknowledging the effort of other state governments to put in place Independent Power Projects (IPPs), he said Odili was the first governor to complete an IPP.
Amanze said Odili had received no less than 17 chieftaincy titles from different parts of the country including one from the Ooni of Ife, Oba Sijuade Okunade. The way he has garnered chieftaincy titles, Amanze said, was the way the Rivers State chief executive has received awards and doctorate degrees. These, he said, were in appreciation of his achievements and his ability to make friends that cut across ethnic and regional boundaries.
For Odili there was no better gift to mark his 57th birthday which was last week than the honour from Mbaise people. After the citation, he, accompanied by his wife and other governors, was led up to the platform for the conferment of the title of Obinwanne Mbaise.
Since it was a title jointly given by all the traditional rulers of the land, his decoration and presentation of paraphernalia of office was done by several traditional rulers. For instance, Eze Stanley Iwu, vice-chairman of the Mbaise traditional rulers' council wore the governor the royal robe said to have been made by several people.
Eze S. O. Uwaegbu, secretary of the council put the royal bead on the neck of the governor while the bead on his wrist was placed by Eze J. S. Anyamele, financial secretary of the council.
He was decorated with elephant tusk by Eze Obinna Obasi while Eze Ositadinma Nwokocha, chairman, Ezinihitte Council of Traditional Rulers presented the governor with royal wrapper. Because wrapper is not tied in public, that task was given to Justice Mary Odili to perform later when the governor would be dressing up for an important outing.
After the conferment of the title of Obinwanne Mbaise, the next event unveiling of the new yam, which normally is the high point of the new yam festival. The yam title holders led by their chairman, Ezeji T. Eze Ihuoma, went into the yam barn prepared at the middle of the arena with palm fronds. It was there that ceremony was performed. The governors present cut the yam as part of the ceremony.
With the cutting of the new yam on August 15, any Mbaise person could now eat the new yam as doing so before the new yam festival is not acceptable. No true son or daughter of Mbaise eats new yam before the festival.
Many people expressed satisfaction with the organisation of this year's festival and the fact that the event has been attracting prominent personalities over the years.
A few years ago, Vice President Atiku Abubakar was special guest at the Mbaise new yam festival. It is one ceremony that has given the Mbaise person cultural identity.

Original_Search_Title: Union Warns Commercial Farmers
Headline: Zambia: Union Warns Commercial Farmers
Body_Text:
THE Agricultural Technical and Professional Staff Union of Zambia (ATPSUZ) has implored commercial farmers to immediately release onto the local market the stocks of maize they are hoarding in the interest of the nation.
ATPSUZ secretary general Teza Nchinga has also asked the commercial farmers to stop asking Government to waive 15 per cent import duty in order to allow them to import maize.
At a Press briefing in Lusaka yesterday, Mr Nchinga wondered whom the commercial farmers were keeping the maize for if they were requesting Government to allow them to import the same commodity that they were hoarding in their bonded warehouses.
He said the union found it hypocritical on the part of the commercial farmers to resolve now that the Food Reserve Agency (FRA) should offload onto the market the available maize stocks.
He called on Government to begin to balance the interest of both the small-scale farmers and commercial farmers in the national agricultural policy.
He called on Government to quickly use the K59.13 billion reserved in the 2005 budget to buy maize, rice and cassava in all outlaying areas to add to the fast dwindling stocks of reserves.
He urged Government to find the mid-point between pursuing a capitalist or socialist approach to agricultural development since Zambia had adopted the capitalist road which had failed to yield positive results.
Zambia National Farmers Union (ZNFU) president Guy Robinson disputed claims that commercial farmers were hoarding maize and not willing to off-load it.
Mr Robinson said it was absolutely unbelievable that commercial farmers could be accused of hoarding maize when there was no single farmer who is involved in the practice.
"I am yet to come across a commercial farmer who is hoarding maize because there is no one hoarding the commodity even if they had to go round they will never find one," he said
Meanwhile, the larger grain borer (LGB) has destroyed about 30 per cent of the 12,000 maize reserve stock under the Food Reserve Agency (FRA) in Kazungula in Southern Province.
Kazungula District Cooperative Union manager in charge of the maize stock Muchabila Mwiinga, said the worst hit sheds were the Nyawa and Kawuwe sheds with the former having 700 by 50 kilogramme bags and the latter 5,500 by 50kg bags destroyed respectively.
Mr Mwiinga said of the sheds storing maize stock for the FRA in the district three of the sheds had condemned maize stocks because it had been attacked by the LGB.
He named the sheds as Nyawa, Kawuwe, Makunka, Kabuyu, and Ngwezi, which were not affected.
Mr Mwiinga attributed the condition of the rotten maize to negligence and poor storage facilities and stated that the Kalomo District Cooperative handed over the maize to him in July when it was already partly damaged.
Meanwhile, Mr Mwiinga has charged that the decision made by Government to allocate only 150 packs of agriculture-subsidised inputs in Kazungula district is unfortunate.
He complained that Kazungula had 12,000 farmers and the 150 packs would not cater for most of them who had the potential to buy.

Original_Search_Title: Bank of Industry Gives N1.3 Billion Loan for Cassava Glucose Industry
Headline: Nigeria: Bank of Industry Gives N1.3 Billion Loan for Cassava Glucose Industry
Body_Text:
The Bank of Industry (BOI) has set aside N1.3 billion for the Ekha Agro Farms Ltd to fund its Cassava Glucose Syrup Company in Ibafo, Ogun State.
An Executive Director (ED) of the bank, Mr. Ubadibo Okonkwo, disclosed this while presenting the report of the implementation committee on Ekha Agro Farms Ltd to the Minister of Commerce, Alh. Idris Waziri, in Abuja, Wednesday.
He said that about N1.1 billion had already been released to the company to enable it purchase required equipment from abroad and expressed the bank's willingness to release the entire sum in record time, to enable the company take advantage of the huge raw materials available in the country.
Okonkwo also said that experts from the International Starch Institute (ISI) based in Denmark, which is collaborating with Ekha Agro Farms Ltd, would arrive in Nigeria at the end of this month to commence work on the project.
The Executive Director who stated that the major equipments needed for the commencement of the industry have already arrived the shores of Nigeria said the project would be ready for commissioning by October this year. He explained that Ekha Agro Farms Ltd. has commenced providing on-lending to farmers to enable them plant enough cassava for the company, even as the company has acquired 500 hectares of land and another 150 hectares of land in two different locations have been acquired for cassava plantation.
The project, according to him, has reached 60 percent completion level and that all expatriates needed to offer technical services would arrive this month to man operations of the company which is expected to have its independent infrastructural facilities such as generator, for steady power supply and water, among others.
Responding, the minister pledged to assist the company in securing every necessary assistance from the government in an attempt to make the project successful and a model for many more in the country.
He enjoined Nigerians to go into massive cassava production, given the many uses to which the produce can be put and make it a major source of foreign exchange for the nation.

Original_Search_Title: Patronise Local Tractor Makers, Govt Advised
Headline: Nigeria: Patronise Local Tractor Makers, Govt Advised
Body_Text:
Lagos — National Network for Small-scale Industrialists (NANSSI) has advised the Federal Government to shelve its plan to import tractors from China.
In a statement sent to THISDAY, president of the organisation Mr. Olukayode Fadahunsi described as unfortunate, the statement credited to the Minister of Agriculture and Rural Development, Otunba Bamidele Dada that Nigeria had concluded plans to import about 150 intermediate tractors known as power tillers, for distribution to co-operative farm groups.
THISDAY reported on Tuesday that Otunba Dada stated this while receiving officials of Genertic International Corporation of China, who were around to have a first-hand knowledge of cassava production and processing techniques.
Fadahunsi noted that it would be inappropriate to import tractors when there were indigenous companies that had responded to government's call on the private sector to be part of the development initiative by establishing small scale industries.
Fadahunsi cited as an example, Betcy Industries Ltd. that had opened a factory in Amuwo Odofin in Lagos, for the manufacture of Power brand of tractors.
According to Fadahunsi, Betcy's tractors were manufactured in partnership with Chinese experts and had after sales back-up with spare parts.
He wondered why the government would not encourage such companies by patronship, while encouraging state governments and other organisations to patronise these indigenous companies.
He said Power tractors manufactured by Betcy Industries are made to fit the Nigerian environment and soil.
The NANSSI president noted that there was no way the government's objective of achieving sustainable economic development and poverty alleviation in the country could succeed if local manufacturers are not encouraged.
He drew a parallel between the importation of BMW cars to organise the All Africa Games in 2003 when cars manufactured by Peugeot Automobile of Nigeria (PAN) were lying waste in Kaduna.
Fadahunsi said the minister should reconsider the decision in order not to kill indigenous companies.
"The government will save foreign exchange and jobs will be created or at least saved if government begins to patronise our local tractor manufacturers," he said.
He commended the cassava initiative of the government and urged it to partner with local equipment manufacturers to ensure success of the project.

Original_Search_Title: 'Government Committed to Women Empowerment'
Headline: Zimbabwe: 'Government Committed to Women Empowerment'
Body_Text:
Harare — GOovernment is fully committed to empower women for a complete turnaround of the economy through land and the revitalisation of various programmes that had been abandoned, Women Affairs, Gender and Community Development Minister Cde Oppah Muchinguri has said.
Addressing participants at the belated commemorations of the World Population Day in Harare yesterday, Cde Muchinguri said there was need for women to take centre stage in the utilisation of the $50 billion availed by the Government in the Mid-Term Fiscal Policy Review on Tuesday. Yesterday's event was jointly organised by her ministry and the United Nations Population Fund (UNFPA).
This year's theme was "To Promote Gender Equality and Empower Women".
"We shall resuscitate women's clubs that were in fashion in the 1980s as one way to empower women. This is to be done in community centres across the country. We are encouraging women to take up cassava and sweet potato projects being spearheaded by my ministry in different parts of the country as a means to reduce dependence among our women. These are drought-resistant crops and are easy to grow countrywide.
"Since education is the key to our total liberty, we have engaged the Women's University to carry out a needs analysis before adopting it nationally."
Cde Muchinguri told the gathering that her ministry had the responsibility of implementing strategies across the country so as to catapult women's efforts in all sectors of the economy.
"This ministry was given the mandate to co-ordinate policies and implement them at district level. We are going to focus on the girl-child and the woman without leaving out men for the smooth transformation of ideals into action," said Cde Muchinguri.
She invited women to take up the 60 percent land offer by the Government from the portion recovered from those who had not occupied it after allocation during the land redistribution exercise.
"I advise women to take up the land on a lease basis that will see them putting both their children and husbands as beneficiaries," she said.
Dr Bruce Campbell, the UNFPA country representative, said engendering equality between men and women was a UN guiding principle with the aim of achieving fair treatment for women in all spheres of the economy worldwide.
These efforts, she said, would be bolstered by the enactment of the Prevention and Protection of Victims Bill, which seeks to protect women against domestic and all forms of violence, intimidation and harassment, among other aims. In the same vein, Ms Sheila Mahere, the Musasa Project director, said "about one in every six women in the country suffers abuse in one way or another", which had the effect of undermining women's self-confidence to a severe extent.
Health and Child Welfare Minister Cde David Parirenyatwa said the traditional male perception of women should change in order to pave way for productive dialogue on some cultural issues.
"Independence brought us liberty which should be extended to both sexes. Polygamy should be stopped, especially in this HIV and Aids era, for women to enjoy their liberty in marriage," said Cde Parirenyatwa.
In a speech read on his behalf by Dr Josephine Chiware, Dr Parirenyatwa said women should take part in deciding family sizes and methods of family planning to complement Government efforts in the promotion of women's health for a healthy nation.

Original_Search_Title: Golden Age of Business and The Collapse of Local Industries (1)
Headline: Ghana: Golden Age of Business and The Collapse of Local Industries (1)
Body_Text:
Kumasi — We have made it easy for someone to obtain import license to import rice than to help the same person to get loan to grow the rice in Ghana. (Flt Lt Jerry John Rawlings, Ghana's Moment of Truth, 1983)
Some few years ago some major companies in the United States eg Enron collapsed under the weight of poor accounting practices. So we can blame the management for the collapse of such industries.
When it comes to Ghana the blame is corporately owned-the president and the people of Ghana we all have an equal share in the collapse of local industries.
Collapses of industries in Ghana is as old as the earliest history books and contemporary as the morning's newspapers. Most of the state-owned enterprise (SOE) set up by Dr Kwame Nkrumah started collapsing. after 1966 till today. Not only SOE's are collapsing but also privately owned enterprises have also collapsed.
Ghanaians were relieved when the president at the time of his first inaugural address said that there would be "golden age of business".
Less than fours years after this proclamation was made Ghanaian companies are folding up from the pressures of cheap imports from Asia. Punitive taxes put to deter people from bringing poultry products into the country have been slashed causing the local poultry farmers to suffocate. Day by day you hear poultry farmers saying that they do not believe in the principle of going concern in business. They cannot see the future of their businesses.
It is not only the poultry factories that are folding up but also textile factories-Akosombo and Juapong.
The solution to this problem can be expressed in one word called DOMESTICATION. One does not need an extensive vocabulary for understanding. To most Ghanaians we have taken the concept of domestication literally as just as synonym for Dan Lartey. In modern Ghanaian politics Dan Lartey holds the trademark of the concept of domestication.
When Arthur Lewis wrote his famous report on industrialization in the Gold Coast in 1953 he enunciated a very important principle, namely the principle of import substitution as the cornerstone to our economic development. Subsequently he drew repeated attention to the importance of agriculture as the basis for the country's economic development in terms of what it could do to provide for the people and of what it could do to provide raw materials for the industries.
Now the time has come for us as nation is to see the concept of domestication as part of the solution to our national problem. For more than four decades we have relied and depended on foreign goods and donor funds to survive. Now after nearly fifty years of existence we have reached our menopause stage and we should be able to depend on ourselves for survival. In biological terms when a woman reaches her menopause it is believed that the woman has set her house and things in order.
If we want to get to somewhere we must produce what we want and use what we produce. The buy Made in Ghana goods campaign must be sustained. Thank God that nowadays every Friday we wear locally made fabric to work and other places. The Ghana Education Service must allow secondary schools to wear a customized traditional wear on Thursdays and Fridays. This will add lifeline to our ailing textiles and garment factories. I can imagine Dr. Kwame Nkrumah looking at us and asking what you done to all these companies which I established?
Go to Tema Habour or AFGO and have a look at things which are imported into the country. You will see something like toothpick, toilet roll, used underwears, used plates and cups, chalk, pencils, matches, used tyres, used combs and coffin.
The white man will not eat our cassava unless he is using it for pharmaceutical purposes. We should appreciate all that God has given to us a nation and use it.
Today our tastes for foreign products have outdistanced the local ones. This has even caused our shoemakers when they manufacture good shoes they have to label it MADE IN ITALY fearing that should it be labeled as MADE IN GHANA it would not get the needed market. When it comes to rice is worse. It is believed that we our annual rice import is more than US$100million which far exceeds budgetary allocation to the Ministry of Food and Agriculture.
When the campaign was made for us to buy and patronize made in Ghana rice it would surprise you that when you visit the storeroom of the government official holding the trump card you will find shades of imported rice from Sultana to Chicago Star.
The reason why government has not been acting well enough to save our local industries is that because the party chairman of the ruling party or any influential party contributor is importing rice, poultry products and other goods from somewhere so should the government move in to prevent importation of goods that can be produced here in Ghana the man's contributions to the party would not be forthcoming.
This is where we are after almost 50 years of self-governance. So we have maximized our selfish interest and minimized national interest.
When Major Courage Quashiga was then Minister for Food and Agriculture he did all humanly possible to make the local rice a competitive alternative to the imported rice. Today the campaign has died down. Because .?
Even chalks which can be manufactured in the country we prefer it to be imported from China or Far East. The reason is that a minister will be getting something from the importer.

Original_Search_Title: Clarify Maize Situation - ZACA
Headline: Zambia: Clarify Maize Situation - ZACA
Body_Text:
THE Zambia Consumer Association (ZACA) is concerned over what it terms
conflicting statements from Government and other stakeholders over maize
sufficiency in the country.
ZACA executive secretary Muyunda Ililonga appealed to Government to
clarify the apparent uncertainty on whether the country had enough maize or
not.
Mr Ililonga said that as long as conflicting statements continued over
the maize issue, no corrective measures would be undertaken.
He said if there was an actual maize shortage as the Food Reserve Agency
(FRA) and the Central Statistical Office (CSO) were saying, Government
needed to move quickly to address the situation in order to avert
hunger.
"Government should act quickly and source for more maize because the
region generally has experienced a drought," he said.
Mr Ililonga said it was better for Government to import extra maize at a
high cost rather than having nothing when there would be need for more
maize.
"While we are quarreling here, our friends are busy preparing and
sourcing for more maize to avoid shortages," he said.
He said it was worrying that there were talks of the country only having
maize stocks to last only up to three months.
Mr Ililonga advised against waiting until the country plunged into
problems before acting on the maize deficit.
"Even talks of cassava being available, this won't change matters
because maize will always be preferred and so Government should ensure its
availability as soon as possible," he said.

Original_Search_Title: FG Tasks States, LGs On Agric Infrastructure
Headline: Nigeria: FG Tasks States, LGs On Agric Infrastructure
Body_Text:
Lagos — SPECIAL Adviser to the President on Food Security, Dr. (Mrs.) Oluwatoyin Adetunji has urged states and local authorities to provide basic infrastructure to boost activities of agricultural sector.
Speaking at a two-day seminar on "Adding Value to Cassava: A New Agricultural Revolution" in Lagos, Dr. Adetunji said the two tiers of government should complement efforts of federal government in ensuring agricultural development.
She said such support should be in areas of provision of rural amenities, micro-processing centres and supply of tractors for land preparation among others.
She noted that there were untapped potentials and insatiable markets for cassava tubers in gari production, starch, glucose syrup, chip industries and ethanol.
"We have no option but to tap these potentials which will reduce unemployment, boost wealth creation and have positive impact on the development of local economic environment thereby ensuring food and better standard of living," she added.
Dr. Adetunji urged commercial banks to complement efforts of federal government by providing soft loans for farmers.
She maintained that government had increased the funding of the Nigerian Agricultural Development Bank (NAGRDB) in order to meet the challenges of agricultural sector.
Adetunji said that as part of efforts to draw Nigerian's attention to opportunities that exist in cassava sub-sector, her office in conjuction with International Institute of Tropical Agriculture (IITA), Nigerian Root crop Research Institute (NRCRI) and the standard Organisation of Nigeria (SON) had between January and May this year carried out sensitisation and training programmes on the production and packaging of high quality cassava flour in 28 states of the federation.
The government, she added, has also funded research exercise which produced five high yielding cassava varieties that could adapt to all ecological zones in the country.
In his remark, CSAD Executive Director, Deacon Agbor Ndoma said the seminar was packaged to address constraints to cassava production.

Original_Search_Title: Dilemma in Jumbo-Human Conflict
Headline: Kenya: Dilemma in Jumbo-Human Conflict
Body_Text:
Nairobi — A boy is treated in hospital following an attack by a lion.
They are intelligent, nasty, brutal and averse to electric shock.
They can kill, shut down schools and create a 6pm-to-6am curfew.
They are the elephants in - and quite often, out of - the Shimba Hills
Game Reserve in Kwale District.
"We live at the mercy of the elephants that leave the forest at night
and come to feed on our crops. There is nothing we can do because they are
protected by the Government," laments a resident.
The Kenya Wildlife Service (KWS), residents claim, cares for the animals
but does nothing for them when their property is destroyed. And sever
punishment is meted out on them if they dare kill or injure a predator.
"You will be sent to jail for seven years for killing a wild animal,"
says a resident.
But the Mombasa regional KWS director Benjamin Kavu believes that the
human-wildlife conflict in Kwale District is man-made.
Animals, he says, lived in the Shimba Hills Forest Reserve and
Mwaluganje Forest for centuries. And since there was no human settlement
between the two forests and Tsavo East National Park, the animals migrated
freely.
"Elephants used to migrate from Tsavo to these two forests every year to
mate or tend calves," Mr Kavu says.
When human settlements started coming up along the migratory routes like
Mackinnon Road, Kinangop, Samburu and Taru in the 1960s the migration
pattern was interrupted.
Some elephant families that were left behind in the forests could not
join the others in Tsavo and vice versa.
Yet elephants live in families, just like human beings, and are also
very intelligent, with very good memory, says Mr Kavu.
Originally, there were less than 200 elephants trapped in the Shimba
Hills Game Reserve and Mwaluganje Elephant Sanctuary.
When the Government created the Shimba Hills Settlement Scheme after
independence in 1963, it demarcated and set aside 250 square kilometres in
Shimba Hills Forest and Game Reserve, and made it the home for the
elephants.
Conflicts between the elephants and human beings intensified as the
population of the animals in the forests increased. Now the animals are
about 700, more than three times the number that the two sanctuaries can
support.
Even the electric fence constructed around the reserve to restrain the
animals within the forests and stop them from going out to interfere with
people's lives and property has not helped much. Most of the 120-kilometre
fence has been run down by the animals in their quest to go out of the
confinement to look for food.
Being intelligent, the elephants have devised a way of destroying the
electric fence without getting electrocuted. They start by pushing the posts
with their tusks, which are poor conductors of electricity. When the posts
fall, the jumbos walk between the wires on the ground, carefully avoiding
them - and into people's farms.
The senior warden of Shimba Hills game reserve Jonathan Kirui said that
because of this, KWS is always repairing the fence.
"We are in fact planning to replace the fence with a modern high-tech
elephant proof one when we get enough money, and we shall surely beat the
elephant at its game," he said.
A Kenya Wildlife Service officer walks away after killing an elephant
that had strayed from Aberdare's Keita Forest into Ngarariga Village in
Kiambu District in this file picture.
At the moment Mr Kirui acknowledges that the elephants have been, to a
large extent, the cause of persistent famine in the areas surrounding the
game reserve.
He said over 30,000 fruit trees, which include mango, orange and palm
had been destroyed. "Food crops like maize, cassava, bananas, beans and
other crops have become food for the elephants before they are ready for
harvest ... every season," he said.
"The soil in our shambas, as you can see, is very fertile, and with good
rains, we can never complain of famine and poverty. Were it not for these
beasts that destroy our crops each season we would be fine," Mzee Mwagari
Gari, a resident near the reserve, lamented.
Mrs Timina Mwasi from Shimba Hills Settlement Scheme says the elephants
have impoverished them. Apart from destroying crops and property, the
elephants have also imposed a curfew on them from 6pm to 600am, within which
anyone venturing out of the house risks being attacked by the jumbos. "It's
like the elephants have declared war on us," she adds.
Because of the curfew, many man-hours are lost and school programme
affected. "This is a conspiracy between the Government and the elephants to
make us poor and retard education of our children," she claims, adding that
some schools are often closed altogether due to the elephant menace.
Mr Kirui agreed that the animals were a real bother to the residents,
and the damage they were causing was devastating and contributed to the
persistent famine and poverty in the area.
He said that since January this year, five people had been seriously
injured and another two trampled to death by rogue elephants.
Sadly, he said, the Government did not compensate people for their crops
or property destroyed by wild animals, only for injuries and lives
lost.
He said the Wildlife Management and Conservation Act of 1989 stipulated
a compensation of Sh15,000 for people injured and Sh30,000 for those
killed.
The amount, according to the residents, is so grossly inadequate, it
adds to the belief that the Government values animals more than its
citizen.
And the bureaucracy involved in claiming the compensation is so tedious
many people give up and never get the money at all.
The game warden agreed that the process was cumbersome because claims
had to be verified by the district compensation committee chaired by the
district commissioner and forwarded to Nairobi, where a national
compensation committee subjected them to further scrutiny before KWS could
pay through the district compensation committee.
He said compensation committees did not convene regularly and sometimes
took several months to sit after a complaint had been filed.
"This is government policy, and there is nothing that we, as KWS, can
do," Mr Kirui said. But he was optimistic that the relocation of animals was
going to provide a lasting solution to the conflict in the area.
Mr Kavu, Mr Kirui and the communities who have lived with the elephants
for decades agree on three things: that elephants are intelligent, live in
families and behave like human beings in many ways.
They believe that elephants do not forget their home and relatives, and
when offended, will always revenge. They become angry, hostile and violent
on human beings when they were disturbed. And that is when they can be
really destructive.
This makes the idea of moving the elephants out of Shimba Hills as a
solution to the animal overpopulation a tricky and risky activity of
doubtful value.
The Government plans to move 40 elephants from the reserve to northern
parts of Tsavo National Park starting this month, but this has been opposed
by leaders from both Kwale and Taita-Taveta districts.
Kinango MP Gonzi Rai, during a public baraza, recently said he feared
the damage that the animals could cause when coming back to look for their
relatives could be worse than the one they were causing now.
He said the animals may not like their new environment after being
separated from their families, and may get angry and violent as they return
through the former migratory routes and cause a devastating havoc.
At the same time, their relatives left behind would likely become more
hostile, and while trying to look for their lost relatives, destroy a lot of
property.
Mr Gonzi said moving the animals would only make the problem worse, and
suggested that they be culled.
But Mr Kavu thinks differently. Although he agrees that an attempt by
KWS to move the elephants them three years ago failed, he blames the method
used at the time.
"The animals were selected at random in disregard of their family units,
and this disrupted their living pattern," he said. "Besides the elephants
were moved to the southern area of the park, just a few kilometres from the
reserve, making it easy for them to find their way back.
"This time round we shall move them in the family units which we have
already identified. We shall also take them to the extreme north of Tsavo
National Park, 500 kilometres away."
He added that KWS had decided to take the animals to Ithumba area in
Kitui District, which, he said, was more habitable for them, but did not
rule out the possibility of the elephants trying to return, only insisting
it would be difficult for them.
Still, the Kinango MP, who says his constituency is the most affected by
the elephant menace, is not convinced that what the Government intends to do
will help.
"The Government values wild animals more than it values human beings,"
he told Coast provincial commissioner during the Kwale District Education
and Prize Giving Day at the Kwale Baraza Park on July 22.
He said that the Government would spend Sh500,000 to move one elephant,
yet paid a paltry Sh30,000 to the next of kin of a person killed by the
beast.
And Ramisi Ward councillor Mshenga Ruga said: "If the Government can
establish a tourist police unit to protect visitors, why can't it establish
a special unit to protect its citizens from wild animals?"
But the regional KWS director has called upon leaders in Kwale,
Tait-Taveta and Kitui districts to educate their people on the importance of
the project and accord KWS the necessary support. (KNA)

Original_Search_Title: A View of The Maize Shortage
Headline: Zambia: Opinion
Body_Text:
Government and the farming community seem to be viewing the maize situation in Zambia from different angles.
While the farmers say Zambia is faced with a maize shortfall of 186,000 tonnes which will have to be imported, Government on the other hand insists that we have enough stocks and there is no need to panic.
Shortfall or no shortfall, we are of the view that a true reflection of the situation on the ground must be tabled sooner rather than later.
Government, the Food Reserve Agency (FRA), the Zambia National Farmers Union (ZNFU) and other stakeholders must put their heads together and clear the confusion on whether we have enough maize stocks or our granaries and silos are running empty.
They may also need a reminder that recently, Indeni Oil Refinery in Ndola experienced technical problems and was forced to shut down. That shut-down resulted in a serious shortage of diesel and jet fuel.
The two-week long diesel shortage had devastating effects on the economy, and was quite costly too to the country.
Diesel is still in short supply and so is jet fuel which has put the airline industry in a quandary.
The lessons learnt from Indeni included the need for strategic reserves as the country struggled to meet demand with imported fuels.
For the maize industry, while Zambia may have strategic reserves in place which may have to be off-loaded on the market soon, perhaps the solution lies in part in diversification.
There are several other crops such as sorghum, cassava and rice that served as staple food side by side with maize in the past.
Encouraging Zambians to grow and live on sorghum, rice and cassava as their provinces dictated in the past will reduce pressure on maize.
The maize situation has also been worsened by the ever increasing demand for the grain by opaque beer brewers and stockfeed manufacturers.
We are, therefore, calling for a proper audit on the maize stocks in the country. Just what has been collected and what has not been collected from outlying areas?
Just how much will have to be imported and by who - Government or the private sector.
The earlier the main players in the maize sector resolve their differences, the better for Zambia since our staple food has in the past caused unnecessary friction.

Original_Search_Title: Civilization And the Penal System
Headline: Ghana: Civilization And the Penal System
Body_Text:
A news summary dated Monday, July 11, 2005, and posted on Ghana's Peace-FM radio's website reported the quite fascinating story of Belgian prison inmates who are reportedly granted a single two-hour conjugal visit from their spouses and partners every month by the system's administrators. What is fascinating about this story is not the simple fact of prisoners being afforded conjugal visits from their spouses and companions; after all, we know this to be the case vis-à-vis the U. S. prison system, and also that of Apartheid South Africa during the period of former president Nelson R. Mandela's incarceration on Robben Island and elsewhere around that country.
What is significantly and interestingly fascinating about this story is the fact that this writer never heard of the issue, or question, of "conjugal visits" being once discussed vis-à-vis the Ghanaian prison system. In sum, on the preceding theme and score, it is almost as if the Ghanaian prisoner deserved no such "human" right. Or perhaps it existed, except that I just wasn't aware of it, since any talk of prison, at least until the P/NDC era when it became a political commonplace, invariably attracted the kind of unspoken taboo that AIDS and prostitution have attracted in recent times. Still, it would be quite interesting to learn about the "conjugal visit" regime as it pertains to Ghanaian prison inmates.
Chances are that there might not exist any such system or regime at all. For generally speaking, the Ghanaian prisoner is a virtual pariah, a forgotten entity, a veritable nonentity, in fact.
Which largely explains why distinguished political prisoners like Dr. J. B. Danquah, Mr. Koi-Larbi, Mr. Joe Appiah and others were once housed in the Condemned Cell Block (or Death-Row) at Ghana's notorious Nsawam Medium-Security Prison under the Nkrumah regime. And also that President Nkrumah reportedly fumed when he learned, from Inspector-General of Police Mr. J. W. K. Harlley, that Dr. Danquah, as well as other political prisoners, was being fed standard dietary fare, pretty much gives us some useful insight into the Ghanaian mainstream perception of prison inmates and prison culture in general. And here, also, it is rather tragic to recall that President Nkrumah, a self-proclaimed "Prison Graduate," under the British colonial regime, did not appear to have learned, or taken to heart, any meaningful lessons, whatsoever, regarding the deplorable prison conditions experienced by erstwhile Gold Coast radical politicians under the British colonial administration. If anything at all, prison life and culture appear to have fascinated the pioneering premier of post-colonial Ghana to the pathological extent of reportedly prompting the former political prisoner to mete out the harshest forms of treatment - including the institutionalization of protein-deficient menus - to politicians and statesmen incarcerated at his behest.
For instance, in the case of Dr. Danquah, President Nkrumah ordered that the former be fed on a daily diet of salted water and garri - farinated cassava - according to National Liberation Council member and former IGP, Mr. J. W. K. Harlley (see Enquiry into the Detention and Death of Dr. Danquah at Nsawam Prison. Accra-Tema: Ghana Information Service, 1967).
NOVELTY
Indeed, it may have been the widely accepted short-shrift treatment accorded Ghanaian prisoners, generally speaking, which motivated the operators and proprietors of Peace-FM radio to post the story regarding the niggardly allowance of "conjugal visits" to Belgian prisoners on the station's website. For, needless to say, in Ghana, as can be readily fathomed, the idea of affording prisoners "conjugal visits" from their spouses reeks of the outright defeatist and preposterous. In sum, any talk of "conjugal visits" for prison inmates, on the part of the average Ghanaian, sounds like a bad joke and may well verge on outright lunacy. After all, what is the primary purpose of incarceration but to deprive the subject or prison inmate of the normative rights and privileges of non-convicts, of which the right to consensual coition or sexual intercourse with a recognized partner is part and parcel.
Consequently, the heading, or slug, that the Peace-FM operators gave their posted article on "conjugal visits" in Belgium could only be deemed in terms of the sarcastic. Emblazoned in bold print was the caption: "Extra Sex Time For Convicts." Initially, this writer's attention was drawn to the aforementioned story because he thought, perhaps, it was about convicts being sentenced to have marathon sexual bouts in prison - which would have been quite "newsy," for the very fact of its novelty, as he routinely teaches his journalism students. But the brief, two-paragraph story was quite fascinating in other ways. In sum, it dealt with the study of a Belgian Catholic University student on that country's prison system, which concluded that the monthly two-hour "conjugal visits" afforded prison inmates was grossly inadequate, unromantic and outright inhumane, which also gives a fascinating dimension to the theme of the prisoner and human rights.
And here, also, it is significant to observe that the study did not make any distinction between political and non-political prisoners. In other words, the right of a convicted murderer to "conjugal visits" is envisaged as being equal to that of any other prison inmate. And the latter drift is no strange tune to Western prison culture. Indeed, a little over a decade ago, a convicted attempted murderess, Amy Fisher, right here in New York State, was overheard discussing "conjugal visits" with a young man whom the teenage convict believed to be genuinely in love with her. It shortly turned out that the entire episode was a trap set up by the alleged suitor, or boyfriend, for purposes other than pure romance and intimacy, if memory serves this writer accurately. But what is even more fascinating is the fact that Belgium's Justice Minister, Laurette Onkelinx, has requested a copy of that country's Catholic University student's study decrying the woefully inadequate "conjugal visits" afforded prison inmates. The student in question, Ina van Havere, concluded in her study that the niggardly brief "conjugal visits" afforded Belgian prison inmates have the deleterious impact of straining relationships; in other words, such brief sexual encounters allowable to prison inmates and their non-inmate spouses often leave either couple emotionally dissatisfied with the other.
