The implication here, it goes without saying, is that rather than cementing conjugal relationships in times of crises, the brief "conjugal visits" actually serve to sever relationships by, quite predictably, forcing the non-inmate spouse, or partner, to seek more emotionally fulfilling intimacy elsewhere.
HUMANITY
The next most logical question becomes: But why should anybody bother whether an inmate's relationship with a partner on the outside is strained or cemented by such brief "conjugal visits"? And the logical answer appears to be that in many a civilized society, the mere fact of one's incarceration, for whatever number of reasons, fundamentally does not detract from one's immutable humanity as well as the need to treat the societally sanctioned as such, a bona fide human being with the right to the visceral expression of one's humanity.
Another dimension of the story and one that is clearly muted, but may yet be its most significant dimension, is the fact that both the student who conducted the study, Ms. Ina van Havere, and the Belgian Justice Minister, Laurette Onkelinx, are women. And in a heavily patriarchal global community, it cannot be gainsaid that women often bring an added and, perhaps, a more humane dimension to the manner and tenor in which proscribed elements of society are perceived. And this is not to be all together unexpected; for having hitherto been unjustifiably proscribed from many avenues of societal endeavors, women, generally and relatively speaking, tend to be more sensitive to questions of injustice than men. Which is why on the issue of romance, Ms. Ina van Havere cast matters in such inimitably deft and delicate terms: "The time constraints [imposed by the niggardly brief conjugal visits] mean [that] couples are forced into bed almost immediately [upon a spouse's visit to a prison inmate, thus] leaving [the couple] no time to talk [and pet]."
And, of course, who said Ghana and Ghanaians could not take a cue from this more humane and civilized treatment of our prison inmates? Even the most criminal elements among us?
*Kwame Okoampa-Ahoofe, Jr., Ph.D., teaches English and Journalism at Nassau Community College of the State University of New York, Garden City. He is the author of ten volumes of poetry and prose, including Sounds of Sirens: Essays in African Politics and Culture.

Original_Search_Title: Oil Workers Strike, Threaten Refinery Fires to Halt Selloffs
Headline: Nigeria: Oil Workers Threaten Fire, to Shut Refineries
Body_Text:
Lagos — The leadership of the National Union of Petroleum and Natural Gas Workers (NUPENG) and its Petroleum and Natural Gas Senior Staff Association of Nigeria (PENGASSAN) counterpart, have directed the nation's oil workers to shut down the four refineries and all petro-chemical plants 14 days from now, should government refuse to halt alleged desperate moves to sell off Eleme Petro-Chemical Company Limited and Port Harcourt Refinery next month.
The Nigerian National Petroleum Corporation (NNPC) has, in the meantime, concluded plans to commence the use of ethanol fuel from the beginning of next year, in an effort to slash imports of Premium Motor Spirit (PMS), while protecting the environment from the hazards of hydrocarbon emission.
Workers of the Eleme Petro-Chemical Company yesterday began an indefinite hunger strike to press home their demands.
President of PENGASSAN, Comrade Uche Okoro, at a joint press conference by NUPENG and PENGASSAN in Lagos yesterday, said the leadership of the two unions under the auspices of NUPENGASSAN, a fusion of NUPENG and PENGASSAN, would meet today with Dr Edmund Daukoru, Minister of State, Petroleum Resources and Dr Funso Kupolokun, Group Managing Director of the NNPC to intimate them of their decision.
He alleged that Eleme Petro-Chemical Company which worth over $2.4 billion was slated to be sold at a meagre $200 million, an amount he said was less than what was required for the Turn Around Maintenance (TAM) that would be due next year.
The oil workers also advised government not to contemplate another hike in price of petroleum products, warning that Nigerians could revolt because the economic reality was very provocative.
"Already, Indians who have no knowledge of petro-chemical plants have been allowed into the premises and have been asking funny questions. In fact, there have been two visitations by people who are not credible and have no knowledge of petro-chemical plants.
"We have discovered that there is desperation to sell the plants by September. We do not know where the desperation is coming from and that is why they are compromising due process. If what is happening is allowed to play itself out, what will happen will be worse than what is happening between NNPC and African Petroleum (AP). The plant is being planned to be sold at a mere $200 million.
"This is even less than what is required to do the TAM due in 2006. An independent assessor we commissioned to assess Eleme Petro-Chemical plant has told us that it is worth over $2.4 billion.
"So, the national secretariats of the two unions have since Saturday at the NEC meeting held in Abia State, directed all our members to shut down all the plants across the country after 14 days, starting from Saturday if government does not halt this desperate move to sell the refineries without due process," he said.
At an earlier press briefing by NUPENGASSAN, addressed by the leadership of both unions, Comrade Okoro said the oil workers had lost confidence in the Bureau of Public Enterprises (BPE) because it continued to undermine due process.
President of NUPENG, Comrade Peter Akpatason, said BPE had jettisoned all the agreements reached by stakeholders especially the partnering option in the privatisation of the refineries where government will still retain 49 per cent equity shares in the refineries and petro-chemical plants.
He said: "As it stands today, the battle line appears to have been drawn between the workers and BPE. All our members are very restive at the moment because of what is happening. In this regard, the Joint NEC-in-Session hereby directs the NNPC in-house unions to take appropriate steps to compel BPE to comply with the agreement."
NNPC employs ethanol to crash prices
The NNPC has also served notice that it expects to make savings in excess of $245 million (about N32.8 billion) by next year when the programme takes off effectively.
Dr. Kupolokun, Group Managing Director of the corporation, announced this at the signing of a Memorandum of Understanding (MoU) by the NNPC, COIMEX and Petrobras of Brazil in Abuja yesterday.
The NNPC boss said petroleum imports reception facility at Mosimi depot as well as the refineries would be revamped before December 31, this year to enable them take delivery and mix ethanol imports with PMS.
On the amount of savings to be made on this approach, he said the real gain was on the technical side, adding: "We won't go into it if we are not going to make money."
The corporation, he said, would be saving Nigeria scarce foreign exchange and that during the seeding programme, $105 million would be saved, while another $140 million would be saved from PMS imports next year when the programme takes off.
He said part of the implementation strategy was to carry out a feasibility study to enable the establishment of the ethanol industry in Nigeria along with seeding the Nigerian market with fuel ethanol in the first phase."This phase will be followed by development and commercialisation of ethanol fuel in Nigeria which will involve cultivation of large scale plantations of sugarcane and cassava and construction and operation of distilleries for the ethanol production of fuel ethanol.
He explained that the objective of the Nigerian ethanol programme was to firmly establish a thriving industry utilising agricultural products like sugarcane, cassava, etc, as a means of triggering significant employment generation, while improving the quality of automotive gasoline in Nigeria.
"Ethanol is emerging as an important motor fuel in view of the Kyoto Protocol Agreement. As a high-octane, water free oxygenate, fuel ethanol is one of the tools available for fighting air pollution from vehicles.
"It is also a green house gas emission friendly fuel which reduces global warming, and ensures complete elimination of the use of octane enhancing tetra ethyl lead which is a toxic pollutant."
As the end of the ceremony, Dr. Kupolokun told Vanguard that the corporation could no longer bear the heavy weight of petroleum products subsidy, and that only cost reflective pricing could spare the downstream sector imminent supply disruption and chaos.
Also speaking at the ceremony, Mr. Samir Anwad, Managing Director of Petrobras said it took Brasil 10 years to develop ethanol fuel. "Today in Brasil, cars run on ethanol fuel, gasoline, or a mixture of both. We are very proud to share with Nigeria all the good values of ethanol and I expect to be back here in a year to discuss the gains Nigeria has made in the use of ethanol fuel," he said.

Original_Search_Title: Oil Workers Oppose Planned Fuel Price Hike
Headline: Nigeria: Oil Workers Oppose Planned Fuel Price Hike
Body_Text:
Abuja/Lagos — Oil workers yesterday expressed their opposition to plans by government to increase fuel price and said in doing so, the Federal Government would be abdicating its social and economic responsibility to the citizenry.
The position of the oil workers was made known by the Joint National Executive Council (NEC) of the National Union of Petroleum and Natural Gas Workers (NUPENG) and the Petroleum and Natural Gas Senior Staff Association of Nigeria (PENGASSAN), and it coincided with a disclosure by the Federal Government that the domestic pump prices of fuel was being subsidized at a daily rate of N300 million.
In a statement released to newsmen yesterday, PENGASSAN and NUPENG condemned the use of import-parity price to determine domestic fuel prices, while the local refineries failed to perform at optimal levels.
"The Joint NEC-in-Session frowns against the import parity and persistent increase in the price of petroleum products in the country...The Joint NEC-in-Session rejects any proposed fuel price hike and that refineries must be made to continually function optimally," the unions stated.
The oilworkers' unions further demanded for the release and immediate implementation of the report of the Senator Ibrahim Mantu-led Palliative Committee.
With their position on the issue PENGASSAN and NUPENG joined the Nigeria Labour Congress (NLC), which last week equally voiced its opposition to fuel price hike.
Moves to raise fuel prices surfaced recently after the NNPC, citing high cost of crude in the international market, said it might soon be unable to finance the supply and distribution of petroleum products if the pump prices are not adjusted to allow for cost recovery. The Corporation said it has lost $900 million so far supplying fuel at N44.50 per litre.
The Petroleum Products Pricing Regulatory Agency (PPPRA), which backed the NNPC in its bid to recover cost, said that going by the current market fundamentals where the landing cost of petrol last Friday stood at N70.00 per litre, the open market price of the fuel should be N76.50 per litre.
Director-General, Budget Office of the Federation, Mr. Olabode Agusto said yesterday that the NNPC was subsidising the pump price of gasoline by over N300 million per day, which, according to him, has impaired the Corporation's ability to pay the federal government as at when due in respect of crude oil sales.
Speaking yesterday in Abuja at an interactive session with the Public Sector on the 2006-2008 Federal Government Medium-Term Expenditure Framework, Agusto said the Federal Government would have to part with an estimated N50 billion of its revenue to offset its own share of the subsidy.
He said the projected revenues for 2005 were down by another N160 billion because the actual crude oil production fell short of the projected production level.
He said that "the budget was predicated on oil production of 2.71 million barrels per day (bpd) whereas actual production for the first quarter was 2.39 mbpd. The government in the 2005 budget had pegged the price of crude oil at $30 per barrel."
He said given the NNPC estimate that the average production for the year is likely to be about 2.4 million bpd, the loss in production would stand at 12 per cent.
Noting that the projected independent revenue has also reduced by N40 billion, the director-general stated that on the whole, the total revenue loss totaled about N250 billion from N1.47 trillion to N1.22 trillion.
He gave the share of the Federal Government in the federation account at 48 per cent and the remaining 52 per cent going to states. Agusto also said N1.5trillion has been appropriated for spending as N450 billion - salaries; N100 billion -pension; N100 billion overheads; N200 billion - domestic debt service and; N100 billion - foreign currency debts service.
According to him, "N950 billion from N1.5trillion has already gone."
Agusto recalled that for the 2004 budget, revenue was N35 billion higher than expected and that oil production volume was targeted at 2.2 million bpd while actual was 2.5 million bpd. He however said this was dampened by a significant shortfall in independent revenue.
On the expenditure side, he said actual spending was N2 billion (5per cent) lower than budgeted because only 80 per cent of overhead was released. For recurrent expenditure, he pointed out that actual spending was N31 billion (6 per cent) higher than planned because "we had to clear some arrears of salaries and pensions).
He added that domestic service was N8 billion (9 percent) higher than planned because of "slightly higher level of debt." He also said foreign debt service was N19 billion (5 per cent higher than planned because "the Federal Government bore the debt service payments of some states and ended up paying 84 per cent of debt instead of 75 per cent."
For capital expenditure, he said spending was N60 billion lower than planned because "budget was approved in April and implementation of the capital budget started then."
Meanwhile, the NNPC said it expects to make savings in excess of $245 million (about N32.6 billion) by next year when it begins the use of ethanol fuel.
The Group Managing Director of the NNPC, Engineer Funsho Kupolokun, made the disclosure at the signing of a Memorandum of Understanding (MOU) between the NNPC, COIMEX and Petrobras of Brazil, in Abuja yesterday.
Kupolokun disclosed that petroleum imports reception facility at Mosimi depot as well as the refineries will be revamped before December 31, this year to enable them take delivery and mix ethanol imports with PMS.
The Corporation, he said, would be saving Nigeria scarce foreign exchange and that during the seeding programme $105 million will be saved, while another $140m will be saved from PMS imports next year when the programme takes off.
He said part of the implementation strategy is to carry out a feasibility study to enable the establishment of the ethanol industry in Nigeria along with seeding the Nigerian market with fuel ethanol in the first phase.
"This phase will be followed by development and commercialization of ethanol fuel in Nigeria which will involve cultivation of large scale plantations of sugarcane and cassava and construction and operation of distilleries for the ethanol production of fuel ethanol," he said.
He said the objective of the Nigerian ethanol programme is to firmly establish a thriving industry utilizing agricultural products like sugarcane, cassava, etc, as a means of triggering significant employment generation, while improving the quality of automotive gasoline in Nigeria.
"Ethanol is emerging as an important motor fuel in view of the Kyoto Protocol Agreement. As a high-octane, water-free oxygenate, fuel ethanol is one of the tools available for fighting air pollution from vehicles.
"It is also a green house gas emission friendly fuel which reduces global warming, and ensures complete elimination of the use of octane enhancing tetra ethyl lead which is a toxic pollutant," he said.
He noted that beside providing improved income for farmers, the use of ethanol fuel would generate additional revenues for government, create new jobs, while the residue largesse can be used for power generation within distilleries with the potential to export surplus power to the national grid.
He said that the decision to partner COIMEX and Petrobras of Brazil is informed by their expertise in the area, and that they would assist in the area of technology and know-how acquisition, marketing and logistics of the fuel ethanol programme.
Petrobras Managing Director, Mr. Samir Awad, expressed the readiness of the Brazilian company to assist in transferring the ethanol fuel technology to Nigeria and to make it a viable and sustainable project that would contribute to the goal of improving the economic condition of the country.
"We are very proud to share with NNPC and Nigeria, all the good points of ethanol, both the solutions that were found in the Brazilian case which can be transferred to the Nigerian case that would help the country in achieving the goals of improving the economic conditions in the country", he said.
Awad described the collaborative initiative as the first major business venture between Brazil and Nigeria adding that it is a noteworthy development in the area of South-South co-operation.
